meta.title: CIRO Supervisor Exam — master question bank
meta.status_definitions: draft = not yet through gates; reviewed = passed Gate 1 (blind solve) + Gate 2 (citation audit) + Gate 3 (lint); published = survived real-user exposure
meta.copyright_note: All questions original. None copied from CIRO's practice exam or CSI materials.
meta.corpus_basis: IDPC Rules consolidation 2026-01-08; Annotated UMIR 2026-01-13; GN-3900-20-001; official syllabus 2025-05-29

questions.stem: An Investment Dealer becomes insolvent. Which statement best describes the role of the Canadian Investor Protection Fund (CIPF)?

options.A: It guarantees that the dealer's creditors are paid before client property is returned.
options.B: It protects client assets held by the insolvent dealer, within prescribed limits, including through the pooling of client assets.
options.C: It assumes regulatory authority over the dealer's remaining Approved Persons.
options.D: It compensates clients for losses in the market value of their investments caused by the insolvency.

questions.rationale: CIPF protects property (cash, securities) held by a member firm on a client's behalf if the firm becomes insolvent, within coverage limits, and participates in the insolvency process including pooling of client assets. It never covers market-value losses (D), does not subordinate clients to creditors (A), and has no registration authority (C).

questions.stem: A Registered Representative receives a written complaint from a client about unauthorized trades. To avoid escalation, the RR personally offers the client $5,000, which the client accepts. The dealer is not informed. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The RR only needed to disclose the settlement to the client's accountant.
options.B: The RR has breached CIRO requirements: settlement agreements with clients require the dealer's approval, and the complaint triggers the dealer's complaint-handling and reporting obligations.
options.C: The matter is resolved because the client accepted the settlement voluntarily.
options.D: The RR acted properly as long as the payment fully compensated the client's loss.

questions.rationale: Personal, undisclosed settlements with clients are prohibited conduct. Client complaints must flow through the dealer's complaint-handling process (designated complaints officer, investigation, reporting via ComSet where applicable), and settlements require dealer approval. Both the RR and potentially their Supervisor face regulatory consequences.

questions.stem: Under the PCMLTFA framework, a client deposits large amounts of cash from an unknown source, then executes a series of offsetting low-risk trades, and finally wires the proceeds to a corporate account abroad. The three stages of money laundering illustrated, in order, are:

options.A: Placement, layering, integration
options.B: Integration, layering, placement
options.C: Layering, placement, conversion
options.D: Structuring, smurfing, integration

questions.rationale: Placement introduces illicit cash into the financial system; layering obscures its origin through transactions (e.g., offsetting trades); integration returns the funds to the launderer as apparently legitimate wealth. The Supervisor Exam tests PCMLTFA at the purpose/requirements level, including the stages of money laundering.

questions.stem: A long-tenured RR asks her Supervisor whether she may accept a $20,000 personal loan from a wealthy retail client to bridge a home purchase, noting the client offered freely and the loan will be documented at market interest. How should the Supervisor respond?

options.A: Approve it if the client signs a waiver acknowledging the conflict.
options.B: Refuse: borrowing from clients is a prohibited personal financial dealing, subject only to narrow exemptions (e.g., certain related-person or financial-institution situations) that do not apply here.
options.C: Approve it, since the loan is documented and at market rates.
options.D: Refer the question to the client's lawyer.

questions.rationale: Personal financial dealings rules prohibit borrowing from (and lending to) clients, accepting consideration from non-clients for client business, and private settlements. Exemptions are narrow (e.g., the client is a related person or a financial institution in the lending business). Documentation and consent do not cure the prohibition.

questions.stem: A 'producing' Supervisor (one who also maintains their own book of clients) is assigned to review their own trades. Under CIRO conflicts-of-interest requirements, which control is required?

options.A: The Supervisor may review their own trades if they document each review.
options.B: Independent supervision: another qualified Supervisor must review the producing Supervisor's client activity.
options.C: The trades need review only if a client complains.
options.D: None — Supervisors are exempt from trade reviews.

questions.rationale: A Supervisor cannot independently supervise themselves; producing Supervisors are subject to applicable standards of care, ethics and independent supervision — their own client activity must be reviewed by another qualified Supervisor (cross-supervision). Self-review is the textbook compensatory/managerial conflict.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a minimum requirement of an Investment Dealer's supervision system?

options.A: Communication of policies to relevant employees, with acknowledgment that they have been read and understood
options.B: A guarantee that no employee will ever violate CIRO requirements
options.C: Annual review of financial and operational policies and procedures
options.D: Written policies and procedures, amended within a reasonable time after rule changes

questions.rationale: The standard is reasonable assurance of compliance, not a guarantee. The system must include written P&Ps kept current, communication and acknowledgment, training, compliance-notice distribution, and annual review of financial/operational P&Ps.

questions.stem: Within an Investment Dealer's governance structure, which two Executives have prescribed reporting obligations to the Board of Directors?

options.A: The Chief Anti-Money Laundering Officer and the Chief Risk Officer
options.B: The Ultimate Designated Person and the Head of Trading
options.C: The Chief Compliance Officer and the Chief Financial Officer
options.D: The designated complaints officer and the Chief Information Officer

questions.rationale: The syllabus highlights the CCO's and CFO's reporting requirements to the Board as part of Executives' supervision responsibilities. Executives are accountable to Directors for establishing the supervision system.

questions.stem: A Supervisor delegates daily trade reviews to a qualified delegate. Six months later, CIRO finds deficiencies in those reviews. Which statement about responsibility is correct?

options.A: The Supervisor retains responsibility: delegation transfers the task, not the supervisory responsibility, and requires controls such as examining the delegate's reviews and regular reporting.
options.B: No one is responsible if the delegate was properly qualified.
options.C: Responsibility transferred to the delegate when the delegation was documented.
options.D: The dealer's CEO is automatically liable instead of the Supervisor.

questions.rationale: Delegation controls require: documented P&Ps for how delegation works, qualified delegates, records, escalation processes, examination of the delegate's reviews (would the delegator have approved the same items?), regular delegate-to-delegator reporting, and at least annual review of the delegation process. The delegator's responsibility is retained throughout.

questions.stem: A dealer uses an automated process to approve new account applications. Which applicant would NOT typically trigger a required manual review?

options.A: An applicant who is a politically exposed person (PEP)
options.B: An applicant below the age of majority
options.C: An applicant who is an insider of a reporting issuer
options.D: An employed adult applicant opening a basic cash account with no trading authorization

questions.rationale: Standard manual-review triggers: applicant is not an individual, is a minor, uses a trading authorization, requests derivatives strategies without adequate knowledge/experience, is an insider, or is a PEP. A routine individual cash-account applicant fits the automated path; the process must still be periodically tested and audited.

questions.stem: A branch Supervisor identifies repeated KYC update failures by an RR but has not been given authority by the dealer to restrict the RR's activity. The compliance department has noted the same issue twice without resolution. What does this situation most clearly indicate?

options.A: A problem belonging exclusively to the RR
options.B: A requirement to dismiss the RR immediately
options.C: An acceptable division of labour between compliance and supervision
options.D: A deficiency in the supervision system: Supervisors must be given authority to act on the dealer's behalf, and unresolved issues must be escalated

questions.rationale: Supervision requires both responsibility and authority. Compliance identifies/advises/monitors/escalates, but Supervisors and management act. A Supervisor without authority to remediate, and repeated unresolved findings, are system deficiencies requiring escalation to Executives.

questions.stem: Which supervisory concern is unique to the order-execution-only (OEO) business model compared with the advisory model?

options.A: Ensuring discretionary authority is renewed annually
options.B: Ensuring no recommendations are provided at all, while supervising risks arising from un-intermediated direct order entry (including DEA and CFD access)
options.C: Ensuring research reports are pre-approved
options.D: Ensuring recommendations are suitable for each client

questions.rationale: OEO dealers are exempt from suitability but must not provide recommendations; supervision focuses on the absence of advice, appropriateness at account opening, automated-approval controls, and direct order-entry risks. Suitability supervision (D) belongs to advisory; (A) is discretionary accounts.

questions.stem: A monthly review shows several retirement-age, income-objective clients of one RR holding leveraged inverse ETFs for many months. Why is this a supervisory red flag?

options.A: The RR should have used swaps instead.
options.B: ETFs cannot be held in registered accounts.
options.C: Leveraged and inverse ETFs reset daily and are generally designed for short-term use; long holding periods in conservative accounts suggest unsuitable recommendations or misunderstood products.
options.D: Inverse ETFs are prohibited products for retail clients.

questions.rationale: Complex products such as leveraged/inverse ETFs carry path-dependency from daily resets; extended holdings can diverge sharply from the underlying index. Concentrations of such products in conservative retail accounts are a classic account-activity red flag requiring follow-up.

questions.stem: An Investment Dealer operates through independent agents under principal-agent arrangements rather than employees. Which statement about supervision is correct?

options.A: Only the agent's professional liability insurer oversees conduct.
options.B: The dealer's supervisory obligations are unchanged: it must supervise agents' securities-related business as it would employees', with the arrangement documented and risks (e.g., outside activities, remote locations) addressed.
options.C: Agents are exempt from dealer supervision because they are not employees.
options.D: Agents supervise themselves under their agency agreement.

questions.rationale: Under the principal-agent model the dealer remains fully responsible for supervision of all securities-related business conducted by its agents. The model changes the working arrangement, not the regulatory obligation.

questions.stem: Which pairing of compensation structure and its characteristic supervisory risk is correct?

options.A: Commission-based → no risk; fee-based → no risk
options.B: Both structures eliminate conflicts of interest
options.C: Commission-based → excessive trading (churning); fee-based → accounts charged ongoing fees with little activity or service (reverse churning)
options.D: Commission-based → reverse churning; fee-based → excessive trading

questions.rationale: Commission compensation creates an incentive to trade more (churning); fee-based compensation creates an incentive to gather assets and trade little while fees accrue (reverse churning / suitability of the fee arrangement itself). Supervisors must monitor both patterns.

questions.stem: Under CIRO account-approval requirements, when must a designated Supervisor (or authorized delegate) approve the opening of a new retail account?

options.A: Within 30 calendar days of the first trade
options.B: Before or promptly after opening, within the prescribed business-day timeline — documented with the approval date
options.C: Approval is not required if the account was opened online
options.D: Only if the account will trade derivatives

questions.rationale: IDPC 3214(3): 'A designated Supervisor must approve each new account no later than one business day after' opening, and 3214(2) prohibits approval until all client account records have been collected; the complete account application must be received no later than the following business day (3214(1)). Approval must be documented (who and when). Online/automated openings remain subject to approval controls and manual-review triggers.

questions.stem: A grain-processing company applies to trade wheat futures to offset input-price risk. For institutional classification as a hedger, what must the dealer's determination establish?

options.A: That the company's CFO holds the Derivatives Exam
options.B: That the company has at least $10 million in net assets, regardless of purpose
options.C: That the company will never take delivery of the commodity
options.D: That the company's derivatives positions offset risks arising from its business — i.e., the transactions relate to and reduce identified business exposures

questions.rationale: A qualifying hedger classification rests on the relationship between the client's business risks and its derivatives activity: the positions must hedge exposures of the business (with the dealer evaluating intended effect and hedge effectiveness). It is purpose-based, not purely asset-based.

questions.stem: The 'account appropriateness' obligation at account opening requires the dealer to:

options.A: Put the prospective client's interest first when determining the appropriate scope and selection of products, services and account types offered to them
options.B: Match the client to the account type with the highest fee revenue
options.C: Offer every client the full product shelf
options.D: Guarantee the account will be profitable

questions.rationale: Appropriateness is the client-interest-first assessment of which account relationships, services and product scope fit the client — distinct from per-trade suitability. It is documented as part of the client account record.

questions.stem: Which item is NOT required content of the Relationship Disclosure provided at account opening?

options.A: Fees, compensation guidelines and relevant costs
options.B: Investment performance benchmarks and the impact of fees, turnover and taxes on returns
options.C: The products, services and account types available to the client, including limitations
options.D: The dealer's internal profit margin on each product

questions.rationale: Relationship Disclosure covers products/services/account types and their limitations, fees and compensation, account maintenance conditions, the wealth-management process, benchmarks, and fee/turnover/tax impacts. Internal margins are not a disclosure item.

questions.stem: Which statement about a discretionary account agreement is correct?

options.A: It converts automatically into a managed account after one year
options.B: It has no maximum term if the client consents in writing
options.C: Its maximum term is 24 months, renewable once
options.D: Its maximum term is 12 months and it is non-renewable

questions.rationale: Discretionary accounts (distinct from managed accounts) are temporary: the agreement must define and restrict the discretionary authority, runs at most 12 months, and cannot be renewed. Termination: client notice effective on receipt; dealer notice effective not less than 30 days after delivery.

questions.stem: A dealer decides to terminate a managed account relationship. To be effective, the dealer's termination notice must be:

options.A: Verbal, given at the next scheduled review
options.B: Written, effective not less than 30 days from delivery of the notice to the client
options.C: Written, effective immediately upon sending
options.D: Approved by CIRO before delivery

questions.rationale: Managed (and discretionary) account agreements terminate by written notice: client-initiated termination is effective on receipt by the dealer (except trades already entered); dealer-initiated termination is effective not less than 30 days from delivery.

questions.stem: A Portfolio Manager wants several of her managed accounts to participate in a new issue underwritten by her own dealer. What does CIRO require?

options.A: Nothing — managed accounts may always buy dealer-underwritten issues
options.B: A 50% position limit per account
options.C: Approval from the issuing company
options.D: The clients' prior written consent (with the relationship disclosed) before managed accounts invest in new issues underwritten by the dealer

questions.rationale: Managed-account conflict rules prohibit investing managed accounts in securities of related/connected issuers or in new issues/secondary offerings underwritten by the dealer without prior written client consent and disclosure. Fair allocation across accounts also applies.

questions.stem: For a retail account trading CFDs and futures (not a hedging account), client cumulative loss limits must be determined:

options.A: Only when the client requests them
options.B: By the clearing house
options.C: Once at account opening, never revisited
options.D: On a lifetime basis validated with the client annually, or on an annual basis updated annually

questions.rationale: Cumulative loss limits apply to accounts transacting in futures, forwards, CFDs, futures options or similar/highly leveraged products (other than hedging accounts), across advisory, discretionary, managed and OEO accounts — set lifetime (validated annually) or annual (updated annually). Supervisors review and approve loss limits considering previous losses.

questions.stem: An OEO dealer's automated system approves derivatives trading without manual review. Which strategy request is permitted to flow through automated approval?

options.A: Uncovered (naked) short calls
options.B: OTC forward contracts
options.C: Covered calls and protective (long) puts
options.D: Contracts for difference (CFDs)

questions.rationale: Automated derivatives approvals at OEO dealers are restricted to low-risk option strategies: long calls, long puts, covered calls, and protective puts. CFDs, OTC derivatives, futures strategies, and higher-risk option strategies require non-automated handling.

questions.stem: Instead of executing the dealer's standard derivatives trading agreement, a pension fund offers a Letter of Undertaking. This alternative is:

options.A: Acceptable for any client with over $1 million in assets
options.B: Required for all hedger accounts
options.C: Never acceptable
options.D: Acceptable for institutional clients only

questions.rationale: A Letter of Undertaking may substitute for a trading agreement for institutional clients only. Retail derivatives clients require the executed derivatives trading agreement (plus the current risk disclosure statement with positive acknowledgment).

questions.stem: During a daily review, a Supervisor notices a retail account with: trading volume far above the client's stated objectives, mounting margin debt, and commissions consuming a large share of equity. The client's KYC shows 'moderate growth, medium risk.' What is the most appropriate characterization and action?

options.A: A marketing opportunity for a fee-based conversion
options.B: Normal activity; no action needed because the client placed the orders
options.C: Possible churning/unsuitable trading red flags; the Supervisor must inquire, document the review, and take and record follow-up action
options.D: A matter to raise only at the annual branch audit

questions.rationale: Excessive trading relative to objectives, commission-to-equity erosion and margin strain are classic churning/suitability red flags listed in the syllabus. Daily/monthly supervision requires detection, inquiry, documentation and resolution — not deferral.

questions.stem: An RR returns from a CIRO suspension for discretionary trading violations. Which supervisory regime does the syllabus contemplate for Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions?

options.A: Strict supervision: pre-approval of orders by the Supervisor and daily and monthly review of all of the RR's client accounts
options.B: Supervision delegated to the RR's branch assistant
options.C: Standard supervision, since the penalty was already served
options.D: Quarterly spot checks

questions.rationale: Strict/close supervisory measures for individuals with infraction histories include order pre-approval and full daily and monthly account reviews — beyond standard sampling. Records of these reviews must be maintained.

questions.stem: A branch suspects an elderly client is being financially exploited by a relative who recently obtained trading authorization. Which tool do CIRO rules provide?

options.A: Closing the account within 24 hours
options.B: Transferring the account to the relative
options.C: A temporary hold on transactions/withdrawals where there is a reasonable belief of financial exploitation of a vulnerable client, subject to conditions including documentation and periodic review
options.D: Permanently freezing the account without notice

questions.rationale: Temporary holds protect vulnerable clients where financial exploitation (or lack of mental capacity) is reasonably suspected. They are conditional: documented basis, internal escalation, client notification, and regular review of whether the hold should continue.

questions.stem: A retail client asks the branch to hold her account mail while she travels for six months. Which control set applies?

options.A: Written client authorization, defined time limits, and control with regular review of held mail
options.B: Mail may be forwarded to the RR's home address
options.C: Mail holds are prohibited in all cases
options.D: Verbal authorization from the RR suffices

questions.rationale: Client mail holds are permitted only with client authorization, subject to time limits and supervised control/regular review — because held mail is a recognized vehicle for concealing unauthorized activity. Routing client mail to an AP's personal address is itself a red flag.

questions.stem: A retail client requests intra-day CFD positions equal to 0.8% of a reporting issuer's float. The dealer must:

options.A: Report it to the issuer
options.B: Prevent it: retail clients must not hold CFD or similar derivative positions representing more than 0.5% of the float of a publicly traded issuer on an intra-day or short-term basis
options.C: Allow it with enhanced margin
options.D: Allow it if the client signs a risk acknowledgment

questions.rationale: Derivatives P&Ps must prevent retail CFD positions exceeding 0.5% of an issuer's float intra-day/short-term, and prohibit offering retail CFDs that confer delivery rights or shareholder rights (e.g., voting). These are bright-line tested parameters.

questions.stem: Where continuous supervision of derivatives trading activity is required, CIRO requires the dealer to:

options.A: Designate one or more alternate Supervisors in addition to the designated Supervisor
options.B: Suspend derivatives trading when the Supervisor is on vacation
options.C: Let the trading desk self-supervise after hours
options.D: Outsource supervision to the clearing house

questions.rationale: Derivatives account handling requires designating alternate Supervisors so supervision is continuous despite absences. Derivatives discretionary and managed accounts also attract daily and monthly reviews.

questions.stem: An institutional desk review finds a proprietary account bought a security minutes before executing a large client order in the same security at a better price for the firm. This pattern most directly indicates:

options.A: Permissible principal trading without conditions
options.B: Best execution
options.C: A clearing error
options.D: Front running of the client order via a proprietary account — improper activity that institutional account supervision P&Ps must detect

questions.rationale: Trading ahead of a known client order in employee or proprietary accounts is front running — an improper/suspicious activity that institutional supervision must detect (along with manipulative practices, restricted-list trading, position/exercise limit breaches). It also engages UMIR Part 4 and client-priority obligations.

questions.stem: Which combination of account events is the strongest signal of possible unauthorized trading by an Approved Person?

options.A: A single profitable trade in a suitable security
options.B: Use of limit orders rather than market orders
options.C: A client depositing funds annually
options.D: Excessive transaction transfers between accounts plus frequent trade cancellations

questions.rationale: The syllabus lists 'excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations' as indicators of possible unauthorized trading — errors get reallocated or cancelled to conceal activity the client never authorized. Account-number changes and late payments are related flags.

questions.stem: A client calls the branch and reaches an Investment Representative (IR). The client asks whether to buy a specific stock. The IR says it 'looks like a strong buy' and enters the order. What should the Supervisor conclude?

options.A: Acceptable: IRs may advise on widely held large-cap securities
options.B: A violation: IRs may take and enter unsolicited orders but are not permitted to provide recommendations or advice — that is RR (or PM/APM) activity
options.C: Acceptable if the trade later proves profitable
options.D: A violation only if the client complains

questions.rationale: Permitted activities differ by category: Investment Representatives execute/take orders; Registered Representatives advise; APMs/PMs manage discretionary assets (APMs under PM supervision/pre-approval of advice). Supervisors must detect category breaches regardless of outcome or complaint.

questions.stem: Which event does NOT trigger a portfolio-level suitability reassessment for a retail advisory account?

options.A: The account is reassigned to a new Registered Representative
options.B: An ordinary dividend is credited to the account
options.C: Securities are deposited or transferred into the account
options.D: A material change to the client's KYC information

questions.rationale: Suitability must be determined on recommendations and trades, and re-assessed on non-trade triggering events: deposits/withdrawals/transfers of securities, change of the responsible Approved Person, and material KYC changes. Routine income credits are not triggers.

questions.stem: How are product due diligence (KYP) obligations divided between the Investment Dealer and the individual Approved Person?

options.A: The dealer assesses and approves products it makes available and monitors them; the Approved Person must understand the products they recommend well enough to support suitability for each client
options.B: Only the Approved Person has KYP obligations
options.C: Only the dealer has KYP obligations
options.D: KYP applies only to derivatives

questions.rationale: KYP is two-layer: firm-level product assessment/approval/monitoring, and representative-level understanding of structure, features, risks, and costs of what they recommend — feeding the client-specific suitability determination.

questions.stem: Before allowing an RR to begin soliciting options business, the Supervisor must first confirm that:

options.A: The RR has at least 10 retail clients
options.B: The branch has a Bloomberg terminal
options.C: The RR's compensation grid includes options commissions
options.D: The RR has met the applicable proficiency/qualification requirements for the derivatives activity

questions.rationale: Supervisors must ensure Approved Persons under their supervision hold the necessary qualifications for their activities (e.g., the Derivatives Exam under the post-2026 model for derivatives dealing). Solicitation of derivatives business also requires Supervisor approval under derivatives P&Ps.

questions.stem: An RR learns the firm will shortly enter a very large client buy order in a thinly traded stock. Before it is entered, the RR buys the same stock in his personal account. Under UMIR this is best described as:

options.A: Front running — trading ahead of a known client order, prohibited under UMIR
options.B: Permissible personal investing
options.C: Best execution
options.D: A wash trade

questions.rationale: Trading ahead of known, undisclosed client order flow is front running (UMIR Part 4). Wash trades (D) are offsetting trades with no beneficial-ownership change — a manipulative practice under UMIR 2.2 but not this fact pattern.

questions.stem: A client with no insider designation repeatedly buys a small-cap stock in the days before unannounced material news, across multiple events. As gatekeeper, the dealer's Supervisor should:

options.A: Ignore it because profits were modest
options.B: Warn the client to trade smaller amounts
options.C: Treat it as a potential gatekeeper matter: compare against the client's typical activity patterns, inquire, document, and make required reports (e.g., to the dealer's compliance/CIRO) where suspicion remains
options.D: Ask the issuer to delay news releases

questions.rationale: Gatekeeping obligations require using knowledge of a client's typical financial activity to identify suspicious transactions (possible insider trading), inquire, document, escalate and report through prescribed channels. Tipping the client (B) compounds the problem.

questions.stem: Which factor is NOT among those a risk-based trading supervision system must reflect?

options.A: The disciplinary history of registered representatives
options.B: The Participant's size (revenue, market share, market exposure, trade volume)
options.C: The Supervisor's personal investment performance
options.D: The number and location of offices and registrants per location

questions.rationale: Risk-based trading supervision (UMIR 7.1/Policy 7.1) scales to the Participant's size, structure, office network, product/service complexity, registrant counts, disciplinary history and risk profile/red flags. A Supervisor's own portfolio returns are irrelevant.

questions.stem: Which item does NOT require approval by a designated Supervisor before use or publication?

options.A: Routine, individualized client correspondence subject to post-use review and sampling
options.B: A telemarketing script
options.C: An original advertisement proof containing performance figures
options.D: A research report

questions.rationale: Pre-use approval is required for research reports, market letters, telemarketing scripts, promotional seminar materials, investment fund communications, original advertisements/proofs, and any material with performance reports used to solicit. Ordinary correspondence may be governed by post-use review and sampling under the dealer's P&Ps.

questions.stem: A dealer is about to publish a research report on an issuer in which the dealer beneficially owns 1.4% of a class of equity securities, and for which it provided investment banking services eight months ago. What disclosure is required?

options.A: Only the banking relationship, since ownership is under 5%
options.B: Both facts: beneficial ownership of 1% or more of the issuer's equity securities, and investment banking services provided within the preceding 12 months
options.C: None — both facts are immaterial
options.D: Only the ownership, since banking services are confidential

questions.rationale: Research disclosure thresholds tested include: 1%+ beneficial ownership (measured at the prescribed month-end), analyst/related-person holdings, investment banking services within the preceding 12 months, analyst compensation tied to banking revenue, market making, and issuer-reimbursed site visits.

questions.stem: An RR's public social media post reads: 'My model portfolio returned 22% last year — join now and lock in the same results!' Which deficiency must the Supervisor identify?

options.A: The post is fine because the 22% figure is accurate
options.B: The post promises specific future results and presents performance without required context — a misleading communication that must not be issued
options.C: Social media may never mention performance
options.D: The only issue is the absence of the dealer's logo

questions.rationale: Communications must not contain unjustified promises of specific results, unrepresentative statistics suggesting unwarranted conclusions, unlabeled opinions/forecasts, or omit material facts and risks. Accuracy of the historical number does not cure the forward 'lock in the same results' promise. Off-channel and social media use must also follow dealer P&Ps.

questions.stem: Which arrangement violates research-supervision requirements?

options.A: Pre-publication access to draft reports is restricted
options.B: The dealer publishes a favourable research report on its own parent holding company's securities
options.C: Analysts' personal trading is restricted and monitored
options.D: A designated Supervisor reviews and approves research reports under cross-supervision controls

questions.rationale: Dealers are prohibited from producing research reports or opinions on their own securities or those of their holding company. The other options are required controls: designated review/cross-supervision, analyst conduct and personal trading restrictions, and controls over early dissemination.

questions.stem: A head-office business location audit program should include all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: Advance assurance to branch managers that no deficiencies will be reported to Executives
options.B: Defined scope and planning, with training for audit staff
options.C: An audit report with documented follow-up of findings
options.D: Risk identification used to set audit frequency and depth

questions.rationale: Business location supervision guidelines cover scope, planning, the audit program and related training, risk identification, and the audit report with follow-up. Suppressing escalation of findings defeats the program; unresolved findings must be tracked to corrective action.

questions.stem: During a branch review, a Supervisor finds multiple client address changes routed to a PO box controlled by one RR, and sales literature in active use at the branch that was never submitted for the dealer's required review. These findings most directly indicate:

options.A: Retail distribution risk red flags — inadequate control over books and records (poor control of client address changes) and failure to meet supervision standards for review of advertising and sales literature, both associated with concealment of improper activity
options.B: Efficient client service
options.C: An IT capacity problem only
options.D: A marketing initiative needing a budget

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.3 names both findings as retail distribution risks: 'inadequate control over books and records, with particular attention to poor control of client address changes' and 'failure to meet regulatory standards for supervision and control, including inadequate control and review of advertising and sales literature.' Address changes routed to an RR-controlled PO box are a classic concealment mechanism for unauthorized activity; unreviewed sales literature breaches IDPC 3602 review requirements. Immediate investigation, documentation and escalation are required.

questions.stem: A new retail client of Cartwright Securities places her initial trade on Tuesday, but several supporting documents are still outstanding. Under CIRO requirements, what specific action must the Dealer Member take regarding required documents that remain outstanding after account opening?

options.A: Close the account if documents are not received within 10 business days of opening
options.B: Take specific action to obtain required documents not received within 25 business days of opening the account, unless a shorter period is prescribed
options.C: Refer the file to CIRO if documents are not received within 30 calendar days
options.D: Restrict the account to liquidating trades immediately upon the first trade settling without full documentation

questions.rationale: Section 3213(2)(iii) requires the Dealer Member to take specific action to obtain required documents not received within 25 business days of opening the account, unless a shorter period is prescribed. Option D describes the restriction that applies under 3214(5) when the designated Supervisor does not approve the account after the initial trade — a different control with a different trigger — not an automatic consequence of pending documentation.
questions.source_quote: "take specific action to obtain required documents that have not been received within 25 business days of opening the account, unless a shorter period is prescribed" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4631

questions.stem: A designated Supervisor at Beaupre Capital reviews a new account application three days after the account's initial trade and decides not to approve the account. What must the Dealer Member do?

options.A: Restrict the account to liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities to the client until the designated Supervisor provides final approval
options.B: Cancel the initial trade and return the client's funds
options.C: Reassign the account to a different Registered Representative and re-submit it for approval
options.D: Report the unapproved account to CIRO within five business days

questions.rationale: Under subsection 3214(5), if a designated Supervisor does not approve a new account after the initial trade, the account must be restricted to only liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities to the client, and the restrictions remain until final approval. Cancelling the executed trade (B) is not the prescribed remedy — the rule preserves the client's ability to exit, not an unwind obligation.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must restrict the account to only liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities to the client" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4649

questions.stem: An employee of Dorval Investments Inc., an Investment Dealer, wants to open a trading account at Halberd Securities, a different Investment Dealer. Before opening the account, Halberd Securities must:

options.A: Obtain written approval from Dorval Investments and designate the account as a non-client account
options.B: Obtain CIRO's consent and flag the account as a restricted account
options.C: Obtain verbal confirmation from the employee's Supervisor at Dorval and treat the account as a standard retail account
options.D: Require the employee to first close any accounts held at Dorval Investments

questions.rationale: Subsection 3214(6) requires that before opening a new account for an employee of another Dealer Member, the Dealer Member obtain written approval from the other Dealer Member and designate the account as a non-client account. Option C is wrong on both points: the approval must be written and from the employing dealer, and the account is designated non-client, not standard retail.
questions.source_quote: "Before opening a new account for an employee of another Dealer Member, the Dealer Member must obtain written approval from the other Dealer Member, and must designate the account as non-client account." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4652

questions.stem: To address the conflict of interest inherent in a Registered Representative controlling their own clients' records, what specific control must a Dealer Member's account opening policies and procedures include for significant changes to client information?

options.A: A requirement that all changes be communicated to the client's trusted contact person
options.B: A requirement that the client re-sign the full account application annually
options.C: A semi-annual audit of all client files by the Chief Compliance Officer
options.D: Procedures independent of the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager for verifying significant changes to client information

questions.rationale: Clause 3213(2)(iv) requires policies and procedures independent of the RR, PM or APM for verifying significant changes to client information — the independence is the conflict control, since the person who could benefit from a falsified change must not be the one verifying it. Option A misapplies the trusted contact person mechanism, which addresses financial exploitation concerns under 3202(4), not record-change verification.
questions.source_quote: "have policies and procedures independent of the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager for verifying significant changes to client information" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4633

questions.stem: Which of the following persons qualifies as an institutional client under the IDPC Rules without needing to request and consent to the classification?

options.A: An individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million
options.B: A hedger opening accounts for qualifying hedging activities
options.C: A non-individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million
options.D: A retired portfolio manager with 25 years of industry experience

questions.rationale: The institutional client definition includes, without any request-and-consent condition, a non-individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million. By contrast, both an individual exceeding $10 million (A) and a hedger (B) qualify only if they request and consent to being classified as an institutional client — the consent requirement is what distinguishes those branches of the definition.
questions.source_quote: "(v) a non-individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million, (vi) an individual ... exceeding $10 million who requests and consents" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 481-487

questions.stem: Pellerin Securities approves a municipal pension board as an institutional client whose account remains subject to suitability determination under section 3403. For a particular transaction, the dealer concludes the board is NOT capable of independently evaluating the investment risk. What must Pellerin Securities then do?

options.A: Decline the order and close the account
options.B: Obtain a written waiver of suitability protections from the board
options.C: Take steps to ensure the institutional client fully understands the investment product, including the potential risks
options.D: Convert the account to a retail account and apply retail suitability requirements

questions.rationale: Under clause 3403(3)(ii), where the Dealer Member does not have reasonable grounds to conclude the institutional client can make an independent investment decision and independently evaluate risk, it must take steps to ensure the client fully understands the investment product, including the potential risks. Option B confuses this with the 3404(3)(ii) waiver, which is available only to certain non-individual permitted clients who waive in writing — it is not the prescribed response to a sophistication shortfall on a given transaction.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must take steps to ensure that the institutional client fully understands the investment product, including the potential risks" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5634-5635

questions.stem: Which account is exempt from the requirement in clause 3211(1)(ii) to determine that the scope of products, services and account relationships accessible within the account is appropriate for the person?

options.A: A discretionary account for a retail client
options.B: A direct electronic access account
options.C: A margin account for a retail client
options.D: A managed account for a retail client

questions.rationale: Subsection 3211(2) exempts order execution only accounts and direct electronic access accounts from clause 3211(1)(ii) — the scope-of-products component of account appropriateness — because the dealer is not selecting products for those clients. Discretionary, managed and margin accounts (A, C, D) all remain fully subject to both limbs of the appropriateness determination.
questions.source_quote: "Clause 3211(1)(ii) does not apply in respect to: (i) an order execution only account, or (ii) a direct electronic access account." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4582-4584

questions.stem: When must a Dealer Member make the account appropriateness determination required by section 3211?

options.A: Within one business day after the initial trade in the account
options.B: At the time of the first significant change to the client's KYC information
options.C: Before opening an account for the person
options.D: Within 25 business days of account opening, together with outstanding documentation

questions.rationale: Section 3211(1) states that before a Dealer Member opens an account for a person, it must determine, on a reasonable basis and putting the person's interest first, that the action is appropriate and the scope of products, services and account relationships is appropriate. Option A confuses appropriateness timing with the designated Supervisor's account approval deadline under 3214(3), which runs one business day after the initial trade.
questions.source_quote: "Before a Dealer Member opens an account for a person, the Dealer Member must determine, on a reasonable basis and putting the person's interest first" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4573

questions.stem: Under the definition of "client account records" in section 3210, which of the following is specifically listed as a client account record?

options.A: The Dealer Member's product due diligence committee minutes
options.B: Documentation supporting the conclusion that the client's identity has been verified
options.C: The Registered Representative's commission grid applicable to the account
options.D: The Supervisor's monthly trade review worksheets for the account

questions.rationale: Section 3210 defines client account records to include (i) documentation supporting the conclusion that the client's identity has been verified, (ii) documentation supporting the account appropriateness assessment, (iii) KYC information collected in accordance with Corporation requirements, and (iv) the client's account application. Supervisory review worksheets (D) are supervision records under Rule 3900, not part of the client account records the dealer must provide to or obtain from the client.
questions.source_quote: "Documentation supporting the conclusion that the client's identity has been verified" — syllabus 4.4 parameter (syllabus-raw.txt ~line 802); records obligation anchored in IDPC 3210/3212

questions.stem: Riverbend Wealth, an Investment Dealer, receives client accounts transferred from its affiliated Mutual Fund Dealer Member. Under what condition relating to fees may Riverbend rely on the affiliate's existing documentation to satisfy its client account record requirements?

options.A: The account service fees and product charges at Riverbend are the same or lower than those at the affiliate firm
options.B: Riverbend discloses any fee differences to the client within 60 days
options.C: The affiliate agrees in writing to indemnify Riverbend for documentation deficiencies
options.D: Total fees at Riverbend do not exceed those at the affiliate by more than 10%

questions.rationale: Subsection 3212(4) permits reliance on an affiliate's documentation only if, among other conditions, the account service fees and charges and the charges for making, disposing and holding investment products are the same or lower as those at the affiliate firm. Option B reflects the general 60-day fee-change notice concept under the fee schedule rules, which is not the condition for affiliate-documentation reliance; the rule requires same-or-lower fees, not disclosure of higher ones.
questions.source_quote: "the following fees and charges associated with the account offering and investment products and services are the same or lower as those at the affiliate firm" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4604

questions.stem: Which statement about who must receive relationship disclosure information is correct?

options.A: It must be provided to all clients, retail and institutional, at account opening
options.B: It must be provided only to clients of advisory accounts, since OEO clients receive no advice
options.C: It must be provided only to clients who request it in writing
options.D: It must be provided to retail clients; Dealer Members are not required to provide it to institutional clients

questions.rationale: Section 3216(1) establishes the minimum requirements for providing relationship disclosure information to retail clients and states expressly that Dealer Members are not required to provide relationship disclosure to institutional clients. Option B is wrong because OEO retail accounts still require relationship disclosure — only the suitability-process content under sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(d) may be omitted if disclosure is made in compliance with section 3241.
questions.source_quote: "This section establishes the minimum requirements for the provision of relationship disclosure information to retail clients. Dealer Members are not required to provide relationship disclosure to institutional clients." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4686-4688

questions.stem: Marlowe Securities uses a standardized Relationship Disclosure document for separate classes of clients rather than customizing one for each client. What does section 3216 require of the review and approval of this disclosure?

options.A: No approval is needed because the document is standardized
options.B: CIRO must approve the standardized document before first use
options.C: It must be approved by a partner, Director, officer or designated Supervisor, and the designated Supervisor must ensure the correct document is used in each client circumstance
options.D: Each client's external legal counsel must confirm the disclosure is adequate

questions.rationale: Subsection 3216(6) requires the relationship disclosure information to be approved by a partner, Director, officer or designated Supervisor regardless of its form; for standardized documents the designated Supervisor must ensure the correct document is used in each client circumstance, and customized documents must each be approved. Option A is the common misconception that standardization removes the supervisory obligation — it only changes its focus from per-document approval to correct-document selection.
questions.source_quote: "must be approved by a partner, Director, officer or designated Supervisor... If the document is a standardized document, the designated Supervisor must ensure that the correct document is used in each client circumstance." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4834-4838

questions.stem: Beyond the time of account opening, when must a Dealer Member provide relationship disclosure information to a retail client again?

options.A: Every 36 months, at the same time KYC information is reviewed
options.B: Whenever the client opens any new position in a derivative
options.C: Each time the client's account is reassigned to a new Registered Representative
options.D: When there is a significant change to the relationship disclosure information previously provided to the client

questions.rationale: Subsection 3216(2) requires relationship disclosure to be provided at the time of opening an account or accounts and when there is a significant change to the relationship disclosure information previously provided. Option A confuses the trigger with the 36-month KYC review cycle in 3209(4) — that cycle governs review of KYC information, not re-delivery of the Relationship Disclosure.
questions.source_quote: "(i) at the time of opening an account or accounts, and (ii) when there is a significant change to the relationship disclosure information previously provided to the client." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4695-4697

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Glenview Capital learns that a retail client intends to use a home equity line of credit to buy securities in his cash account. The client received and acknowledged a leverage risk disclosure statement four months ago when leverage was first recommended. What must the dealer do now?

options.A: Provide a new leverage risk disclosure statement and obtain a fresh acknowledgement before accepting the order
options.B: Nothing further is required, because a leverage risk disclosure statement was provided in accordance with the rule within the last six months
options.C: Convert the cash account to a margin account before the purchase
options.D: Obtain the Supervisor's written pre-approval of each leveraged purchase

questions.rationale: Subsection 3217(2) exempts the dealer from re-delivering the leverage risk disclosure statement where one was provided in accordance with subsection 3217(1) within the last six months. Option A would be correct if the prior delivery were more than six months old; the six-month window is the tested threshold. Borrowing outside the dealer to invest does not itself require a margin account (C) — the leverage disclosure regime applies precisely because the client borrows outside the dealer.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member is not required to comply with subsection 3217(1) where it has provided the retail client with a leverage risk disclosure statement in accordance with subsection 3217(1) within the last six months." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4854-4856

questions.stem: What must a Dealer Member do before opening a margin account for a client?

options.A: Obtain the designated Supervisor's approval of the client's first margin trade
options.B: File the proposed margin terms with CIRO
options.C: Obtain a letter of undertaking covering the client's indebtedness
options.D: Deliver a margin account agreement to the client and obtain a copy of the agreement signed by the client

questions.rationale: Subsection 3247(1) requires that prior to opening a margin account, the dealer deliver a margin account agreement to the client and obtain a copy signed by the client. Option C misapplies the letter of undertaking, which under 3253 is an institutional-client alternative to a derivatives trading agreement — it has no role in margin account documentation.
questions.source_quote: "Prior to opening a margin account, a Dealer Member must: (i) deliver a margin account agreement to the client, and (ii) obtain a copy of the margin account agreement signed by the client." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5125-5127

questions.stem: A retail client of Stanfield Brokers complains that the firm sold securities in her margin account to cover a deficiency without telephoning her first. Under the margin account agreement content requirements, which statement is correct?

options.A: The agreement must describe the dealer's right to sell assets in the client's account, and if the client requires prior notice, set out the nature of the notice and the client's obligations to remedy any deficiency
options.B: The dealer must always provide at least two business days' written notice before selling client assets
options.C: Sales to cover deficiencies require CIRO's prior consent
options.D: The dealer may sell assets only if the agreement is renewed annually

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(v) requires the agreement to describe the dealer's right to sell assets in the client's account and make purchases to cover short sales; if the client requires prior notice, the dealer must set out the nature of the notice and the client's obligations to remedy any deficiency. Option B states a fixed notice period the rule does not impose — notice is a matter of agreement terms, not a prescribed regulatory minimum.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member's right to sell assets in the client's account and make purchases to cover short sales. If the client requires prior notice, the Dealer Member must set out the nature of the notice" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5136-5138

questions.stem: Which of the following is required content of a margin account agreement under section 3247?

options.A: The client's right to convert the margin account into a managed account on request
options.B: The extent to which the Dealer Member has the right, if any, to use a security in the client's account for delivery against a short sale in an account owned or controlled by the Dealer Member, a partner or Director
options.C: A schedule of the maximum interest rates the Dealer Member may charge on debit balances
options.D: The Dealer Member's obligation to convert all short positions to long positions before month end

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(vii) requires disclosure of the extent to which the dealer has the right, if any, to use a security in the client's account for delivery against a short sale in an account owned or controlled by the Dealer Member, a partner or Director. Option C is close but wrong: clause 3247(2)(ii) requires describing the client's obligation to pay interest on debit balances, not a schedule of maximum rates.
questions.source_quote: "the right, if any, to use a security in the client's account for delivery against a short sale in an account owned or controlled by the Dealer Member, a partner or Director" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5141-5143

questions.stem: A derivatives trading agreement gives Quaile Derivatives Inc. the right to take the other side of a client's transaction. Under section 3252, what condition applies to this dealer activity?

options.A: It is prohibited in all retail derivatives accounts
options.B: It is permitted only if the dealer reports each principal transaction to the clearing house within 24 hours
options.C: The dealer must obtain client consent before taking the other side of the client's transaction, and document whether the client provides such consent
options.D: It is permitted automatically because executing the agreement constitutes blanket consent

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(v)(b) requires the agreement to set out the dealer's obligation to obtain client consent before the dealer may take the other side to the client's transaction and to document whether the client provides such consent. Option D is the key misconception — signing the agreement does not itself confer consent; the obligation is consent before taking the other side plus documentation of whether consent was given.
questions.source_quote: "obtain client consent before the Dealer Member may take the other side to the client's transaction, and document whether the client provides such consent" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5211-5212

questions.stem: A client wishes to grant discretionary authority to her dealer within the derivatives trading agreement itself. For this to be acceptable under clause 3252(1)(vi), the agreement must include:

options.A: Disclosures explaining the discretionary authority given and the client's acknowledgement of consent, with the authority consistent with Part G of Rule 3200
options.B: A clause stating the discretionary authority renews automatically each year
options.C: A power of attorney notarized in the client's province of residence
options.D: The Supervisor's undertaking to personally execute all discretionary trades

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(vi) requires, where discretionary authority is given to the dealer, disclosures explaining the authority and the client's acknowledgement of consent, provided the authority is consistent with Part G of Rule 3200 (unless given through a separate compliant agreement). Option B contradicts Part G: a discretionary account agreement must not be renewable and has a maximum 12-month term, so automatic renewal could never satisfy the consistency condition.
questions.source_quote: "(a) disclosures explaining the discretionary authority that has been given, (b) the client's acknowledgement that is has consented to the giving of the authority, provided the authority given is consistent with the requirements contained within Part G" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5214-5218

questions.stem: Which of the following is a Dealer Member RIGHT that must be set out in the derivatives trading agreement?

options.A: The right to amend the client's cumulative loss limit without notifying the client
options.B: The right to waive the client's exercise notice deadlines for in-the-money options
options.C: The right to refuse to address errors and omissions affecting client positions
options.D: The right to exercise discretion in accepting orders and to impose trading or position limits or closeout positions under specified conditions

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(ii) requires the agreement to set out the dealer's right to exercise discretion in accepting orders and to impose trading or position limits or closeout positions under specified conditions. Option C inverts an obligation: clause 3252(1)(v)(c) requires the agreement to state the dealer's obligation to address situations when errors and omissions occur — that is a duty, not a waivable right.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member's right to: (a) exercise discretion in accepting orders, (b) impose trading or position limits or closeout positions under specified conditions" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5196-5198

questions.stem: Under the client-related content of a derivatives trading agreement, the client must acknowledge an ongoing obligation to inform and update the Dealer Member about which circumstance?

options.A: Any change in the client's preferred order entry channel
options.B: Any circumstances under which they would be considered an insider of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded
options.C: Any intention to open derivatives accounts at other Dealer Members
options.D: Any change in the client's tax residency

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(ix)(b) requires the client's acknowledgement of their obligation to inform and update the dealer of any circumstances under which they would be considered an insider of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded — insider status drives supervision for derivatives used to avoid insider trading restrictions. The other acknowledgements listed (A, C, D) are not prescribed by section 3252; the only other prescribed acknowledgement is receipt of the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement.
questions.source_quote: "their obligation to inform and update the Dealer Member of any circumstances under which they would be considered to be an insider of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5231-5233

questions.stem: For options and similar derivatives, a derivatives trading agreement must disclose that the Dealer Member may apply cash-only terms during which period?

options.A: The last 30 days before the option's expiry
options.B: Any period when the underlying security is halted
options.C: The last 10 days before expiry
options.D: The five business days following assignment

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3252(1)(xi)(c)(II) requires disclosure that the Dealer Member may apply cash-only terms during the last 10 days before expiry. The 30-day figure (A) is a plausible confusion with the dealer's managed/discretionary account termination notice period — the option expiry cash-only window is 10 days.
questions.source_quote: "The Investment Dealer may apply cash-only terms during the last 10 days before expiry" — syllabus 4.10 parameter (syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 916-918)

questions.stem: Anders Grain Co-op maintains a hedging account at Fairmont Futures that transacts exclusively in futures contracts to offset its crop price exposure. Must a cumulative loss limit be set for this account under clause 3252(1)(vii)?

options.A: Yes — loss limits apply to all accounts that transact in futures contracts without exception
options.B: Yes, but only if the account is also an OEO account
options.C: No, because cumulative loss limits apply only to discretionary and managed accounts
options.D: No — the cumulative loss limit applies to an account other than a hedging account

questions.rationale: Subsection 3252(2)(ii) states the cumulative loss limit applies to an account, other than a hedging account, whether the account is advisory, discretionary, managed or OEO. A bona fide hedging account is the express carve-out — losses on the derivative leg are expected to be offset by gains on the underlying exposure. Option C inverts the rule: the limit applies across all four account types, not only discretionary and managed ones.
questions.source_quote: "applies to an account, other than a hedging account, whether the account is an advisory account, a discretionary account, a managed account or an order execution only account" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5261-5263

questions.stem: Lakehurst Pension Trust, an institutional client whose derivatives account carries a debit balance subject to interest, provides a letter of undertaking instead of a derivatives trading agreement. Beyond agreeing to comply with Corporation requirements, applicable laws and the requirements of trading, clearing or issuing entities, what must the letter of undertaking state?

options.A: The names of all individuals at the trust authorized to place orders
options.B: The conditions under which transfers of the client's funds, securities or other property may be made between accounts, unless these conditions are acknowledged by the client in another document
options.C: The trust's lifetime cumulative loss limit and its annual validation date
options.D: The dealer's fee schedule for derivative transactions

questions.rationale: Clause 3253(2)(ii) requires that, if the client has an account charged interest on a debit balance, the letter of undertaking state the conditions under which transfers of the client's funds, securities or other property may be made between accounts, unless acknowledged in another document. Option C imports the retail-oriented cumulative loss limit content of the trading agreement; the letter of undertaking's prescribed content under 3253(2) is limited to the compliance undertaking and the inter-account transfer conditions.
questions.source_quote: "if the client has an account that is charged interest on a debit balance, the conditions under which transfers of the client's funds, securities or other property may be made between accounts" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5281-5283

questions.stem: Which document must a Dealer Member provide to a client as part of accepting managed accounts under section 3277?

options.A: A copy of its policy ensuring fair allocation of investment opportunities
options.B: A copy of the designated Supervisor's written approval of the account
options.C: The managed account committee's most recent annual review report
options.D: The Portfolio Manager's personal trading records for the prior year

questions.rationale: Clause 3277(1)(vi) requires the dealer to provide the client with a copy of its policy ensuring fair allocation of investment opportunities. Option B confuses two record obligations: the designated Supervisor must approve each managed account in writing and the dealer must retain a record of that approval (3277(1)(iv)-(v)), but the approval record is retained by the dealer, not delivered to the client.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must provide the client with a copy of its policy ensuring fair allocation of investment opportunities" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5418-5419

questions.stem: On June 1, a retail client mails written notice terminating her managed account agreement with Copeland Asset Management; the firm receives it June 4. The Portfolio Manager had entered two transactions for the account on June 3. Which statement correctly describes the termination?

options.A: Termination is effective June 1, and the June 3 transactions must be cancelled
options.B: Termination is effective not less than 30 days after June 4, because all managed account terminations require 30 days' notice
options.C: Termination is effective June 4 on receipt by the dealer, except for transactions entered prior to receipt of the notice — so the June 3 transactions stand
options.D: Termination is ineffective because only the dealer may terminate a managed account agreement

questions.rationale: Subsection 3278(2)(i) makes client-initiated termination by written notice effective on receipt by the Dealer Member, except for transactions entered prior to receipt of the notice — so trades entered June 3, before the June 4 receipt, are unaffected. Option B wrongly applies the 30-day minimum, which under 3278(2)(ii) governs only dealer-initiated termination.
questions.source_quote: "by the client, effective on receipt by the Dealer Member, except for transactions entered prior to receipt of the notice" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5429-5430

questions.stem: A Portfolio Manager at Westcastle Securities proposes to sell a block of corporate bonds from one of her managed client accounts directly to her own personal account at an independently verified market price. As the designated Supervisor, you should:

options.A: Approve the trade, since the price is at fair market value
options.B: Approve the trade if the client provides prior written consent
options.C: Block the trade: a responsible person must not knowingly cause a managed account to purchase or sell a security from or to the account of a Portfolio Manager, and the rule provides no client-consent exception
options.D: Approve the trade if the managed account committee reviews it at its next annual meeting

questions.rationale: Subsection 3280(3)(i) prohibits knowingly causing a managed account to purchase or sell a security or derivative from or to the account of a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager or an associate of either — and unlike the 3280(2) investment restrictions, subsection 3280(3) contains no prior-written-consent relief. Option B is the closest trap: written consent cures related/connected-issuer and dealer-underwritten-issue investments under 3280(2), but not principal trades with the PM's own account under 3280(3).
questions.source_quote: "must not knowingly cause any managed account to: (i) purchase or sell a security or derivative of a security of an issuer from or to the account of a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager or an associate" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5463-5466

questions.stem: An equities trader at Pinegrove Capital learns from an internal system that the firm's managed account program will begin accumulating a mid-cap stock tomorrow. He buys the stock in his personal account today. Which managed-account conflict rule has been engaged?

options.A: The prohibition on a responsible person or Dealer Member trading for their own account, or knowingly permitting an associate or affiliate to trade, in reliance upon information relating to trades made or to be made in a managed account
options.B: The prohibition on charging performance-contingent fees without a written agreement
options.C: The requirement to fairly allocate investment opportunities among managed accounts
options.D: The prohibition on a managed account holding publicly traded securities of the Dealer Member

questions.rationale: Subsection 3280(1) prohibits a responsible person or the Dealer Member from trading their own account — or knowingly permitting or arranging any associate or affiliate to trade — in reliance upon information relating to trades made or to be made in a managed account; trading ahead of known managed-account flow is exactly this conduct. Option C is the closest adjacent rule (3280(4)) but fair allocation governs distributing opportunities among the managed accounts themselves, not personal trading on managed-account information.
questions.source_quote: "must not trade for their or the Dealer Member's own account, or knowingly permit or arrange any associate or affiliate to trade, in reliance upon information relating to trades made or to be made in a managed account" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5448-5450

questions.stem: Hollis Private Wealth wants to charge a managed account client a fee contingent upon the performance of the client's account. Under section 3281, this is:

options.A: Prohibited in all circumstances for managed accounts
options.B: Permitted only for institutional clients
options.C: Permitted automatically once disclosed in the Relationship Disclosure
options.D: Permitted only if the client has provided the Dealer Member with a written agreement setting out the manner in which such fees may be charged

questions.rationale: Subsection 3281(1) prohibits charging a client directly for managed account services based on transaction volume/value or contingent on profit or performance, unless the client has provided a written agreement setting out the manner in which the fees may be charged. Option A overstates the rule — it is a conditional prohibition cured by the prescribed written agreement, not an absolute ban.
questions.source_quote: "contingent upon profit or performance of the client's account, unless the client has provided the Dealer Member with a written agreement which sets out the manner in which the fees may be charged" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5475-5477

questions.stem: A Dealer Member designs a bonus plan paying its Portfolio Managers an amount that increases with the number and dollar value of trades executed in the managed accounts they handle. Under subsection 3281(2), this compensation plan is:

options.A: Acceptable if disclosed to managed account clients in writing
options.B: Acceptable provided each underlying trade is suitable
options.C: Acceptable for Associate Portfolio Managers but not for Portfolio Managers
options.D: Prohibited — a Dealer Member must not compensate a person authorized to deal with managed accounts on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account

questions.rationale: Subsection 3281(2) flatly prohibits compensating a person referred to in section 3279 (PMs, APMs, sub-advisers) on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account — transaction-based pay would incentivize churning discretion the client cannot police. Option A wrongly imports the written-agreement cure from 3281(1); that relief applies to direct client charges, while the internal compensation prohibition in 3281(2) has no disclosure or consent exception.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must not compensate a person referred to in section 3279, on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5479-5480

questions.stem: The designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts at Brockton Securities is overloaded and asks a qualified compliance officer to perform the required monthly financial performance reviews of each discretionary account on his behalf. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — supervisory tasks may always be delegated to a qualified delegate provided the delegation is documented
options.B: No — the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must not delegate the performance of these reviews to any other person
options.C: Yes, provided the compliance officer reports the results to the Supervisor weekly
options.D: No, unless the compliance officer is also approved as a Portfolio Manager

questions.rationale: Subsection 3970(3) provides that the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must not delegate the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2) to any other person. Option A states the general delegation framework in section 3907 (tasks may be delegated, responsibility may not), but 3970(3) is a specific override: for these discretionary account reviews, even the task cannot be delegated.
questions.source_quote: "The designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must not delegate the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2) to any other person." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 8384-8385

questions.stem: Which sequencing requirement applies when a Dealer Member accepts a discretionary account?

options.A: The discretionary account agreement may be signed any time within 25 business days of the first discretionary trade
options.B: The Dealer Member must enter into a discretionary account agreement with the client prior to accepting the account as a discretionary account
options.C: The agreement is required only if the account will trade derivatives
options.D: The agreement may be replaced by a verbal authorization recorded on a taped line

questions.rationale: Clause 3273(1)(iv) requires the dealer to enter into a discretionary account agreement with the client prior to accepting the account as a discretionary account, and under 3273(1)(v) the designated Supervisor must approve both the account and the signed agreement. Option A wrongly borrows the 25-business-day documentation follow-up window from 3213(2)(iii), which addresses pending supporting documents generally — the discretionary agreement itself must exist before acceptance.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must enter into a discretionary account agreement with the client prior to accepting the account as a discretionary account" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5359-5360

questions.stem: To accept discretionary accounts, the Supervisors a Dealer Member designates as responsible for those accounts must:

options.A: Be Executives of the Dealer Member
options.B: Hold Portfolio Manager approval
options.C: Meet the proficiency requirements set out in Rule 2600
options.D: Have personally managed discretionary money for at least five years

questions.rationale: Clause 3273(1)(i) requires the dealer to designate one or more designated Supervisors who meet the proficiency requirements set out in Rule 2600 to be responsible for discretionary accounts. Option B confuses the Supervisor's qualification standard with the trading authorization standard — section 3275 governs which Registered Representatives may effect discretionary trades (at least two years of active experience), and PM approval is a separate category.
