questions.source_quote: “ocers commission and fee-based accounts cannot select accounts for review solely based on commission levels, they should also have a procedure for selecting fee- based accounts.” — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 186

questions.stem: Redwillow Securities is drafting policies and procedures to supervise its growing book of retail fee-based accounts, where no commission is charged for trades. What does IDPC section 3945(3) require these policies and procedures to do?

options.A: Apply the same commission-level screens used for transactional accounts
options.B: Review fee-based accounts only when the client complains
options.C: Cap the annual fee at 1% of assets under administration
options.D: Address account activity review requirements and use criteria other than commission levels

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(3) requires the dealer to develop policies and procedures specifically addressing supervision of retail accounts where no commission is charged for trades, such as fee based accounts, and those policies must address account activity review requirements and use criteria other than commission levels — because in a fee-based account, commission screens detect nothing. Option A is precisely what the rule forbids relying on; B abandons proactive review; C invents a fee cap that the rules do not impose.
questions.source_quote: “The Dealer Member must develop policies and procedures that specifically address supervising retail client accounts where a commission is not charged for trades placed by or for a client, such as fee based accounts. These policies and procedures must:” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8175

questions.stem: Birchgrove Direct, an approved OEO service provider, is designing its employee compensation plan. The proposal includes paying customer-service employees a share of trade commissions on transactions executed in OEO accounts. Why must the dealer reject this design?

options.A: OEO employees may only be paid in company shares
options.B: A dealer must not compensate employees by giving them trade commissions for transactions executed in order execution only accounts
options.C: Commission compensation is permitted only if the employee is also a Supervisor
options.D: Trade-commission compensation is permitted provided it does not exceed 5% of salary

questions.rationale: Subsection 2207(5) prohibits a dealer from compensating employees by giving them trade commissions for transactions executed in OEO accounts — transaction-linked pay would create an incentive to stimulate trading in a channel where no recommendations may be given. Options C and D invent role-based and percentage carve-outs the prohibition does not contain; A invents a share-only pay rule that does not exist.
questions.source_quote: “A Dealer Member must not compensate employees by giving them trade commissions for transactions executed in order execution only accounts.” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1363

questions.stem: A managed account client of Eastpoint Capital negotiates a performance-based fee: the dealer will charge 15% of gains above a benchmark. Under IDPC section 3281, what condition must be met before the dealer may charge the client directly on this basis?

options.A: The fee must first be approved by a vote of all the dealer's managed account clients
options.B: No condition applies — performance fees are always prohibited for managed accounts
options.C: The dealer must rebate any performance fee that exceeds the prior year's commission equivalent
options.D: The client must have provided the dealer with a written agreement setting out the manner in which fees contingent upon profit or performance may be charged

questions.rationale: Subsection 3281(1) prohibits charging a managed account client directly on a basis tied to volume or value of transactions or contingent upon profit or performance unless the client has provided a written agreement setting out the manner in which such fees may be charged — so a properly documented written agreement makes the negotiated performance fee permissible. Option B overstates the rule into an outright ban; A and C invent client-vote and rebate mechanisms that have no basis in section 3281.
questions.source_quote: “(1) A Dealer Member may not charge a client directly for services rendered to the managed account, that is: (i) based upon the volume or value of transactions in the account initiated for the account, or (ii) contingent upon profit or performance of the client’s account,” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5472

questions.stem: The compliance team at Govan Hill Securities is allocating syllabus outcome 3.10 between two training modules. Which two compensation arrangements does the Supervisor Exam syllabus identify in outcome 3.10 as carrying supervisory risks a Supervisor must understand?

options.A: Negotiated flat fees and referral fees
options.B: Stock option grants and deferred bonuses
options.C: Trailer commissions and soft-dollar arrangements
options.D: Performance bonuses and signing bonuses

questions.rationale: Outcome 3.10 reads: understand the supervisory risks associated with negotiated flat fees and referral fees. Option C names real industry compensation flows, but they are not the pair the 3.10 parameter enumerates; B and D describe employment compensation rather than client-facing fee arrangements within the outcome.
questions.source_quote: “3.10 Understand the supervisory risks associated with negotiated flat fees and referral fees.” — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 770

questions.stem: Within outcome 3.9, the syllabus directs Supervisors to analyze compensation structures, and outcome 3.10 extends the compensation theme. Which statement correctly distinguishes the two outcomes?

options.A: Outcome 3.9 covers negotiated flat fees, while 3.10 covers commission-based and fee-based structures
options.B: Outcome 3.9 addresses analyzing commission-based and fee-based compensation structures, while 3.10 addresses understanding the supervisory risks of negotiated flat fees and referral fees
options.C: Both outcomes deal exclusively with Supervisor salary arrangements
options.D: Outcome 3.10 requires applying client loss limits to compensation

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 3.9 is to analyze the supervisory risks associated with compensation structures, considering commission-based and fee-based; 3.10 is to understand the supervisory risks associated with negotiated flat fees and referral fees. Option A swaps the two outcomes' content — the closest distractor; C misreads both outcomes as internal salary topics; D imports loss limits, a derivatives account concept unrelated to either outcome.
questions.source_quote: “3.9 Analyze the supervisory risks associated with each of the following compensation structures. Consider:” — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 767

questions.scenario: Lansdowne Park Securities Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer with two lines of business. Its retail advisory division operates through 38 independent advisors engaged as agents under principal-agent arrangements; the advisors keep their own storefront offices and pay their own expenses. Its private client division offers in-house managed accounts: investment decisions are made centrally by an investment committee and applied across roughly 600 managed accounts, with two Portfolio Managers and one Associate Portfolio Manager, Priya Raman, handling client portfolios. Some managed account clients have negotiated asset-based fees; one large client pays a fee contingent on performance under a signed written fee agreement. Recently, agent Marc Belisle began selling unrelated insurance products from his office, and the firm's new Chief Compliance Officer, Dana Whitfield, is reviewing how Lansdowne's supervisory structure should respond to each feature of this business model.
questions.stem: Whitfield first considers the disclosure clients of the agent advisors must receive. Regarding Belisle's insurance sales, what must Lansdowne or the agent disclose to clients at the time of opening an account?

options.A: That the dealer guarantees all of the agent's activities, including insurance sales
options.B: The list of securities and derivatives related business conducted by the agent for which the dealer is responsible, and that the dealer is not responsible for any other business activity conducted by the agent
options.C: That clients may not purchase insurance from any person associated with the dealer
options.D: Nothing — outside business activity disclosure is made only to CIRO

questions.rationale: The dealer-Corporation agreement under section 2303 requires written disclosure to clients at account opening of the list of securities and derivatives related business conducted by the agent for which the dealer is responsible, and that the dealer is not responsible for any other business activity conducted by the agent — Belisle's insurance sales fall in the second category. Option A overstates dealer responsibility to cover non-dealer business; C wrongly converts a disclosure regime into a sales ban; D ignores that the disclosure is client-facing, not merely a regulatory filing.
questions.source_quote: “(i) the list of securities and derivatives related business conducted by the agent for which the Dealer Member is responsible, and (ii) that the Dealer Member is not responsible for any other business activity conducted by the agent.” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1739

questions.scenario: Lansdowne Park Securities Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer with two lines of business. Its retail advisory division operates through 38 independent advisors engaged as agents under principal-agent arrangements; the advisors keep their own storefront offices and pay their own expenses. Its private client division offers in-house managed accounts: investment decisions are made centrally by an investment committee and applied across roughly 600 managed accounts, with two Portfolio Managers and one Associate Portfolio Manager, Priya Raman, handling client portfolios. Some managed account clients have negotiated asset-based fees; one large client pays a fee contingent on performance under a signed written fee agreement. Recently, agent Marc Belisle began selling unrelated insurance products from his office, and the firm's new Chief Compliance Officer, Dana Whitfield, is reviewing how Lansdowne's supervisory structure should respond to each feature of this business model.
questions.stem: Turning to the private client division, Whitfield checks the structure for supervising the managed account program. Which combination satisfies the IDPC requirements for a dealer that has managed accounts?

options.A: Designate a Supervisor responsible for the supervision of managed accounts, and maintain policies and procedures specifically addressing the supervision of individuals responsible for handling managed accounts
options.B: The investment committee supervises itself, since it makes the investment decisions
options.C: Any branch Supervisor may review managed accounts informally; no designation or specific policies are needed
options.D: Outsource all managed account supervision to the firm's external auditor

questions.rationale: Subsection 3971(1) requires a dealer with managed accounts to designate a Supervisor responsible for their supervision and to have policies and procedures specifically addressing the supervision of the individuals who handle managed accounts, providing reasonable assurance of compliance. Option B creates self-supervision by the decision-makers being supervised; C omits both the designation and the specific policies the rule mandates; D delegates a supervisory function to an external auditor, who provides assurance, not account supervision.
questions.source_quote: “(1) A Dealer Member that has managed accounts must: (i) designate a Supervisor to be responsible for the supervision of managed accounts, and (ii) have policies and procedures that specifically address the supervision of individuals responsible for handling managed accounts” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8404

questions.scenario: Lansdowne Park Securities Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer with two lines of business. Its retail advisory division operates through 38 independent advisors engaged as agents under principal-agent arrangements; the advisors keep their own storefront offices and pay their own expenses. Its private client division offers in-house managed accounts: investment decisions are made centrally by an investment committee and applied across roughly 600 managed accounts, with two Portfolio Managers and one Associate Portfolio Manager, Priya Raman, handling client portfolios. Some managed account clients have negotiated asset-based fees; one large client pays a fee contingent on performance under a signed written fee agreement. Recently, agent Marc Belisle began selling unrelated insurance products from his office, and the firm's new Chief Compliance Officer, Dana Whitfield, is reviewing how Lansdowne's supervisory structure should respond to each feature of this business model.
questions.stem: Whitfield learns that Associate Portfolio Manager Priya Raman has been emailing portfolio advice directly to managed account clients without anyone else seeing it first. What does IDPC section 3971 require regarding an Associate Portfolio Manager's advice?

options.A: An Associate Portfolio Manager may advise freely once the client signs the managed account agreement
options.B: The advice must be approved by a Portfolio Manager at the dealer before the Associate Portfolio Manager provides it, as part of the required direct supervision of the Associate Portfolio Manager
options.C: Only advice on derivatives requires pre-approval; equity advice does not
options.D: The Chief Financial Officer must co-sign every piece of advice

questions.rationale: Subsection 3971(3) requires policies and procedures addressing the direct supervision of any Associate Portfolio Manager providing discretionary management, including a prohibition on the APM providing advice unless it has been approved by a Portfolio Manager at the dealer before the APM provides it — consistent with the Rule 1200 definition placing APMs under a Portfolio Manager's supervision. Option A confuses the client agreement with the internal pre-approval control; C invents a derivatives-only carve-out; D assigns the role to the CFO, whose responsibilities are financial.
questions.source_quote: “the direct supervision of any Associate Portfolio Manager that provides discretionary management to managed accounts, including a prohibition on the Associate Portfolio Manager providing advice unless the advice has been approved by a Portfolio Manager at the Dealer Member prior to the Associate Portfolio Manager providing the advice.” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8419

questions.scenario: Lansdowne Park Securities Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer with two lines of business. Its retail advisory division operates through 38 independent advisors engaged as agents under principal-agent arrangements; the advisors keep their own storefront offices and pay their own expenses. Its private client division offers in-house managed accounts: investment decisions are made centrally by an investment committee and applied across roughly 600 managed accounts, with two Portfolio Managers and one Associate Portfolio Manager, Priya Raman, handling client portfolios. Some managed account clients have negotiated asset-based fees; one large client pays a fee contingent on performance under a signed written fee agreement. Recently, agent Marc Belisle began selling unrelated insurance products from his office, and the firm's new Chief Compliance Officer, Dana Whitfield, is reviewing how Lansdowne's supervisory structure should respond to each feature of this business model.
questions.stem: Finally, Whitfield reviews how the private client division pays the individuals who handle the managed accounts. A draft plan would pay the Portfolio Managers a bonus calculated on the dollar value of transactions executed in the managed accounts they handle. Why must this plan be rejected?

options.A: Bonuses for Portfolio Managers may only be paid in the dealer's own shares
options.B: Portfolio Manager compensation must be approved by each client annually
options.C: Transaction-value bonuses are permitted only if disclosed in the relationship disclosure
options.D: A dealer must not compensate persons authorized to deal with managed accounts on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account

questions.rationale: Subsection 3281(2) prohibits compensating a person referred to in section 3279 — the Portfolio Managers, Associate Portfolio Managers and sub-advisors who deal with managed accounts — on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account, removing any incentive to trade discretionary portfolios for compensation. Option C wrongly treats disclosure as curing a flat prohibition; note the written-agreement relief in 3281(1) applies to charging clients directly, not to compensating the dealer's own people; A and B invent payment-form and client-approval rules that do not exist.
questions.source_quote: “(2) A Dealer Member must not compensate a person referred to in section 3279, on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account.” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5479

questions.scenario: Pacifica Trade Direct Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer approved to provide order execution only account services through a separate business unit. Retail clients enter their own orders through Pacifica's web and mobile platforms with no intermediation by employees. The unit offers listed equities and options, and recently added over-the-counter contracts for difference (CFDs) on Canadian listed shares for retail clients. The OEO unit's derivatives activities are overseen by one designated Supervisor, Jonas Reid, who is qualified for both options and the futures-class derivatives that include CFDs; Reid has no appointed back-up. A surveillance alert shows retail client Vera Kowalski day-trading CFD positions on a thinly traded reporting issuer, with intra-day positions that surveillance estimates at about 0.7% of the issuer's float. Another client has asked Pacifica's call-centre staff which CFD to buy. The unit's head, Tomas Vega, is reviewing the supervisory implications.
questions.stem: A call-centre employee at Pacifica is tempted to answer Vera Kowalski's fellow client who asked which CFD to buy. Under the OEO supervision requirements, what must Pacifica's policies and procedures ensure on this point?

options.A: That recommendations are limited to two per client per year
options.B: That recommendations are given only by employees who have completed a derivatives course
options.C: That clients are not provided with recommendations, whether through a separate legal entity, a separate business unit, or the dealer itself
options.D: That recommendations may be given verbally but never in writing

questions.rationale: Clause 3955(1)(ii) requires the OEO dealer's policies and procedures to ensure clients are not provided with recommendations as a result of having an account with a separate legal entity, a separate business unit, or the dealer itself — answering the client's question with a product pick would breach the OEO model, under which the client is solely responsible for investment decisions. Options B, A and D each permit some recommendations, which is incompatible with the OEO account definition regardless of who gives them, how often, or in what form.
rule_refs: IDPC 1201 (definition of order execution only account)
questions.source_quote: “(ii) the Dealer Member provides no recommendation to purchase, sell, hold or exchange any security, including any class of security or security of a class of issuer, or transact in any derivative.” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 678

questions.scenario: Pacifica Trade Direct Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer approved to provide order execution only account services through a separate business unit. Retail clients enter their own orders through Pacifica's web and mobile platforms with no intermediation by employees. The unit offers listed equities and options, and recently added over-the-counter contracts for difference (CFDs) on Canadian listed shares for retail clients. The OEO unit's derivatives activities are overseen by one designated Supervisor, Jonas Reid, who is qualified for both options and the futures-class derivatives that include CFDs; Reid has no appointed back-up. A surveillance alert shows retail client Vera Kowalski day-trading CFD positions on a thinly traded reporting issuer, with intra-day positions that surveillance estimates at about 0.7% of the issuer's float. Another client has asked Pacifica's call-centre staff which CFD to buy. The unit's head, Tomas Vega, is reviewing the supervisory implications.
questions.stem: Vega examines the surveillance alert on Vera Kowalski's intra-day CFD positions estimated at 0.7% of the reporting issuer's float. Under IDPC section 3962, what must Pacifica's policies and procedures do about this situation?

options.A: Permit the position because intra-day positions are exempt from all limits
options.B: Convert the CFD position into the underlying shares automatically
options.C: Allow up to 2% of the float provided the client signs a leverage acknowledgement
options.D: Prevent a retail client from holding CFD or similar derivatives positions representing more than 0.5% of the float of a reporting issuer on an intra-day or short-term basis

questions.rationale: Clause 3962(5)(v) requires policies and procedures that prevent a retail client from holding contracts for difference or similar derivatives positions representing more than 0.5% of the float of a reporting issuer (or other publicly traded issuer) on an intra day or short term basis — Kowalski's estimated 0.7% breaches the threshold and must be prevented. Option A inverts the rule, which is specifically aimed at intra-day and short-term positions; C uses a fabricated 2% threshold with an acknowledgement cure the rule does not offer; B contradicts clause 3962(5)(vi), under which retail CFDs must not confer the right to acquire or deliver the underlying interest.
questions.source_quote: “prevent a retail client from holding contracts for difference or similar derivatives positions representing more than 0.5% of the float of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded on an intra day or short term basis, and” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8331

questions.scenario: Pacifica Trade Direct Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer approved to provide order execution only account services through a separate business unit. Retail clients enter their own orders through Pacifica's web and mobile platforms with no intermediation by employees. The unit offers listed equities and options, and recently added over-the-counter contracts for difference (CFDs) on Canadian listed shares for retail clients. The OEO unit's derivatives activities are overseen by one designated Supervisor, Jonas Reid, who is qualified for both options and the futures-class derivatives that include CFDs; Reid has no appointed back-up. A surveillance alert shows retail client Vera Kowalski day-trading CFD positions on a thinly traded reporting issuer, with intra-day positions that surveillance estimates at about 0.7% of the issuer's float. Another client has asked Pacifica's call-centre staff which CFD to buy. The unit's head, Tomas Vega, is reviewing the supervisory implications.
questions.stem: A product manager proposes a new retail CFD at Pacifica that would let the holder elect physical delivery of the underlying shares and exercise the voting rights attached to them. How do the derivatives supervision requirements treat this proposal?

options.A: It is permitted if the client deposits 50% margin
options.B: It is permitted because delivery features reduce risk for clients
options.C: It is prohibited — the dealer's policies and procedures must prohibit offering retail clients CFDs or similar derivatives that confer the right or obligation to acquire or deliver the underlying interest or confer other shareholder rights such as voting rights
options.D: It is permitted for clients who already hold the underlying shares in another account

questions.rationale: Clause 3962(5)(vi) requires policies and procedures that prohibit offering retail clients contracts for difference or similar derivatives that confer the right or obligation to acquire or deliver the underlying interest or confer any other rights of shareholders, such as voting rights — a CFD must remain a cash-settled price-difference instrument for retail clients. Options A and D invent margin and existing-holdings cures the prohibition does not allow; B mischaracterizes delivery and voting features, which would blur the CFD into share ownership, as risk-reducing.
questions.source_quote: “prohibit the offering of contracts for difference or similar derivatives to a retail client that confer the right or obligation to acquire or deliver the underlying interest or confer any other rights of shareholders, such as voting rights.” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8334

questions.scenario: Pacifica Trade Direct Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer approved to provide order execution only account services through a separate business unit. Retail clients enter their own orders through Pacifica's web and mobile platforms with no intermediation by employees. The unit offers listed equities and options, and recently added over-the-counter contracts for difference (CFDs) on Canadian listed shares for retail clients. The OEO unit's derivatives activities are overseen by one designated Supervisor, Jonas Reid, who is qualified for both options and the futures-class derivatives that include CFDs; Reid has no appointed back-up. A surveillance alert shows retail client Vera Kowalski day-trading CFD positions on a thinly traded reporting issuer, with intra-day positions that surveillance estimates at about 0.7% of the issuer's float. Another client has asked Pacifica's call-centre staff which CFD to buy. The unit's head, Tomas Vega, is reviewing the supervisory implications.
questions.stem: Vega notes that Jonas Reid is the only designated Supervisor for the unit's derivatives activities and has no appointed back-up, even though clients trade options and CFDs continuously through the platform. What does IDPC section 3960 require in this situation?

options.A: Nothing further — a single designated Supervisor always suffices regardless of coverage gaps
options.B: The dealer must appoint one or more alternate Supervisors if necessary to ensure continuous supervision of its derivatives activities
options.C: The dealer must suspend all derivatives trading whenever Reid takes vacation
options.D: Reid must transfer his designation to the Ultimate Designated Person during absences

questions.rationale: Subsection 3960(3) requires the dealer to appoint one or more alternate Supervisors if necessary to ensure continuous supervision of its derivatives activities; under 3960(4) the alternate assumes the designated Supervisor's responsibilities when the designated Supervisor is absent or trading activity requires additional qualified individuals. Option C halts business rather than maintaining supervision, which the alternate mechanism is designed to avoid; A ignores the continuity requirement on these facts; D wrongly routes derivatives supervision to the UDP, whose role is firm-level conduct responsibility, not derivatives desk coverage.
questions.source_quote: “(3) The Dealer Member must appoint one or more alternate Supervisors if necessary to ensure continuous supervision of its derivatives activities.” — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8285

questions.stem: Helene Bouchard is approved as an Associate Portfolio Manager at Drummond Capital Inc. Her Supervisor learns that, while the firm's only Portfolio Manager was on a two-week vacation, Helene gave several managed account clients advice to rotate out of long bonds. No Portfolio Manager saw the advice before it was given. How should the Supervisor assess Helene's conduct?

options.A: Acceptable, because an Associate Portfolio Manager holds discretionary management proficiency and may advise independently for short periods
options.B: Acceptable, provided the Portfolio Manager ratifies the advice within one business day of returning
options.C: A violation: an Associate Portfolio Manager must not advise on securities unless, before giving the advice, the advice has been pre-approved by a Portfolio Manager
options.D: A violation only if a client suffered losses from following the advice

questions.rationale: Subsection 2553(2) prohibits an APM from advising on securities unless, before giving the advice, the advice has been pre-approved by a Portfolio Manager — the standard is prior approval, with no vacation or short-period exception. Option B is the closest distractor, importing the next-business-day review concept that applies to certain discretionary orders under 3970(4), not to APM advice; A confuses proficiency with the pre-approval condition; D wrongly makes the violation outcome-dependent.
questions.source_quote: "An Associate Portfolio Manager must not advise on securities unless, before giving the advice, the advice has been pre-approved by a Portfolio Manager." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2994

questions.stem: Sylvain Garneau is approved as a Portfolio Manager at Castor Wealth Inc. A long-standing advisory client, who has no managed account, asks Sylvain directly for a recommendation on a TSX-listed equity. The Supervisor reviewing the interaction should conclude that Sylvain:

options.A: May provide the recommendation, because a Portfolio Manager is also permitted to conduct activities carried on by a Registered Representative in accordance with the applicable Corporation requirements
options.B: Breached his approval category, because a Portfolio Manager may only act for managed accounts
options.C: Must first obtain pre-approval of his recommendation from another Portfolio Manager
options.D: Must route the client to an Investment Representative, who will provide the recommendation

questions.rationale: Subsection 2553(1) states that a Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager is also permitted to conduct activities carried on by a Registered Representative in accordance with Corporation requirements applicable to Registered Representatives — so advising an advisory client is within the PM's permitted scope. Option B wrongly confines PMs to managed accounts; C imports the APM pre-approval condition, which does not apply to PMs; D fails because Investment Representatives trade in, but do not advise on, securities.
questions.source_quote: "A Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager is also permitted to conduct activities carried on by a Registered Representative in accordance with Corporation requirements applicable to Registered Representatives." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2991

questions.stem: Priya Raman is a Registered Representative at Northgate Securities whom CIRO has approved to deal with institutional clients. A retail investor who found Priya's name online calls her and asks her to recommend and execute a purchase of bank shares. What should Priya's Supervisor conclude about her ability to act?

options.A: She may both advise and take the order, because all Registered Representatives may serve all client types
options.B: She may take the order but not advise, because advice to retail clients is reserved for retail-approved individuals
options.C: She may act only if the trade is in mutual funds or government-guaranteed debt
options.D: She may not act for this investor: a Registered Representative dealing with institutional clients may take orders from, or give advice to, institutional clients only

questions.rationale: Clause 2553(3)(ii)(b) provides that a Registered Representative dealing with institutional clients may take orders from, or give advice to, institutional clients only; only an RR dealing with retail clients may serve all types of clients under 2553(3)(ii)(a). Option A states the retail-side rule, the closest distractor; B invents a take-orders-only carve-out that exists nowhere for institutional RRs; C confuses the product-scope notification categories in 2553(3)(iv) with the client-type restriction.
questions.source_quote: "(b) institutional clients, may take orders from, or give advice to, institutional clients only." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3006

questions.stem: Registered Representative Chantal Dube of Rocheleau Securities holds no discretionary or managed account approvals. A retail client emails her: "I'm travelling — buy whichever of the two ETFs we discussed looks better this week, your call." Chantal waits three days for a price dip and buys one of them. The account is an ordinary advisory account. How should her Supervisor characterize this?

options.A: Permissible time and price discretion within an existing client instruction
options.B: Permissible because the client invited the discretion in writing
options.C: Prohibited discretionary trading: discretion may only be exercised in a discretionary account or managed account opened in accordance with Part G of Rule 3200
options.D: Permissible provided the trade is reviewed by the Supervisor within one business day

questions.rationale: Subsection 3221(1) requires the Dealer Member to ensure individuals trading on its behalf do not engage in any discretionary trading, including time and price discretion, unless discretion is exercised in a discretionary account or managed account per Part G of Rule 3200. Choosing which security to buy goes beyond even time and price discretion, and the account is not a discretionary account. Option A is the closest distractor — the 3221(2) carve-out covers only time and price discretion in fulfilling best execution for an order for a specific amount or specific security, not security selection; B ignores that client willingness does not create a discretionary account; D confuses the discretionary-order review timing in 3970 with authorization.
questions.source_quote: "do not engage in any discretionary trading, including time and price discretion, unless discretion is exercised in a discretionary account or managed account in accordance with the requirements set out in Part G of Rule 3200." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4918

questions.stem: Bernard Cloutier resigned from Lachapelle Securities last Friday and his CIRO approval ceased on the same day. On Monday, several of his former clients call him at home and he provides them with specific buy recommendations "as a favour until you're reassigned." His former Supervisor learns of this. Which statement best describes Bernard's obligation?

options.A: If an Approved Person ceases to be approved, the former Approved Person must immediately cease any activity requiring Corporation approval
options.B: He may continue advising former clients for 30 days to ensure continuity of service
options.C: He may continue advising provided he charges no commission
options.D: He may continue advising provided each conversation is recorded for the dealer's files

questions.rationale: Subsection 2551(6) states that if an Approved Person ceases to be approved, the former Approved Person must immediately cease any activity requiring Corporation approval — advising on trades is an approval-requiring activity. Option B invents a wind-down period the rule does not give; C and D wrongly suggest that foregoing compensation or recording conversations cures acting without approval.
questions.source_quote: "If an Approved Person ceases to be approved, the former Approved Person must immediately cease any activity requiring Corporation approval." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2928

questions.stem: Therese Vachon was approved as a Registered Representative dealing with derivatives before December 31, 2025, and relies on the proficiency exemption for individuals approved prior to that date instead of writing the new Derivatives Exam. Her Supervisor reviews her current marketing materials, which advertise her as available to advise on "the full range of derivatives, including CFDs and swaps." What is the supervisory concern?

options.A: None — the grandfathering exemption preserves her full derivatives scope
options.B: An exempted Approved Person may only deal in, as applicable, legacy options or futures contracts and futures contract options, and must ensure the scope of their permitted activities is clear in all communications and dealings
options.C: She must rewrite the Derivatives Exam within 12 months before using any derivatives title
options.D: Marketing derivatives capability is prohibited for all Registered Representatives

questions.rationale: Subsection 2625(3) limits an Approved Person who is exempt under 2625(2) from the Derivatives Exam to dealing in, as applicable, legacy options or futures contract, futures contract options, and requires that the scope of their permitted activities be clear in all their communication and in all their dealings — advertising the full derivatives range including CFDs misstates that scope. Option A overstates the grandfathering; C invents a rewrite deadline; D is false — properly qualified RRs may market derivatives services.
questions.source_quote: "may only deal in, as applicable, legacy options or futures contract, futures contract options, and must ensure that the scope of their permitted activities are clear in all their communication and in all their dealings." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3334

questions.stem: Sandrine Lapierre, the designated Supervisor at Mercier Securities, is asked to give final approval to a new retail advisory account. The file is missing the signed account application; the Registered Representative says it will arrive next week and asks her to approve now so the relationship 'looks clean.' The initial trade has already settled. What must Sandrine do?

options.A: Approve now and diarize the missing application for follow-up
options.B: Approve now because the one-business-day approval deadline overrides documentation gaps
options.C: Refuse to approve: the designated Supervisor must not approve a new account unless all client account records have been collected, and the account must be restricted to liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities until final approval
options.D: Delegate the approval decision to the branch's most senior Registered Representative

questions.rationale: Subsection 3214(2) prohibits the designated Supervisor from approving a new account unless all client account records have been collected, and 3214(5) requires that where approval is not given after the initial trade, the account be restricted to only liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities until final approval. Option B is the trap — the one-business-day deadline in 3214(3) does not authorize approving an incomplete file; the correct mechanic is restriction. A approves contrary to 3214(2); D puts approval in the hands of someone who is not the designated Supervisor.
questions.source_quote: "The designated Supervisor must not approve a new account unless all client account records have been collected." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4641

questions.stem: Tania Wong, an employee of Fairmount Securities (an Investment Dealer), wants to open a personal trading account at Pelletier Capital, a different Investment Dealer. What must Pelletier do before opening the account?

options.A: Obtain CIRO's prior approval and flag the account for daily review
options.B: Open the account normally — restrictions apply only to accounts of Pelletier's own employees
options.C: Require Tania to first close all accounts at Fairmount
options.D: Obtain written approval from the other Dealer Member and designate the account as a non-client account

questions.rationale: Subsection 3214(6) requires that before opening a new account for an employee of another Dealer Member, the Dealer Member must obtain written approval from the other Dealer Member and must designate the account as a non-client account. Option A substitutes CIRO approval for the employer-dealer's written approval; B misses that the rule targets employees of other dealers precisely; C invents an account-closure precondition.
questions.source_quote: "Before opening a new account for an employee of another Dealer Member, the Dealer Member must obtain written approval from the other Dealer Member, and must designate the account as non-client account." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4652

questions.stem: Gagnon Wealth is onboarding a retail client to a new advisory account. The Registered Representative plans to recommend, at the first meeting, that the client take out a home-equity line of credit to fund the initial purchases. Before making that initial recommendation to purchase securities using borrowed money, what must the firm do under the leverage disclosure requirements?

options.A: Obtain the branch manager's written consent to the lending arrangement
options.B: Provide the retail client with a copy of the leverage risk disclosure statement and obtain the client's positive acknowledgement of receipt
options.C: Provide the disclosure within 30 days after the first leveraged purchase settles
options.D: Nothing, because the borrowing is from a bank rather than from the Dealer Member

questions.rationale: Subsection 3217(1) requires that, prior to making an initial recommendation to a retail client to purchase securities using borrowed money (or when first becoming aware of such an intention), the Dealer Member provide a copy of the leverage risk disclosure statement and obtain the retail client's positive acknowledgement of receipt. Option C is the closest distractor — the obligation is prior to the recommendation, not after settlement; A substitutes an internal consent that is not the rule's requirement; D is wrong because the statement addresses using borrowed money generally, regardless of lender.
questions.source_quote: "(i) provide each retail client with a copy of the leverage risk disclosure statement, and (ii) obtain the retail client’s positive acknowledgement that they are in receipt of the disclosure statement" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4851

questions.stem: Designated Supervisor Karim Osman is reviewing the file for a new retail derivatives account at Tessier Futures before the client's first transaction. The client intends to write uncovered index options. Karim concludes the strategy's risk characteristics do not fit the client's financial circumstances and risk profile, but the client still wants a derivatives account for covered calls. What does subsection 3251(2) direct Karim to do?

options.A: Reject the derivatives account entirely, since one intended strategy is inappropriate
options.B: Approve the account without conditions and rely on post-trade reviews to catch problems
options.C: Restrict the account from using the inappropriate strategies, note any trading restrictions imposed on the derivatives account approval, and communicate those restrictions to the Approved Person assigned to the account
options.D: Transfer the appropriateness decision to the Chief Financial Officer

questions.rationale: Subsection 3251(2) requires the relevant designated Supervisors to determine whether the risk characteristics of the strategies the client intends to use are appropriate, and if they are not, to restrict the account from using inappropriate strategies, note on the derivatives account approval any trading restrictions imposed and communicate those restrictions to the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager assigned to the account. Option A overshoots — the rule contemplates restriction, not mandatory outright rejection where other strategies remain appropriate; B abandons the gatekeeping function; D assigns the task to the wrong officer.
questions.source_quote: "the designated Supervisors should restrict the account from using inappropriate strategies and note on the derivatives account approval any trading restrictions imposed and communicate those restrictions to the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager assigned to the account." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5183

questions.stem: Renaud Brokerage is opening a margin account for retail client Donata Ferri. The new-accounts team has the margin account agreement ready. What sequencing does section 3247 require?

options.A: Prior to opening the margin account, deliver the margin account agreement to the client and obtain a copy signed by the client
options.B: Deliver the agreement with the first monthly statement and obtain the signature within 90 days
options.C: Obtain a verbal acknowledgement before opening and the signed agreement before the first margin call
options.D: Deliver the agreement only if the client intends to short sell

questions.rationale: Subsection 3247(1) requires that prior to opening a margin account, the Dealer Member deliver a margin account agreement to the client and obtain a copy of the margin account agreement signed by the client. Option B defers both steps past account opening; C substitutes a verbal step the rule does not permit for the signed agreement; D wrongly limits the agreement to short selling — it applies to all margin accounts.
questions.source_quote: "Prior to opening a margin account, a Dealer Member must: (i) deliver a margin account agreement to the client, and (ii) obtain a copy of the margin account agreement signed by the client." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5125

questions.stem: Designated Supervisor Lucie Arseneault is approving the conversion of an existing advisory account at Beaupre Securities into a discretionary account. The Registered Representative says he will have the client sign the discretionary account agreement "within a week or two of starting to trade with discretion." Lucie should respond that:

options.A: Trading may begin once the client gives verbal consent, with paperwork to follow
options.B: The Dealer Member must enter into a discretionary account agreement with the client prior to accepting the account as a discretionary account, and the designated Supervisor must approve the account and the signed agreement
options.C: The agreement is optional because the client already has an advisory relationship with the firm
options.D: Only the firm's managed account committee can authorize the conversion

questions.rationale: Clause 3273(1)(iv) requires the Dealer Member to enter into a discretionary account agreement with the client prior to accepting the account as a discretionary account, and clause 3273(1)(v) requires the designated Supervisor to approve the account as a discretionary account and approve the discretionary account agreement signed by the client. Option A inverts the sequence; C is wrong because the discretionary regime applies regardless of a pre-existing advisory relationship; D confuses the managed account committee (3972) with discretionary account acceptance.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must enter into a discretionary account agreement with the client prior to accepting the account as a discretionary account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5359

questions.stem: Faubert Direct, a CIRO-approved order execution only (OEO) division, is onboarding a new retail client online. Compliance asks the Supervisor which written disclosures must be provided prior to opening the OEO account. In addition to stating that the dealer will not provide recommendations and that the client is solely responsible for all investment decisions, the disclosures must confirm that the dealer:

options.A: Guarantees best execution at the national best bid and offer at all times
options.B: Will assess account appropriateness annually using the client's KYC information
options.C: Will not be responsible for making a suitability determination and, in particular, will not consider the client's personal and financial circumstances, investment needs and objectives, investment knowledge, risk profile or investment time horizon
options.D: Will assign a dedicated Registered Representative to monitor the account

questions.rationale: Clause 3241(3)(i)(b) requires a written statement confirming the Dealer Member will not be responsible for making a suitability determination and, in particular, will not consider the client's personal and financial circumstances, investment needs and objectives, investment knowledge, risk profile, investment time horizon, nor other similar factors; 3241(3)(i)(c) adds the no-appropriateness-determination statement, and 3241(3)(ii) requires positive acknowledgement. Option B contradicts the required disclosure — OEO accounts are exempt from the product/service-scope appropriateness assessment under 3211(2); A is an execution-quality claim not in the disclosure list; D contradicts the OEO model of no recommendations.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member will not consider the client’s personal and financial circumstances, investment needs and objectives, investment knowledge, risk profile, investment time horizon, nor other similar factors," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5007

questions.stem: Retail client Edouard Pham instructs his Registered Representative at Carillon Securities to put 60% of his account into a single speculative junior miner. The dealer determines the order does not satisfy the suitability requirements in subsection 3402(1). Under subsection 3402(5), the dealer may nonetheless carry out the instruction only if it has done all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: Informed the client of the basis for the determination that the action will not satisfy the suitability requirements and advised the client against proceeding
options.B: Recommended to the client an alternative action that satisfies the suitability requirements
options.C: Received recorded confirmation of the client's instruction to proceed despite the determination
options.D: Obtained the client's signature on a blanket waiver of all future suitability determinations for the account

questions.rationale: Subsection 3402(5) sets out three conditions for executing a client-directed unsuitable action: inform the client of the basis for the determination and advise against proceeding, recommend an alternative action that satisfies 3402(1), and receive recorded confirmation of the client's instruction to proceed. A blanket prospective waiver of suitability is not among them and is not available to retail clients — the written waiver mechanism in 3404(3)(ii) applies only to certain non-individual institutional permitted clients. Options A, B and C are each verbatim conditions of the rule.
questions.source_quote: "(iii) received recorded confirmation of the retail client’s instruction to proceed with the action despite the determination referred to in clause 3402(5)(i)." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5591

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Lanthier Capital is verifying that suitability determinations on a retail advisory book consider all required factors under subsection 3402(1). Which of the following is one of the factors the rule expressly requires?

options.A: Whether the client has previously filed a complaint with the dealer
options.B: Whether the security has been profitable for other clients of the firm
options.C: The Registered Representative's monthly commission target
options.D: The impact of the action on the retail client's account, including the concentration and liquidity of securities within the account

questions.rationale: Clause 3402(1)(i)(c) expressly lists the impact of the action on the retail client's account, including the concentration of securities, derivatives or precious metals bullion within the account and the liquidity of those holdings, among the suitability factors (alongside the client's KYC information, the firm/AP product understanding under Rule 3300, the impact of costs on returns, and a reasonable range of alternative actions). Options A, B and C are not 3402(1) factors — B substitutes other clients' outcomes, C is the compensation conflict the determination must be insulated from, and A is complaint history, not a suitability input.
questions.source_quote: "the impact of the action on the retail client’s account, including the concentration of securities, derivatives or precious metals bullion, within the account and the liquidity of those securities, derivatives or precious metals bullion," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5545

questions.stem: Sommet Securities serves Avalon Pension Trust, an institutional client. Before accepting Avalon's orders, the Registered Representative makes the required institutional suitability determination. Avalon retains three other investment dealers, runs an in-house analytics team with long derivatives experience, and routinely rejects Sommet's recommendations in favour of its own models. Applying subsection 3403(3), if the dealer has reasonable grounds for concluding Avalon is capable of making an independent investment decision and independently evaluating the investment risk, then:

options.A: The dealer must still document a full retail-style portfolio suitability analysis for each trade
options.B: The dealer's suitability obligation is fulfilled for that transaction
options.C: The dealer must obtain a written waiver before each order
options.D: The dealer must take steps to ensure the client fully understands the investment product, including the potential risks

questions.rationale: Clause 3403(3)(i) provides that where the Dealer Member has reasonable grounds for concluding the institutional client is capable of making an independent investment decision and independently evaluating the investment risk, the Dealer Member's suitability obligation is fulfilled for that transaction. Option D is the closest distractor — it states the 3403(3)(ii) consequence that applies only when the dealer does NOT have such reasonable grounds; A imports the retail regime; C confuses the case-by-case sophistication assessment with the separate written waiver available to certain permitted clients under 3404(3)(ii).
questions.source_quote: "capable of making an independent investment decision and independently evaluating the investment risk, then the Dealer Member’s suitability obligation is fulfilled for that transaction," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5629

questions.stem: At Montcalm Wealth, Registered Representative Gilles Forand is overloaded and asks his licensed assistant — who is not an Approved Person assigned to the accounts — to 'run the suitability checks' on his recommendations before they go out. The Supervisor intervenes. Which rule supports the intervention?

options.A: Suitability assessments may be performed by any employee who has passed the Canadian Investment Regulatory Exam
options.B: Suitability is solely the Dealer Member's institutional obligation, so individual assignment is irrelevant
options.C: Registered Representatives, Portfolio Managers and Associate Portfolio Managers must not delegate their responsibility for suitability assessment obligations to any other person
options.D: Delegation is permitted provided the Supervisor counter-signs each assessment

questions.rationale: Section 3406 makes compliance with suitability requirements primarily the responsibility of the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager assigned to the client account, and subsection 3406(2) states they must not delegate their responsibility for suitability assessment obligations to any other person. Option D is the trap — even Supervisor counter-signature does not authorize delegating the responsibility; A confuses exam proficiency with account assignment; B ignores the rule's express allocation of primary responsibility to the assigned individual.
questions.source_quote: "Registered Representatives, Portfolio Managers and Associate Portfolio Managers must not delegate their responsibility for suitability assessment obligations to any other person." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5674

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Daviault Securities reviews a proposed retail purchase. Each individual security in the recommendation looks defensible, but the resulting holdings would leave the client's account almost entirely in one cyclical sector. The Registered Representative argues that because each security passed suitability on its own, the trade is compliant. Under subsection 3402(4), what additional determination does the dealer owe?

options.A: That at least one alternative action was offered to the client in writing
options.B: That the trade qualifies for the institutional sophistication carve-out
options.C: That the client signed a concentration acknowledgement letter
options.D: That the retail client's account portfolio of investments that would result from the investment action is suitable for the retail client and puts the retail client's interest first

questions.rationale: Subsection 3402(4) requires that, when making a suitability determination under 3402(1), the Dealer Member determine on a reasonable basis that the retail client's account portfolio of investments that would result from the investment action it takes, recommends or exercises discretion to take is suitable for the retail client and puts the retail client's interest first — security-by-security clearance is not enough. Option A garbles the alternatives factor in 3402(1)(i)(e), which is a consideration, not a written-offer requirement; B applies an institutional concept to a retail client; C invents an acknowledgement letter mechanism.
questions.source_quote: "the retail client’s account portfolio of investments that would result from the investment action the Dealer Member takes, recommends or exercises discretion to take is suitable for the retail client and puts the retail client’s interest first." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5582

questions.stem: Under section 3301, before Quintal Securities makes a newly launched structured note available to its clients, the Dealer Member must take reasonable steps to do which of the following?

options.A: Have each Registered Representative individually approve the product for the firm's shelf
options.B: Obtain CIRO's written pre-clearance of the product
options.C: Confirm at least three competing dealers already distribute the product
options.D: Assess the relevant aspects of the security including its structure, features, risks, initial and ongoing costs and the impact of those costs; approve it to be made available to clients; and monitor it for significant changes

questions.rationale: Subsection 3301(1) sets out the firm-level product due diligence steps: assess the relevant aspects of the securities or derivatives, including structure, features, risks, initial and ongoing costs and the impact of those costs; approve the securities or derivatives to be made available to clients; and monitor them for significant changes. Option B substitutes regulator pre-clearance for the dealer's own diligence; C invents a market-adoption test; A misallocates shelf approval to individual representatives — the AP-level obligation is the KYP understanding in 3302, not shelf approval.
questions.source_quote: "(i) assess the relevant aspects of the securities or derivatives, including the securities’ or derivatives’ structure, features, risks, initial and ongoing costs and the impact of those costs," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5494

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Charbonneau Capital wants to recommend a private debt fund she discovered at an industry conference. The fund has not gone through the firm's product review and is not on its approved list. Under subsection 3301(2), the Approved Person:

options.A: Must not purchase it for, or recommend it to, a client unless it has been approved by the Dealer Member to be made available to clients
options.B: May recommend it provided she personally understands its structure, features, risks and costs
options.C: May recommend it to institutional clients only
options.D: May recommend it if she discloses in writing that the product is unapproved

questions.rationale: Subsection 3301(2) provides that an Approved Person must not purchase securities or derivatives for, or recommend securities or derivatives to, a client unless the securities or derivatives have been approved by the Dealer Member to be made available to clients under subsection 3301(1). Option B is the closest distractor — personal KYP under 3302 is necessary but cannot substitute for firm approval; C invents an institutional carve-out from product approval; D treats disclosure as a cure for selling off-shelf, which the rule does not allow.
questions.source_quote: "An Approved Person must not purchase securities or derivatives for, or recommend securities or derivatives to, a client unless the securities or derivatives have been approved by the Dealer Member to be made available to clients under subsection 3301(1)." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5498

questions.stem: Under subsection 3302(2), how is the depth of an Approved Person's know-your-product obligation calibrated?

options.A: The steps required to understand the securities or derivatives are those that are reasonable to enable the Approved Person to meet their suitability obligations under Rule 3400
options.B: The Approved Person must match the level of analysis performed by the issuer's auditors
options.C: The Approved Person must complete a CIRO-administered exam for each product category sold
options.D: The obligation is fixed: read the prospectus or offering document in full for every trade

questions.rationale: Subsection 3302(2) states that, for purposes of 3302(1), the steps required to understand the securities or derivatives are those that are reasonable to enable the Approved Person to meet their obligations under Rule 3400 — KYP depth is tied to enabling the suitability determination. Option B sets an audit standard the rule does not impose; C confuses KYP with proficiency exams; D substitutes a single fixed procedure for the reasonableness standard.
questions.source_quote: "the steps required to understand the securities or derivatives are those that are reasonable to enable the Approved Person to meet their obligations under Rule 3400." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5506

questions.stem: Aldébaran Direct operates order execution only accounts. Its Supervisor is mapping which know-your-product obligations apply. Under section 3303, which statement is accurate for an order execution only account?

options.A: Both the dealer's product due diligence under section 3301 and the Approved Person's KYP under section 3302 are inapplicable
options.B: The Approved Person's KYP obligation in section 3302 does not apply to the account, but the dealer's product due diligence obligation in section 3301 still applies
options.C: Both obligations apply in full, identical to advisory accounts
options.D: The dealer's product due diligence under section 3301 does not apply, but each Approved Person must still document product understanding per account

questions.rationale: Subsection 3303(2) exempts an order execution only account from section 3302 (the Approved Person's KYP), but subsection 3303(1) exempts section 3301 only for carrying-broker / execution-clearing-settlement-custody-only accounts — OEO is not in that list, so the dealer-level product due diligence in 3301 continues to apply. Option A wrongly extends the OEO exemption to 3301; D reverses the two exemptions; C ignores the 3303(2)(i) exemption entirely.
questions.source_quote: "Section 3302 does not apply in respect to: (i) an order execution only account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5514

questions.stem: At Verrier Wealth, the compliance department asks who bears primary responsibility for know-your-client compliance on each client account, and whether it can be shifted. Under section 3209:

options.A: Primary responsibility rests with the designated Supervisor and may be delegated to any qualified employee
options.B: Primary responsibility rests with the Chief Compliance Officer firm-wide
options.C: Compliance with know-your-client requirements is primarily the responsibility of the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager assigned to the client account, and that responsibility must not be delegated to any other person
options.D: Responsibility rotates annually between the assigned Approved Person and their Supervisor

questions.rationale: Subsections 3209(1) and 3209(2) place primary responsibility for know-your-client compliance on the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager assigned to the client account and provide that this responsibility must not be delegated to any other person. Option A misassigns primary responsibility to the Supervisor (whose role is oversight) and contradicts the no-delegation rule; B confuses the CCO's monitoring function with account-level KYC responsibility; D invents a rotation scheme.
questions.source_quote: "Compliance with the Corporation requirements relating to know‐your‐client is primarily the responsibility of the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager assigned to the client account." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4545

questions.stem: During onboarding training at Falardeau Securities, a new Registered Representative asks the Supervisor why the firm insists clients explain their debts, future goals and current cash-flow commitments rather than just their investable assets. The best grounding for the Supervisor's answer is that the dealer must:

options.A: Establish the client's creditworthiness in every account, even where no financing is provided
options.B: Collect sufficient information regarding the client's personal circumstances, financial circumstances, investment needs and objectives, investment knowledge, risk profile and investment time horizon to enable it to meet its suitability obligations under Rule 3400
options.C: Verify the client's income with the Canada Revenue Agency before the first trade
options.D: Determine whether the client qualifies for margin before any cash trade is accepted

questions.rationale: Clause 3202(1)(iii) requires the dealer to ensure it has collected sufficient information on the client's personal circumstances, financial circumstances, investment needs and objectives, investment knowledge, risk profile and investment time horizon to enable it to meet its obligations under Rule 3400 — full and transparent disclosure of debts, goals and cash flows feeds the financial-circumstances picture that drives suitability. Option A misstates 3202(1)(iv), which requires creditworthiness only where the dealer is financing the client's acquisition of a security; C invents third-party income verification; D conflates margin qualification with KYC collection.
questions.source_quote: "ensure it has collected sufficient information regarding all of the following to enable it to meet its obligations under Rule 3400:" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4424

questions.stem: The official syllabus directs Supervisors to ensure Approved Persons educate clients about the changing nature of their own circumstances. According to the syllabus parameter for outcome 6.6, the fluid nature of a client's circumstances may impact which of the following?

options.A: Only the client's investment time horizon
options.B: Only the client's risk profile and finances
options.C: The dealer's product shelf and commission schedule
options.D: Their finances, investment knowledge, risk profile, investment needs and objectives, and time horizon

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 6.6 lists the fluid nature of a client's circumstances as impacting their finances, investment knowledge, risk profile, investment needs and objectives, and time horizon — all five elements, which is why clients must be educated to report changes. Options A and B truncate the list; C shifts the impact to the dealer's offerings rather than the client's own KYC elements.
questions.source_quote: "Fluid nature of a client’s circumstances that may impact their: - Finances - Investment knowledge - Risk profile - Investment needs and objectives - Time horizon" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1335

questions.stem: A retail client at Beauchemin Wealth complains that the Relationship Disclosure she received talks about fees 'too much.' The Supervisor explains that the document must, among other things, include fee-related content. Which of the following is required content of the relationship disclosure information under subsection 3216(5)?

options.A: A description of all account service fees and charges the client will or may incur relating to the general operation of the account
options.B: A guaranteed schedule of future fee increases for the next five years
options.C: The Registered Representative's personal compensation grid
options.D: A list of every commission charged to other clients of the branch in the prior quarter

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(h) requires a description of all account service fees and charges the client will or may incur relating to the general operation of the account (with (i) covering charges for making, disposing and holding investments and (j) a general explanation of the potential impact of fees on returns including compounding). Option B demands a forward guarantee no rule requires; C and D confuse client-facing fee disclosure with internal compensation and other clients' confidential information.
questions.source_quote: "a description of all account service fees and charges the client will or may incur relating to the general operation of the account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4808

questions.stem: Rimouski Capital opens an advisory account for Glacier Re Holdings, an institutional client. The new-accounts clerk asks the Supervisor whether the firm must produce a Relationship Disclosure document for Glacier Re. The correct answer is:

options.A: Yes — relationship disclosure is required for every account regardless of client type
options.B: Yes, but only the fee sections need to be provided
options.C: No — Dealer Members are not required to provide relationship disclosure to institutional clients
options.D: No, provided Glacier Re signs a suitability waiver first

questions.rationale: Subsection 3216(1) states that Dealer Members are not required to provide relationship disclosure to institutional clients — the requirements set minimums for retail clients; consistently, 3230(2)(i) excepts sections 3216 through 3219 when opening institutional advisory accounts. Option A overextends the retail rule; B invents a partial-document obligation; D ties the exception to a waiver, but no waiver is needed — the exception flows from institutional classification itself.
questions.source_quote: "Dealer Members are not required to provide relationship disclosure to institutional clients." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4687

questions.stem: Doyon Securities is opening an initial account for the Cardinal Family Trust. Per section 3203, in the case of a trust, what identifying information must the dealer obtain when opening the initial account?

options.A: Only the name of the trustee placing the orders
options.B: The names and addresses of all trustees and all known beneficiaries and settlors of the trust
options.C: The audited financial statements of the trust for the last three years
options.D: A court order confirming the validity of the trust deed

questions.rationale: Clause 3203(1)(i) requires the Dealer Member, in the case of a trust, to obtain the names and addresses of all trustees and all known beneficiaries and settlors of the trust; the dealer must also establish the existence of the trust and identify individuals exercising control, and determine insider status including for known beneficiaries of more than 10% of the trust. Option A under-collects; C and D substitute documents the rule does not demand for the prescribed identity information.
questions.source_quote: "in the case of a trust, obtain the names and addresses of all trustees and all known beneficiaries and settlors of the trust," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4457

questions.stem: Sabourin Investments opens an initial account for Taiga Components Inc., a private corporation. Which combination correctly states two of the section 3204 requirements for opening an initial corporate account?

options.A: Obtain the names of all shareholders within 10 days; verify the CFO's personal credit history
options.B: Obtain the corporation's banking references; restrict the account to cash trades for 90 days
options.C: Obtain the names of all directors of the corporation within 30 days of opening the account; establish the identity of any individual who beneficially owns or controls 25% or more of the voting rights
options.D: File the corporation's articles with CIRO; obtain a director's guarantee of account debts

questions.rationale: Clauses 3204(1)(i) and (iii) require the Dealer Member to obtain the names of all directors of the corporation within 30 days of opening the account, and to establish the identity of any individual who is the beneficial owner, or exercises direct or indirect control or direction, of 25% or more of the voting rights attached to the outstanding voting securities. Option A swaps directors for all shareholders and invents a 10-day window plus a credit check; B and D list measures (banking references, CIRO filing, guarantees) the rule does not require.
questions.source_quote: "obtain the names of all directors of the corporation within 30 days of opening the account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4468

questions.stem: Branch Supervisor Nadia Cherif at Ravel Securities is herself qualified only for securities business. Two Registered Representatives in her branch actively advise retail clients on listed options. Under the proficiency principle and Rule 3900's resourcing requirements, what is the dealer's obligation regarding Nadia's situation?

options.A: Nothing — Supervisors review conduct, not products, so product proficiency is irrelevant
options.B: Nadia may supervise the options activity provided she completes a derivatives webinar within the year
options.C: Options supervision can be left entirely to the firm's automated alerts
options.D: The dealer must take reasonable steps to ensure Supervisors understand the products that those under their supervision trade in or advise on, to the degree necessary to properly supervise them — so the options activity needs a qualified Supervisor

questions.rationale: Subsection 3905(5) requires the Dealer Member to take reasonable steps to ensure all of its Supervisors and Executives are fully proficient and understand the products that employees and Approved Persons under their supervision trade in or advise on, as well as the services they provide, to the degree necessary to properly supervise them; GN-3900-20-001 adds that where a Supervisor is unqualified for certain products, supervision may be divided among qualified Supervisors. Option B substitutes an informal webinar for proficiency; A and C deny the product-proficiency dimension of supervision and over-rely on automation.
questions.source_quote: "ensure all of its Supervisors and Executives are fully proficient and understand the products that employees and Approved Persons under their supervision trade in or advise on," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7931

questions.stem: Under the proficiency principle in section 2602, when is an Approved Person permitted to perform an activity that requires approval?

options.A: Once CIRO approval is granted, regardless of any further education or experience
options.B: Only after satisfying the applicable proficiency requirements of Rule 2600 and having the education, experience and training that a reasonable person would consider necessary to perform the activity competently, including understanding the structure, features and risks of each product dealt with, recommended or supervised
options.C: Whenever the activity is performed under the direct observation of a Supervisor
options.D: After completing any two CIRO exams of the individual's choosing

questions.rationale: Subsection 2602(1) prohibits an Approved Person from performing an activity requiring approval unless they have satisfied the applicable Rule 2600 proficiency requirements and have the education, experience and training a reasonable person would consider necessary to perform the activity competently, including understanding the structure, features and risks of each security, derivative and precious metals bullion they deal with, recommend or supervise. Option A treats approval as the end of the matter, ignoring the competence overlay; C substitutes observation for proficiency; D mistakes the prescribed category-specific exams of 2603 for an elective menu.
questions.source_quote: "has the education, experience and training that a reasonable person would consider necessary to perform the activity competently, including understanding the structure, features and risks of each security, derivative and precious metals bullion the Approved Person deals with, recommends or supervises." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3099

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Trudel Capital is told that Registered Representative Yannick Hebert has not completed his applicable post-approval proficiency requirements within the prescribed timelines. What consequence does subsection 2552(2) attach to this failure?

options.A: The Approved Person is limited to mutual fund trades until completion
options.B: CIRO issues a warning letter, and suspension follows only after a disciplinary hearing
options.C: The dealer may internally extend the deadline by six months without notifying CIRO
options.D: The Corporation will automatically suspend the Approved Person

questions.rationale: Subsection 2552(2) provides that the Corporation will automatically suspend an Approved Person if they do not complete the applicable post-approval proficiency requirements in their category as set out in the enumerated provisions, with reinstatement under 2552(3) once requirements are completed and the Corporation notified. Option B inserts a hearing process that the automatic mechanism bypasses; C gives the dealer an extension power it does not have; A invents a mutual-funds fallback instead of suspension.
questions.source_quote: "The Corporation will automatically suspend an Approved Person if they do not complete the applicable post-approval proﬁciency requirements in the Approved Persons category" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2975

questions.stem: Cadieux Wealth is sponsoring Mira Stanic's application for approval as an Associate Portfolio Manager. The Supervisor checks her file: she completed Level 1 of the CFA program two years ago. Under sub-clause 2603(1)(ii)(b), what minimum experience must she also demonstrate before approval?

options.A: Minimum four years of relevant experience, one of which was in the last year
options.B: No experience requirement applies to Associate Portfolio Managers
options.C: Minimum two years of relevant investment management experience acceptable to the Corporation within three years prior to the date of application for approval
options.D: Five years working for an investment dealer with at least three years in a compliance capacity
