questions.rationale: Sub-clause 2603(1)(ii)(b) requires an Associate Portfolio Manager applicant to have minimum two years of relevant investment management experience acceptable to the Corporation within three years prior to the date of application for approval, in addition to the exam/designation requirement in 2603(1)(i)(g). Option A approximates the Portfolio Manager CIM-route experience standard (four years), the closest distractor; B denies an express requirement; D recites the Chief Compliance Officer experience pathway.
questions.source_quote: "Associate Portfolio Manager: Minimum two years of relevant investment management experience acceptable to the Corporation within three years prior to the date of application for approval," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3161

questions.stem: Before accepting an initial derivatives order from a retail client, Provencher Futures must comply with section 3254. Which combination of steps does the rule require?

options.A: Mail the statement within 10 days after the first fill and keep no distribution log
options.B: Provide the risk disclosure statement only to clients trading futures, not options
options.C: Provide the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement or similar Corporation-approved document before accepting the initial order, obtain the client's acknowledgement of receipt, provide any amendments, and maintain a record of the names, addresses and dates of provision
options.D: Obtain a notarized waiver of the disclosure from sophisticated retail clients

questions.rationale: Subsection 3254(1) requires the dealer to provide each derivatives retail client with the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement or similar Corporation-approved document before accepting an initial derivatives order, obtain the client's acknowledgement of receipt, provide any amendments, and maintain a record of the names and addresses of all clients to whom it was provided and the dates of provision. Option A reverses the timing and drops the recordkeeping; B has no product carve-out basis; D invents a waiver mechanism for retail clients.
questions.source_quote: "provide each derivatives retail client with the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement or other similar document, approved by Corporation before accepting an initial derivatives order from the client," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5286

questions.stem: Veilleux Direct must keep evidence that each order execution only client acknowledged the prescribed account disclosures. Under subsection 3241(4), which is an acceptable form for the record of acknowledgement?

options.A: A tape recording of a verbal acknowledgement
options.B: A file note written by the Registered Representative summarizing the call from memory
options.C: The absence of a client complaint within 30 days of account opening
options.D: An unsigned screenshot of the disclosure web page

questions.rationale: Subsection 3241(4) lists three acceptable forms for the accessible record of acknowledgement: the client's signature or initials on a form or document specifically related to the disclosure and acknowledgement, an electronic acknowledgement attached to the disclosure and acknowledgement text, or a tape recording of a verbal acknowledgement. Option B is a after-the-fact summary, not one of the prescribed forms; C treats silence as acknowledgement; D shows the disclosure existed but captures no client acknowledgement.
questions.source_quote: "(iii) a tape recording of a verbal acknowledgement." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5027

questions.stem: When must relationship disclosure information be provided to a retail client of Marquette Securities, according to subsection 3216(2)?

options.A: Only once, at the firm's next scheduled annual mailing after account opening
options.B: At the time of opening an account or accounts, and when there is a significant change to the relationship disclosure information previously provided to the client
options.C: Only when the client specifically requests it in writing
options.D: Within 60 days after the first trade settles

questions.rationale: Subsection 3216(2) requires relationship disclosure information to be provided to each retail client at the time of opening an account or accounts, and when there is a significant change to the relationship disclosure information previously provided to the client. Option A misses both the at-opening timing and the significant-change trigger; C makes a mandatory delivery contingent on request; D invents a post-trade window.
questions.source_quote: "(i) at the time of opening an account or accounts, and (ii) when there is a significant change to the relationship disclosure information previously provided to the client." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4695

questions.stem: The relationship disclosure package at Sicotte Wealth is a standardized document used for its fee-based retail advisory clients. Under subsection 3216(6), what review obligation applies before it goes to clients?

options.A: No approval is needed for standardized documents, only for customized ones
options.B: The document must be filed with CIRO for pre-approval each year
options.C: Each client must counter-sign the document before any trade
options.D: The relationship disclosure information must be approved by a partner, Director, officer or designated Supervisor, and for a standardized document the designated Supervisor must ensure the correct document is used in each client circumstance

questions.rationale: Clause 3216(6)(i) requires the relationship disclosure information provided to the client to be approved by a partner, Director, officer or designated Supervisor regardless of form; for a standardized document the designated Supervisor must ensure the correct document is used in each client circumstance, while customized documents must each be approved. Option A reverses the rule — approval applies regardless of the form; B substitutes regulator filing for internal approval; C converts the firm's delivery obligation into a client signature precondition for trading, which 3216 does not impose.
questions.source_quote: "The relationship disclosure information provided to the client must be approved by a partner, Director, officer or designated Supervisor. This approval must occur regardless of the form the relationship disclosure information takes." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4834

questions.stem: Designated Supervisor Eloise Marchetti is responsible for discretionary accounts at Quenneville Securities. She currently reviews the financial performance of each discretionary account quarterly, in line with the firm's managed account cycle. Which statement correctly identifies the gap in her ongoing review program?

options.A: Discretionary accounts require performance review only at agreement renewal
options.B: The designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must review the financial performance of each discretionary account at least monthly
options.C: Quarterly reviews are sufficient, because Rule 3900 imposes the same cycle for discretionary and managed accounts
options.D: Discretionary account reviews are required only when a client complains

questions.rationale: Subsection 3970(1) requires the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts to review the financial performance of each discretionary account at least monthly — a quarterly cycle falls short. Option C is the closest distractor — the quarterly cycle in 3973(1) applies to managed accounts, not discretionary accounts; A is also wrong because discretionary agreements are non-renewable under 3274(1)(iv); D substitutes complaint-driven review for the standing monthly obligation.
questions.source_quote: "the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must also review the financial performance of each discretionary account at least monthly." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8378

questions.stem: Designated derivatives Supervisor Paolo Greco at Manseau Futures sees that several retail derivatives accounts at the firm are designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts. How frequently must he review those particular derivatives accounts under subsection 3962(3)?

options.A: Quarterly, aligned with the managed account review
options.B: On a daily and monthly basis, the designated Supervisor must review all derivatives accounts that are designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts
options.C: Weekly during expiry months only
options.D: Annually, when loss limits are validated

questions.rationale: Subsection 3962(3) requires that, on a daily and monthly basis, the designated Supervisor review all derivatives accounts that are designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts — a heightened cadence reflecting the combined risk of discretion plus derivatives. Option A applies the general managed-account quarterly cycle from 3973, which does not displace the derivatives-specific daily/monthly requirement; C invents an expiry-month rhythm; D confuses the annual loss-limit validation in 3252(2) with account review frequency.
questions.source_quote: "On a daily and monthly basis, the designated Supervisor must review all derivatives accounts that are designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8314

questions.stem: Lalonde Direct, an order execution only business unit, asks its Supervisor what its trade-review policies and procedures must specifically address, given that no employee intermediates the orders. Under subsection 3955(2), the policies and procedures relating to the review of client trading must specifically address:

options.A: The suitability of each client's portfolio relative to KYC information
options.B: Annual face-to-face meetings between clients and Registered Representatives
options.C: Pre-approval of every order above $10,000 by a designated Supervisor
options.D: The risks associated with the method of order entry and the absence of intermediation by employees of the Dealer Member

questions.rationale: Subsection 3955(2) requires the OEO unit's policies and procedures relating to review of client trading to specifically address the risks associated with the method of order entry and the absence of intermediation by employees of the Dealer Member; under 3955(1)(iii), reviews cover the Rule 3900 concerns other than those relating to the suitability requirements. Option A is the trap — OEO accounts are exempt from suitability-based review; B contradicts the no-recommendation OEO model; C invents a dollar-threshold pre-approval regime.
questions.source_quote: "must specifically address the risks associated with the method of order entry and the absence of intermediation by employees of the Dealer Member." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8267

questions.stem: During a monthly review at Bilodeau Securities, Supervisor Anouk Pelchat notices a retail advisory client's account shows a burst of trade cancellations and frequent transfers of positions between that account and another unrelated client account, with no documented client instructions. Mapping this to the detection standards for retail accounts in subsection 3945(2), which concern do these patterns most directly raise?

options.A: Excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations indicating possible unauthorized trading
options.B: Outstanding margin calls
options.C: Deterioration of the quality of client holdings
options.D: Undisclosed short sales

questions.rationale: Clause 3945(2)(vi) requires retail supervision policies to specifically address detecting excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations indicating possible unauthorized trading — exactly the cancellations-plus-transfers pattern observed; section 3946 also requires informing Supervisors about transfers of funds and positions between unrelated accounts. Option B concerns unmet margin obligations not present here; C concerns the credit quality of holdings; D concerns short positions, none of which match the cancellation/transfer fact pattern.
questions.source_quote: "excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations indicating possible unauthorized trading," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8153

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc., an Investment Dealer, runs a retail branch in Laval. The branch roster includes: Investment Representative Denis Caya, approved to trade in securities for retail clients; Registered Representative Fatima Zidane, approved to deal with retail clients in general securities business; Associate Portfolio Manager Louis Berthiaume; and Portfolio Manager Ghislaine Audet, who oversees the branch's managed account program. Branch Supervisor Roxane Demers is conducting her quarterly conduct review and is examining four interactions: (1) Denis told a hesitant caller that a utility stock was 'a safe place to park money' and then entered the caller's buy order; (2) Fatima accepted a $500 gift card from an insurance company for referring her Vaillancourt clients to its products; (3) Louis emailed a model-portfolio rebalancing recommendation to his managed account clients that Ghislaine had not seen; and (4) Ghislaine personally advised one of her long-time advisory (non-managed) clients to trim a bank position.
questions.stem: Roxane concludes that interaction (1) involved Denis acting outside his Investment Representative approval category. The branch manager suggests letting it slide because 'Denis is technically the one on the hook, not the firm.' Which provision contradicts the branch manager and obliges the firm to act?

options.A: Subsection 2551(4), which places only the individual, never the firm, under Corporation jurisdiction
options.B: Subsection 2551(2), which limits Approved Person status to a Dealer Member's director, partner, officer or employee
options.C: Subsection 2551(3): a Dealer Member must ensure that each Approved Person at the Dealer Member complies with Corporation requirements applicable to that individual's Approved Person category
options.D: Subsection 2551(1)(ii), which requires the individual to be registered under securities laws in each relevant jurisdiction

questions.rationale: Subsection 2551(3) requires the Dealer Member to ensure that each Approved Person at the Dealer Member complies with Corporation requirements applicable to that individual's Approved Person category — category compliance is a firm obligation enforced through supervision, so Roxane cannot let the breach slide. Option A misreads 2551(4), which actually confirms all Approved Persons are subject to Corporation jurisdiction without relieving the firm of its 2551(3) duty; B governs who can be an Approved Person, not ongoing category compliance; D concerns securities-law registration status, not staying within the approved category's permitted activities.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must ensure that each Approved Person at the Dealer Member complies with Corporation requirements applicable to that individual’s Approved Person category." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2920

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc., an Investment Dealer, runs a retail branch in Laval. The branch roster includes: Investment Representative Denis Caya, approved to trade in securities for retail clients; Registered Representative Fatima Zidane, approved to deal with retail clients in general securities business; Associate Portfolio Manager Louis Berthiaume; and Portfolio Manager Ghislaine Audet, who oversees the branch's managed account program. Branch Supervisor Roxane Demers is conducting her quarterly conduct review and is examining four interactions: (1) Denis told a hesitant caller that a utility stock was 'a safe place to park money' and then entered the caller's buy order; (2) Fatima accepted a $500 gift card from an insurance company for referring her Vaillancourt clients to its products; (3) Louis emailed a model-portfolio rebalancing recommendation to his managed account clients that Ghislaine had not seen; and (4) Ghislaine personally advised one of her long-time advisory (non-managed) clients to trim a bank position.
questions.stem: Roxane turns to interaction (2), Fatima's $500 gift card from the insurance company for referrals of Vaillancourt clients. Which subsection of Rule 2500 makes this a problem, and why?

options.A: Subsection 2551(5), because the gift card misrepresents her title to the public
options.B: Subsection 2551(7), because an Approved Person must not accept, directly or indirectly, any remuneration, gratuity, benefit or other consideration from any person other than the Dealer Member, its related companies, or affiliates for Dealer Member related activities
options.C: Subsection 2551(2), because only directors, partners, officers or employees can be Approved Persons
options.D: Subsection 2552(4), because the gift was not reported on the monthly conditions report

questions.rationale: Subsection 2551(7) prohibits an Approved Person from accepting, or allowing an associate to accept, directly or indirectly, any remuneration, gratuity, benefit or other consideration from any person other than the Dealer Member, its related companies, or affiliates for Dealer Member related activities — a third-party gift card tied to referrals of her dealer clients falls squarely within the prohibition. Option A cites the titles-and-designations rule, which addresses public-facing titles, not compensation; C concerns who may be an Approved Person; D concerns the dealer's report on conditions imposed under Rule 8200/9200, not gifts.
questions.source_quote: "an Approved Person must not accept, nor allow an associate to accept, directly or indirectly, any remuneration, gratuity, benefit or other consideration from any person other than the Dealer Member," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2930

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc., an Investment Dealer, runs a retail branch in Laval. The branch roster includes: Investment Representative Denis Caya, approved to trade in securities for retail clients; Registered Representative Fatima Zidane, approved to deal with retail clients in general securities business; Associate Portfolio Manager Louis Berthiaume; and Portfolio Manager Ghislaine Audet, who oversees the branch's managed account program. Branch Supervisor Roxane Demers is conducting her quarterly conduct review and is examining four interactions: (1) Denis told a hesitant caller that a utility stock was 'a safe place to park money' and then entered the caller's buy order; (2) Fatima accepted a $500 gift card from an insurance company for referring her Vaillancourt clients to its products; (3) Louis emailed a model-portfolio rebalancing recommendation to his managed account clients that Ghislaine had not seen; and (4) Ghislaine personally advised one of her long-time advisory (non-managed) clients to trim a bank position.
questions.stem: Roxane assesses interaction (3) — Louis, the Associate Portfolio Manager, emailing a rebalancing recommendation to his managed account clients before Ghislaine saw it. Roxane should conclude that Louis's conduct was:

options.A: Acceptable, because rebalancing within an existing model is administrative rather than advice
options.B: Acceptable, provided Ghislaine reviews the email within five business days
options.C: Acceptable, because Louis is approved by the Corporation to provide discretionary portfolio management
options.D: A breach, because an Associate Portfolio Manager must not advise on securities unless the advice was pre-approved by a Portfolio Manager before it was given

questions.rationale: Subsection 2553(2) prohibits an Associate Portfolio Manager from advising on securities unless, before giving the advice, the advice has been pre-approved by a Portfolio Manager; Rule 1201 likewise defines the APM as providing discretionary portfolio management under the supervision of a Portfolio Manager. Option C is the closest distractor — Louis's approval to manage discretionary assets does not remove the pre-approval condition on his advice; A mislabels a rebalancing recommendation as administrative; B substitutes after-the-fact review for the required prior approval.
questions.source_quote: "An individual designated by the Dealer Member and approved by the Corporation to provide discretionary portfolio management for managed accounts under the supervision of a Portfolio Manager." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 207

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc., an Investment Dealer, runs a retail branch in Laval. The branch roster includes: Investment Representative Denis Caya, approved to trade in securities for retail clients; Registered Representative Fatima Zidane, approved to deal with retail clients in general securities business; Associate Portfolio Manager Louis Berthiaume; and Portfolio Manager Ghislaine Audet, who oversees the branch's managed account program. Branch Supervisor Roxane Demers is conducting her quarterly conduct review and is examining four interactions: (1) Denis told a hesitant caller that a utility stock was 'a safe place to park money' and then entered the caller's buy order; (2) Fatima accepted a $500 gift card from an insurance company for referring her Vaillancourt clients to its products; (3) Louis emailed a model-portfolio rebalancing recommendation to his managed account clients that Ghislaine had not seen; and (4) Ghislaine personally advised one of her long-time advisory (non-managed) clients to trim a bank position.
questions.stem: Finally, Roxane evaluates interaction (4): Portfolio Manager Ghislaine advising a long-time advisory (non-managed) client to trim a bank position. Which conclusion is correct?

options.A: No category breach: a Portfolio Manager is also permitted to conduct activities carried on by a Registered Representative, in accordance with the Corporation requirements applicable to Registered Representatives
options.B: A breach: Portfolio Managers may only act through managed account mandates
options.C: A breach unless another Portfolio Manager pre-approved the advice
options.D: No breach, but only because the advice was to sell rather than to buy

questions.rationale: Subsection 2553(1) permits a Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager to also conduct activities carried on by a Registered Representative in accordance with Corporation requirements applicable to Registered Representatives — advising an advisory client is RR activity within Ghislaine's permitted scope. Option B wrongly confines PMs to managed accounts; C applies the APM pre-approval condition of 2553(2) to a full Portfolio Manager; D invents a buy/sell distinction with no basis in the rules.
questions.source_quote: "is also permitted to conduct activities carried on by a Registered Representative in accordance with Corporation requirements applicable to Registered Representatives." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2992

questions.scenario: Castellane Capital Inc., an Investment Dealer, offers retail advisory, discretionary, managed and derivatives accounts, and operates an order execution only (OEO) division within a separate business unit. Marie-Pier Goulet is the designated Supervisor for retail account activity, and Etienne Vachon is the designated Supervisor for derivatives accounts. In one review cycle the following surface: (a) Etienne is drafting the firm's client notification policies and procedures for derivatives accounts; (b) the OEO unit's marketing team proposes that OEO staff send 'idea lists' of suggested stocks to active OEO clients to boost volumes; (c) Marie-Pier finds a retail account where the client has been buying while three margin calls remain unmet; and (d) a futures client's account shows cumulative losses now exceeding the loss limit approved when the account was opened.
questions.stem: Item (a): Etienne is drafting the derivatives client-notification policies and procedures. Under subsection 3962(4), which set of matters must clients be notified of?

options.A: Approaching expiry dates; significant changes in derivatives resulting from changes in the underlying interest; any changes in the Dealer Member's business policy; and any new developments in the trading or regulation of derivatives that may impact clients
options.B: Only margin calls and account statements
options.C: The names of the firm's market-making counterparties for each contract
options.D: Quarterly summaries of the firm's proprietary derivatives positions

questions.rationale: Subsection 3962(4) requires policies and procedures that specifically address notifying clients of approaching expiry dates, significant changes in derivatives resulting from changes in the underlying interest, any changes in the Dealer Member's business policy, and any new developments in the trading or regulation of derivatives that may impact clients. Option B truncates the list to routine account communications; C and D concern dealer-side trading information that is not part of the prescribed client notifications.
questions.source_quote: "(i) approaching expiry dates, (ii) significant changes in derivatives resulting from changes in the underlying interest, (iii) any changes in the Dealer Member’s business policy, and (iv) any new developments in the trading or regulation of derivatives that may impact clients." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8318

questions.scenario: Castellane Capital Inc., an Investment Dealer, offers retail advisory, discretionary, managed and derivatives accounts, and operates an order execution only (OEO) division within a separate business unit. Marie-Pier Goulet is the designated Supervisor for retail account activity, and Etienne Vachon is the designated Supervisor for derivatives accounts. In one review cycle the following surface: (a) Etienne is drafting the firm's client notification policies and procedures for derivatives accounts; (b) the OEO unit's marketing team proposes that OEO staff send 'idea lists' of suggested stocks to active OEO clients to boost volumes; (c) Marie-Pier finds a retail account where the client has been buying while three margin calls remain unmet; and (d) a futures client's account shows cumulative losses now exceeding the loss limit approved when the account was opened.
questions.stem: Item (b): the OEO marketing proposal to have OEO staff send 'idea lists' of suggested stocks to active OEO clients. Why must the Supervisor reject this proposal?

options.A: Idea lists are permitted but must be limited to ten stocks per month
options.B: The OEO unit's policies and procedures must ensure that clients are not provided with recommendations as a result of having an account with the separate business unit or the Dealer Member
options.C: Idea lists are permitted if every listed stock is on the firm's approved product shelf
options.D: Idea lists are permitted provided clients positively acknowledge receipt

questions.rationale: Clause 3955(1)(ii) requires the OEO unit's policies and procedures to ensure that clients are not provided with recommendations as a result of the client having an account with a separate legal entity of the Dealer Member, a separate business unit, or the Dealer Member itself; consistently, the Rule 1201 OEO definition requires that the dealer provide no recommendation to purchase, sell, hold or exchange any security. Suggested-stock lists are recommendations. Options A, C and D each treat recommendations as permissible with conditions (volume caps, shelf status, acknowledgement) — but the OEO model excludes recommendations altogether.
questions.source_quote: "ensure that clients are not provided with recommendations as a result of the client having an account with: (a) a separate legal entity of the Dealer Member, (b) a separate business unit of the Dealer Member, or (c) the Dealer Member itself," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8252

questions.scenario: Castellane Capital Inc., an Investment Dealer, offers retail advisory, discretionary, managed and derivatives accounts, and operates an order execution only (OEO) division within a separate business unit. Marie-Pier Goulet is the designated Supervisor for retail account activity, and Etienne Vachon is the designated Supervisor for derivatives accounts. In one review cycle the following surface: (a) Etienne is drafting the firm's client notification policies and procedures for derivatives accounts; (b) the OEO unit's marketing team proposes that OEO staff send 'idea lists' of suggested stocks to active OEO clients to boost volumes; (c) Marie-Pier finds a retail account where the client has been buying while three margin calls remain unmet; and (d) a futures client's account shows cumulative losses now exceeding the loss limit approved when the account was opened.
questions.stem: Item (c): the retail client buying while three margin calls remain unmet. Marie-Pier checks the firm's retail supervision standards. Under subsections 3945(2) and 3946(1), how is this situation captured?

options.A: It is not a supervisory matter because margin is a credit decision, not a conduct issue
options.B: It is captured only by the firm's AML program
options.C: Retail supervision policies must specifically address detecting outstanding margin calls, and Supervisors must be informed of trading while the account is under margined
options.D: It requires escalation only if the client is also an insider

questions.rationale: Clause 3945(2)(xiv) requires retail supervision policies and procedures to specifically address the detection of outstanding margin calls, and clause 3946(1)(iv) requires identifying, dealing with and informing the appropriate Supervisors of trading while the account is under margined. Option A wrongly severs margin discipline from conduct supervision; B routes the issue to AML, which addresses different risks; D conditions escalation on insider status, which is a separate detection concern.
questions.source_quote: "(iv) trading while the account is under margined." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8195

questions.scenario: Castellane Capital Inc., an Investment Dealer, offers retail advisory, discretionary, managed and derivatives accounts, and operates an order execution only (OEO) division within a separate business unit. Marie-Pier Goulet is the designated Supervisor for retail account activity, and Etienne Vachon is the designated Supervisor for derivatives accounts. In one review cycle the following surface: (a) Etienne is drafting the firm's client notification policies and procedures for derivatives accounts; (b) the OEO unit's marketing team proposes that OEO staff send 'idea lists' of suggested stocks to active OEO clients to boost volumes; (c) Marie-Pier finds a retail account where the client has been buying while three margin calls remain unmet; and (d) a futures client's account shows cumulative losses now exceeding the loss limit approved when the account was opened.
questions.stem: Item (d): the futures client whose cumulative losses now exceed the approved loss limit. Which provision makes this a matter Etienne's derivatives transaction-review program must be specifically designed to detect?

options.A: Clause 3963(1)(iv) — review of derivative transactions must specifically address detecting cumulative losses exceeding approved client loss limits in the relevant accounts
options.B: Subsection 3216(2) — relationship disclosure must be re-delivered after significant losses
options.C: Subsection 3219(1) — hold-mail accounts must be reviewed by a Supervisor
options.D: Subsection 3247(2) — the margin account agreement must describe the dealer's right to pledge assets

questions.rationale: Clause 3963(1)(iv) requires the Dealer Member's policies and procedures to specifically address the review of derivative transactions to detect cumulative losses exceeding approved client loss limits in accounts determined in accordance with clause 3252(1)(vii) and subsection 3252(2) — the loss-limit breach is a named derivatives detection item, and 3962(1)(i) ties the Supervisor to reviewing and approving loss limits when set or updated. Options B, C and D cite real provisions (relationship disclosure timing, hold-mail review, margin agreement content) that have nothing to do with detecting loss-limit breaches.
questions.source_quote: "cumulative losses exceeding approved client loss limits in accounts determined in accordance with clause 3252(1)(vii) and subsection 3252(2)," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8344

questions.stem: Beaupré Valeurs Mobilières is drafting its compliance manual chapter on public communications. Under IDPC Rule 3600, what two things must the dealer's framework for communications with the public include?

options.A: Policies and procedures that specifically address communication with the public, and monitoring of compliance with those policies and procedures
options.B: CIRO pre-clearance of all advertising, and an annual attestation filed with the Corporation
options.C: A designated Executive's signature on every client letter, and quarterly reporting to the board
options.D: Outsourcing of all advertising review to an external law firm, and a standing media-relations committee

questions.rationale: Section 3601(1) requires the dealer's policies and procedures to specifically address communication with the public and requires the dealer to monitor compliance with those policies and procedures to provide reasonable assurance of compliance by the dealer, its employees and Approved Persons. Option B is wrong because CIRO does not pre-clear dealer advertising; review and approval are internal obligations. Option C overstates the requirement — pre-use approval by a designated Supervisor applies only to the materials listed in 3602(3), not every client letter, and no Executive signature is prescribed. Option D invents an outsourcing requirement found nowhere in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member’s policies and procedures must specifically address communication with the public and the Dealer Member must monitor compliance with these policies and procedures" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5839-5841

questions.stem: A draft brochure at Carmichael Investments states that 'clients who followed our balanced strategy averaged 11% annually' based on a hand-picked subset of accounts, without explaining how the subset was chosen. Why does this violate the advertising standards in IDPC section 3602?

options.A: It uses unrepresentative statistics to suggest unwarranted or exaggerated conclusions and fails to identify the material assumptions made in arriving at those conclusions
options.B: Performance figures may never appear in a brochure under any circumstances
options.C: All statistics in sales literature must be audited by an external accounting firm before use
options.D: Brochures may only quote index returns, never account returns

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(1)(iii) prohibits advertisements, sales literature or correspondence that uses unrepresentative statistics to suggest unwarranted or exaggerated conclusions, or fails to identify the material assumptions made in arriving at those conclusions — a cherry-picked account subset with no methodology disclosure does both. Option B is wrong: performance content is not banned; it triggers heightened controls (material containing performance reports used to solicit clients requires designated-Supervisor pre-approval under 3602(3)(vi)). Options C and D invent audit and index-only requirements that do not exist in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "uses unrepresentative statistics to suggest unwarranted or exaggerated conclusions, or fails to identify the material assumptions made in arriving at these conclusions," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5858-5859

questions.stem: A market commentary email drafted by a Registered Representative at Halverson Securities says 'the Bank of Canada will cut rates twice this year, so bond prices are heading up.' The statement is presented as fact. Under IDPC 3602(1), what is the deficiency?

options.A: Rate predictions may only be made by the dealer's chief economist
options.B: Commentary on interest rates is prohibited in client emails
options.C: It contains an opinion or forecast of future events that is not clearly labeled as such
options.D: It is acceptable as long as the prediction later proves accurate

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(1)(iv) prohibits communications containing any opinion or forecast of future events which is not clearly labeled as such. The rate-cut prediction is a forecast presented as fact, so it must be clearly labeled as opinion or forecast. Option D is the closest trap — accuracy after the fact does not cure the labeling failure, because the prohibition attaches at the time the communication is issued. Options A and B invent restrictions on who may comment and on subject matter that do not exist in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "contains any opinion or forecast of future events which is not clearly labeled as such," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5860

questions.stem: Drummond Capital prepares a one-page flyer on a covered-call ETF that describes the income features in detail but says nothing about the strategy's capped upside or market risk. Every statement in the flyer is factually accurate. Which standard in IDPC 3602(1) does the flyer still breach?

options.A: It contains an untrue statement of material fact
options.B: It uses an unapproved professional title
options.C: It promises specific results
options.D: It fails to fairly present the potential risks to the client

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(1)(v) prohibits issuing material that fails to fairly present the potential risks to the client — accuracy of what is said does not cure a one-sided presentation that omits the risk side. Option A is the closest distractor: the related prohibition in 3602(1)(i) covers untrue statements or omissions of material fact, but the question specifies all statements are accurate, and the distinct, squarely applicable standard is the fair presentation of risks. Options B and C describe different prohibitions (misleading titles under 3640; unjustified promises of specific results under 3602(1)(ii)) not engaged by these facts.
questions.source_quote: "fails to fairly present the potential risks to the client," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5861

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Okafor Wealth wants to add 'Chairman's Club Top Producer 2025' to her email signature. The award is granted by the dealer to its highest-revenue advisors. Under IDPC 3640, may the Supervisor permit this?

options.A: Yes, because the award is genuine and internally verifiable
options.B: No, because email signatures may not contain any awards of any kind
options.C: Yes, provided the dealer's logo appears next to the award name
options.D: No — an Approved Person who interacts with clients must not use a title, designation, award, or recognition that is based partly or entirely on their sales activity or revenue generation

questions.rationale: Clause 3640(2)(i) prohibits a client-facing Approved Person from using a title, designation, award, or recognition if it is based partly or entirely on that Approved Person's sales activity or revenue generation — a 'top producer' award is the paradigm case. Option A is the closest distractor: genuineness is irrelevant because the prohibition targets the revenue basis of the recognition, not its accuracy. Option C's logo does not change the analysis. Option B overshoots — awards not based on sales activity or revenue generation (for example, a bona fide professional designation) are not caught by this clause.
questions.source_quote: "if based partly or entirely on that Approved Person’s sales activity or revenue generation, a title, designation, award, or recognition," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6109-6110

questions.stem: A client-facing Registered Representative at Pelletier Securities prints business cards calling himself 'Vice-President, Private Clients.' The dealer has never appointed him to any corporate office. Under IDPC 3640(2), this use of the title is:

options.A: Acceptable, since 'Vice-President' is a common courtesy title in the industry
options.B: Acceptable if his branch manager verbally agrees
options.C: Prohibited — a corporate officer title may not be used unless the dealer has appointed the Approved Person to that corporate office pursuant to applicable corporate law
options.D: Prohibited only if a client actually complains of being misled

questions.rationale: Clause 3640(2)(ii) prohibits a client-facing Approved Person from using a corporate officer title unless their dealer has appointed that Approved Person to that corporate office pursuant to applicable corporate law. Option A is the trap — industry custom does not override the express prohibition. Option B fails because the clause requires an appointment to the office under corporate law, not informal managerial consent. Option D is wrong because the prohibition operates regardless of whether anyone was actually misled; 3640(1) is framed around what 'could reasonably be expected to deceive or mislead.'
questions.source_quote: "a corporate officer title, unless their Dealer Member has appointed that Approved Person to that corporate office pursuant to applicable corporate law," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6111-6112

questions.stem: A team of Registered Representatives at Whitfield Securities markets itself under the trade name 'Whitfield Pension Guardians,' although the team offers no pension administration services and is not affiliated with any pension plan. Which aspect of IDPC 3640(1) is engaged?

options.A: Trade names are entirely prohibited; only the dealer's legal name may appear in marketing
options.B: Holding themselves out through a trade name in a manner that could reasonably be expected to mislead as to the products or services provided, or to be provided
options.C: Trade names need only be registered with the local chamber of commerce
options.D: The trade name is acceptable because no specific performance is promised

questions.rationale: Subsection 3640(1) prohibits an Approved Person from holding themselves out, including through the use of a trade name, in a manner that could reasonably be expected to deceive or mislead as to (among other things) the products or services provided, or to be provided. 'Pension Guardians' implies a pension service the team does not offer. Option A is wrong — trade names are not banned outright; the rule regulates misleading use. Option D confuses this with the separate 3602(1)(ii) prohibition on promises of specific results; a communication can mislead about services without promising performance. Option C invents an irrelevant registration test.
questions.source_quote: "a Dealer Member must not hold itself or its Approved Persons out, including through the use of a trade name, in a manner that could reasonably be expected to deceive or mislead any person or company" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6097-6099

questions.stem: The compliance department at Sherbrooke Investments asks how long it must keep copies of past advertisements and the related records of supervisory review, and in what state of accessibility. Under IDPC 3602(6) and section 3803, the answer is:

options.A: Two years, in any format, available within 30 days of a CIRO request
options.B: Five years, but only for advertisements that generated client complaints
options.C: A minimum of seven years from the date the record is created, in durable and accessible form, and readily available for inspection by the Corporation
options.D: Indefinitely, in original paper form only

questions.rationale: Subsection 3602(6) requires the dealer to retain copies of all advertisements, sales literature and correspondence and all records of supervision for the period set out in section 3803, and those items must be readily available for inspection by the Corporation. Section 3803(1) sets that period at a minimum of seven years from the date the record is created, in durable and accessible form, unless a different period is prescribed. Option A understates both the period and the availability standard. Option B is wrong — retention applies to all such records, not only complaint-linked ones. Option D overstates the period and the rule does not require paper originals.
questions.source_quote: "for a minimum of seven years from the date the record is created unless Corporation requirements or securities laws relating to the specific type of record require a different retention period." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6721-6723

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Lachapelle Securities is updating the firm's marketing policies and procedures and lists the legal regimes the review program must take into account. According to the Supervisor Exam syllabus parameters for outcome 8.1, which piece of legislation is specifically identified as a legislative requirement relevant to supervising client communications?

options.A: The Bank Act
options.B: The Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act
options.C: The Canada Business Corporations Act
options.D: Canadian Anti-spam Legislation

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for outcome 8.1 expressly lists 'Legislative requirements, including Canadian Anti-spam Legislation' among the matters a Supervisor must understand when reviewing and supervising advertisements, sales literature and client correspondence — commercial electronic messages to clients and prospects engage CASL. This also aligns with clause 3602(1)(vii), which prohibits communications that fail to comply with Corporation requirements or any applicable laws. The Bank Act, BIA and CBCA (options A, B, C) are real statutes but are not the legislation the syllabus singles out for communications supervision.
questions.source_quote: "Legislative requirements, including Canadian Anti-spam Legislation" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1531

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Verdun Capital discovers that a Registered Representative has been discussing trade ideas with clients through a personal encrypted messaging app instead of the dealer's approved email and chat systems. Why is this 'off-channel' communication a supervisory problem under the IDPC framework?

options.A: Encrypted apps are illegal in Canada for any business use
options.B: Business correspondence sent outside firm systems escapes the dealer's required review and supervision and cannot be retained with the records of supervision the dealer must keep and make readily available for inspection
options.C: It is only a problem if a client suffers a loss on a recommended trade
options.D: Personal devices may be used freely as long as the Representative summarizes the conversations monthly

questions.rationale: Off-channel communication issues are an express syllabus parameter under outcome 8.1, and the underlying rule obligations explain why: 3602(2) requires policies and procedures specifically addressing the review and supervision of correspondence relating to the dealer's business, and 3602(6) requires the dealer to retain copies of all correspondence and all records of supervision, readily available for inspection by the Corporation. Correspondence on a personal app defeats both review and retention. Option C is wrong because the obligations are not loss-contingent. Option D's monthly summaries do not satisfy retention of the correspondence itself. Option A is a false legal claim.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must retain copies of all advertisements, sales literature and correspondence and all records of supervision for the period set out in section 3803." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5889-5890

questions.stem: For advertising, sales literature or correspondence that is NOT on the list of items requiring designated-Supervisor approval before use, IDPC 3602(4) requires the Investment Dealer to ensure the material is reviewed in a manner appropriate to its type through which of the following methods?

options.A: Pre-use approval, post-use review, or post-use sampling
options.B: Post-use sampling only; pre-use approval is prohibited for unlisted material
options.C: External legal review followed by CIRO filing
options.D: No review of any kind, since only the 3602(3) items are regulated

questions.rationale: Subsection 3602(4) requires that all advertising, sales literature or correspondence not listed in subsection 3602(3) is reviewed in a manner appropriate to the type of material through pre-use approval, post-use review, or post-use sampling. Option B inverts the rule — pre-use approval remains an available (often the most cautious) method for unlisted material; it is mandatory only for the 3602(3) items. Option D is wrong because unlisted material is still subject to review, just with flexibility on method. Option C invents external review and CIRO filing obligations.
questions.source_quote: "is reviewed in a manner appropriate to the type of material through: (i) pre-use approval, (ii) post-use review, or (iii) post-use sampling." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5880-5883

questions.stem: A client-facing Approved Person at Moreau Wealth begins using the designation 'Certified Retirement Income Architect' on LinkedIn. The designation comes from a legitimate course she completed, but Moreau Wealth has not approved her use of it. Under IDPC 3640(2), the use of the designation is:

options.A: Permitted, since the designation was legitimately earned
options.B: Prohibited, because if the Approved Person's Dealer Member has not approved the use of a title or designation by that Approved Person, that title or designation must not be used
options.C: Permitted on social media but not on business cards
options.D: Prohibited only if the designation relates to insurance products

questions.rationale: Clause 3640(2)(iii) prohibits a client-facing Approved Person from using a title or designation if the Approved Person's Dealer Member has not approved its use by that Approved Person. Option A is the closest distractor — legitimately earning a designation does not substitute for the dealer's approval of its use; the clause makes dealer approval the gate. Option C has no basis: the prohibition is not medium-specific, and social media posts are communications with the public. Option D invents a product-line carve-out.
questions.source_quote: "if the Approved Person’s Dealer Member has not approved the use by that Approved Person of a title or designation, that title or designation." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6113-6114

questions.stem: A national newspaper invites Arbour Hill Securities to nominate advisors for its 'Top 50 Wealth Advisors' ranking, which scores nominees primarily on assets gathered and revenue produced. A Supervisor reviewing whether nominated Approved Persons may promote their inclusion to clients should be most concerned that:

options.A: Newspaper rankings are automatically exempt from the dealer's communications policies
options.B: Promoting a recognition that is based partly or entirely on the Approved Person's sales activity or revenue generation is prohibited for client-facing Approved Persons, and the use of advisor ranking/contest lists is a specific supervision concern under the syllabus
options.C: The only requirement is that the dealer pay the newspaper's licensing fee
options.D: Rankings may be promoted freely as long as the methodology is published by the newspaper

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 8.1 flags 'Use of Advisor ranking/contest lists' as a supervision topic, and clause 3640(2)(i) prohibits a client-facing Approved Person from using a title, designation, award, or recognition based partly or entirely on that Approved Person's sales activity or revenue generation — a ranking scored on assets and revenue is such a recognition. Option D is the closest distractor: third-party publication of the methodology does not change the revenue basis of the recognition. Options A and C have no foundation; external origin does not exempt the material from the firm's communications supervision.
questions.source_quote: "Use of Advisor ranking/contest lists" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1526

questions.stem: Tanaka Securities is launching an evening cold-calling campaign for its new fee-based program. The branch manager drafts a calling script and tells the team to start dialing that night, planning to 'tidy up the wording later.' What does IDPC 3602(3) require before the campaign begins?

options.A: Nothing — scripts are internal documents outside the communications rules
options.B: Post-use sampling of recorded calls within 30 days of the campaign
options.C: Approval of the telemarketing script by a designated Supervisor before use
options.D: Filing of the script with CIRO five business days before first use

questions.rationale: Subsection 3602(3) lists telemarketing scripts among the items a Dealer Member must ensure are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication. Calling before approval breaches the rule regardless of later 'tidying up.' Option B is the closest distractor — post-use sampling is a permissible method only for material not listed in 3602(3); scripts are listed, so pre-use approval is mandatory. Option A mischaracterizes a script read to the public as internal. Option D invents a CIRO filing; the approval obligation is internal, by a designated Supervisor.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must ensure that the following items are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication: (i) research reports, (ii) market letters, (iii) telemarketing scripts," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5866-5870

questions.stem: Rocheleau Investments will host two client events next month: (1) a 'Wealth Strategies Showcase' presenting the firm's new structured-note offering, and (2) a plain financial-literacy session on how registered plans are taxed, with no firm products mentioned. Applying IDPC 3602(3)(iv), which seminar texts require designated-Supervisor approval before use?

options.A: The showcase text, because promotional seminar texts require pre-use approval while educational seminar texts are excluded from the pre-approval list
options.B: Both texts, because all seminar materials require pre-use approval
options.C: Neither text, because spoken-event materials are not 'publications'
options.D: The literacy session text, because tax content is higher risk than product content

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(3)(iv) requires designated-Supervisor approval before use or publication of promotional seminar texts and expressly excludes educational seminar texts from that pre-approval list. The product showcase is promotional; the literacy session as described is educational. Option B is the trap — it ignores the parenthetical exclusion. Note the educational text is still subject to review under 3602(4) by an appropriate method, but not mandatory pre-approval. Option C wrongly treats seminar texts as outside the rule when they are expressly listed. Option D inverts the rule's promotional/educational distinction with an invented risk ranking.
questions.source_quote: "promotional seminar texts (excluding educational seminar texts)," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5871

questions.stem: Marketing staff at Delorme Capital design a brand-new template advertisement for digital banners, with placeholders for branch phone numbers. Six branches will populate the placeholders and run the banners locally. How should the designated Supervisor's pre-approval obligation under IDPC 3602(3) be applied?

options.A: Each of the six populated banners is a separate original advertisement requiring six separate pre-approvals
options.B: No approval is needed because digital banners are not 'publications'
options.C: Approval may occur after the first week of the campaign as a post-use review
options.D: The original template advertisement must be approved by a designated Supervisor before use; the rule's pre-approval list targets original advertisements or original template advertisements

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(3)(v) requires designated-Supervisor approval before use or publication of 'original advertisements or original template advertisements.' The new template is the original item requiring approval; the rule's inclusion of 'original template advertisements' addresses exactly this design-once, populate-many pattern. Option A is the closest distractor — it would require re-approval of each routine population of an approved template, which is the inefficiency the 'original template' wording avoids (branch-populated copies remain reviewable under the dealer's 3602(4) methods). Option C is wrong because listed items cannot be left to post-use review. Option B mischaracterizes digital advertising as outside the rule.
questions.source_quote: "original advertisements or original template advertisements, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5872

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Fontaine Securities prepares a one-page leave-behind for prospect meetings showing the five-year performance summary of her model portfolios. She argues it is 'just a meeting handout, not an advertisement.' Under IDPC 3602(3), the handout:

options.A: Requires approval by a designated Supervisor before use, because it is material containing performance reports or summaries that is used to solicit clients
options.B: Requires no review because it is given only to people she meets in person
options.C: May be used immediately and sampled afterwards under the firm's post-use program
options.D: Requires approval only if the performance shown is negative

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(3)(vi) requires designated-Supervisor approval before use or publication of 'any material containing performance reports or summaries that is used to solicit clients' — a prospect leave-behind with model-portfolio performance is squarely that. The label the Representative gives the document is irrelevant; the trigger is the performance content plus the solicitation use. Option C is the closest distractor: post-use sampling under 3602(4) is reserved for material not listed in 3602(3). Option B confuses distribution channel with regulatory character. Option D invents a sign-of-returns condition.
questions.source_quote: "any material containing performance reports or summaries that is used to solicit clients." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5873

questions.stem: The fixed-income desk at Bouchard & Associates wants to email all retail clients a weekly 'Bond Market Pulse' letter commenting on rate moves and recommending sector positioning. The desk head proposes sending each issue immediately and having compliance read it the next morning. What must the Supervisor insist on under IDPC 3602(3)?

options.A: The proposal is fine because the letter goes only to existing clients
options.B: Each issue is a market letter and must be approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication
options.C: Only the first issue needs approval; subsequent issues are automatically covered
options.D: The letter needs no approval if it is shorter than 500 words

questions.rationale: Market letters appear in the 3602(3) pre-approval list, so a designated Supervisor must approve each issue before use or publication — next-morning review is post-use review, which 3602(4) reserves for material not listed in 3602(3). Option C is the closest distractor: unlike an approved original template that branches later populate, each weekly market letter contains new substantive commentary and recommendations, so the pre-use approval obligation attaches to the material actually being published. Option A is irrelevant — the rule does not exempt client-only distribution. Option D invents a length threshold.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must ensure that the following items are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication: (i) research reports, (ii) market letters," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5866-5869

questions.stem: At Stellan Securities, the head of marketing — who is not an Approved Person in any supervisory capacity — signs off on a new original print advertisement, and it runs in a weekend paper. The firm's designated Supervisor for advertising sees it on Monday and finds it compliant in content. What is the supervisory failure?

options.A: None — the content was compliant, so the process is irrelevant
options.B: The advertisement should have been filed with the provincial securities commission
options.C: The approval must come from a designated Supervisor before use or publication; sign-off by a non-designated marketing head followed by Monday review does not satisfy 3602(3)
options.D: The only failure is that the advertisement ran on a weekend

questions.rationale: Subsection 3602(3) requires that listed items, including original advertisements, be 'approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication.' Two elements failed here: the approver was not a designated Supervisor (the IDPC definition of designated Supervisor includes a Supervisor responsible for the pre-approval of advertising, sales literature and correspondence under Part A of Rule 3600), and the review occurred after publication. Option A is the trap — compliant content does not cure the absent pre-use approval; the rule regulates process as well as content. Options B and D invent a filing requirement and a timing taboo that do not exist.
questions.source_quote: “the pre-approval of advertising, sales literature and correspondence under Part A of Rule 3600, and” — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 329-330

questions.stem: An equity analyst at Calloway Securities finishes a 30-page initiation-of-coverage report at 6 p.m. and wants it emailed to clients before the market opens, ahead of the designated research Supervisor's morning review. The analyst notes the report 'only repeats consensus views.' May the report go out overnight?

options.A: Yes, because initiation reports reflecting consensus carry no price impact
options.B: No — research reports must be approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication, with no exception for consensus views
options.C: Yes, if the head of investment banking signs off instead
options.D: Yes, provided the disclosures page is attached

questions.rationale: Research reports are the first item in the 3602(3) list of materials a Dealer Member must ensure are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication; the dealer must also designate one or more Supervisors responsible for reviewing and approving research reports under 3607(2). There is no consensus-view exception. Option C is doubly wrong — beyond not being the designated Supervisor, the investment banking department is expressly prohibited from approving research reports under 3621(2)(i). Options A and D substitute price-impact and disclosure-attachment tests the rule does not recognize for the approval requirement.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must designate one or more Supervisors to be responsible for reviewing and approving research reports." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5915-5916

questions.stem: Ferland Capital's wholesaling team prepares a glossy piece promoting an in-house investment fund to prospective purchasers, featuring the fund's since-inception return summary. The team lead says fund pieces 'follow fund rules, not dealer rules.' Within the IDPC framework, before this piece is used the dealer must ensure:

options.A: Nothing — investment fund material is governed exclusively by the fund manager
options.B: A designated Supervisor approves it before use, since it is material containing performance reports or summaries used to solicit clients, and dealer communications must also comply with Corporation requirements and any applicable laws
options.C: Only that the piece is reviewed within 10 business days after first use
options.D: Only that the fund's auditor confirms the return calculation

questions.rationale: Investment fund practices are an express syllabus parameter under outcome 8.2, and on the rule text this piece is caught by 3602(3)(vi) as material containing performance reports or summaries used to solicit clients, requiring designated-Supervisor approval before use. Clause 3602(1)(vii) further prohibits material that fails to comply with Corporation requirements or any applicable laws — securities-law sales communication rules apply in addition to, not instead of, the dealer's obligations. Option C describes post-use review, unavailable for listed items. Options A and D wrongly outsource the dealer's own approval obligation to the fund manager or auditor.
questions.source_quote: "fails to comply with Corporation requirements or any applicable laws." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5863

questions.stem: The designated Supervisor for advertising at Coteau Securities is reviewing a proposed seminar invitation flyer. The flyer promotes a 'Retirement Income Masterclass' hosted by a Registered Representative and includes a table of the Representative's model portfolio returns since 2020, with a sign-up form for prospective clients. How many independent grounds in IDPC 3602(3) require pre-use approval of this flyer?

options.A: None — invitations are exempt correspondence
options.B: One — only because it mentions retirement products
options.C: Three — every flyer triggers three approvals
options.D: Two — it is material containing performance reports or summaries used to solicit clients, and the promotional seminar text it supports is itself on the pre-approval list

questions.rationale: The flyer is caught by 3602(3)(vi) as material containing performance reports or summaries that is used to solicit clients — the returns table plus the prospect sign-up form satisfy both elements — and the promotional seminar it advertises engages 3602(3)(iv), under which promotional seminar texts (excluding educational seminar texts) must be approved before use. Option A wrongly treats solicitation material as exempt correspondence. Option B substitutes a product-category trigger that does not exist. Option C garbles the analysis — the rule requires approval of listed items before use, not a fixed number of approvals per document.
questions.source_quote: "(iv)     promotional seminar texts (excluding educational seminar texts), (v)      original advertisements or original template advertisements, and (vi)     any material containing performance reports or summaries that is used to solicit clients." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5871-5873

questions.stem: Crestbrook Securities will issue a research report on Tundra Minerals Corp. on April 6. As of March 31, Crestbrook and its affiliates beneficially owned 1.2% of Tundra's common shares. Under IDPC 3608(2)(i), which measurement date governs whether the ownership disclosure threshold is met, and is disclosure required?

options.A: The end of the second most recent month (February 28), because the April 6 issuance is less than 10 days after the end of the prior month
options.B: March 31, the end of the month prior to issuance; since 10 days have not yet elapsed in April that prior month-end cannot be used
options.C: The issuance date itself (April 6); no disclosure since intraday positions fluctuate
options.D: No measurement is needed because the threshold is 5%

questions.rationale: Clause 3608(2)(i) measures the 1% beneficial ownership as of the end of the month prior to the issuance date, or — where the report issuance date is less than 10 days after the end of the prior month — as of the end of the second most recent month. April 6 is less than 10 days after March 31, so the operative date is the end of February; the dealer must determine its February month-end position and disclose if it was 1% or more. Option B states the general rule but misapplies the sub-10-day branch, which exists precisely because month-end data may not yet be compiled. Option C uses a date the rule does not recognize. Option D misstates the threshold, which is one percent or more of any class of the issuer's equity securities.
questions.source_quote: "as of the end of the second most recent month if the report issuance date is less than 10 days after the end of the prior month," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5932-5933

questions.stem: An analyst at Marigold Capital is finalizing a report on Boreal Foods Inc. The analyst owns no Boreal securities, but her spouse holds Boreal call options in his own account, and the editorial assistant who fact-checked the report is short Boreal shares. Under IDPC 3608(2)(ii), what must the report disclose?

options.A: Nothing, because the analyst herself holds no Boreal securities
options.B: Only the assistant's short position, because options are not securities
options.C: Only the spouse's position, because assistants are not Approved Persons
options.D: Both — the holdings of an associate of the analyst and of any person directly involved in the preparation of the report, where they hold or are short any of the issuer's securities directly or indirectly

questions.rationale: Clause 3608(2)(ii) requires disclosure if the analyst, an associate of the analyst, or any person directly involved in the preparation of the report holds or is short any of the issuer's securities directly or indirectly. The spouse is an associate; the fact-checking assistant is a person directly involved in the preparation of the report; both positions are caught (the 'directly or indirectly' language reaches the spouse's account, and the syllabus parameter expressly extends to the issuer's securities or derivatives). Option A stops at the analyst personally, ignoring the two further categories. Options B and C each capture only one limb and add false premises about options and Approved Person status — the clause turns on involvement in preparation, not registration category.
questions.source_quote: "(b)     an associate of the analyst, or (c)     any person directly involved in the preparation of the report, holds or is short any of the issuer’s securities directly or indirectly," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5936-5938

questions.stem: Nine months before Aldercrest Securities issues a research report on Pinnacle Logistics, a Director of Aldercrest personally provided paid strategic-restructuring consulting to Pinnacle. The firm's normal-course trade execution desk also executed routine trades for Pinnacle's treasury throughout the year. Under IDPC 3608(2)(iii), what must the research report disclose?

options.A: The Director's paid consulting services, because services provided for remuneration during the preceding 12 months are disclosable, other than services provided in the normal course investment advisory or trade execution services
options.B: Both the consulting and the routine trade execution services
options.C: Neither, because the Director was not the analyst
options.D: Only services provided within the preceding 6 months

questions.rationale: Clause 3608(2)(iii) requires disclosure of any services provided by any partner, Director or officer of the Dealer Member or analyst involved in the preparation of a report — other than normal course investment advisory or trade execution services — to the issuer for remuneration during the 12 months immediately preceding the report. The restructuring consulting at nine months is disclosable; routine trade execution falls within the express carve-out, so option B over-discloses. Option C misreads the clause, which reaches partners, Directors and officers of the dealer, not only the analyst. Option D shortens the 12-month look-back.
questions.source_quote: "other than services provided in the normal course investment advisory or trade execution services to the issuer for remuneration, during the 12 months immediately preceding the date a research report or recommendation was issued," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5940-5943

questions.stem: Hollis & Mercer Securities is publishing research on Cobalt Ridge Resources. The dealer's Chief Operating Officer sits on Cobalt Ridge's board of directors. Applying IDPC 3608(2)(v), what does the research report have to include about this relationship?

options.A: A general statement that 'conflicts may exist' without identifying anyone
options.B: Nothing, because board service is a personal matter
options.C: Nothing, provided the COO recuses herself from the research department
options.D: The name of the partner, Director, officer, employee or agent of the dealer who is a partner, director, officer or employee of the issuer

questions.rationale: Clause 3608(2)(v) requires the research report to disclose the name of any partner, Director, officer, employee or agent of the Dealer Member who is a partner, director, officer or employee of the issuer, or who serves in an equivalent advisory capacity to the issuer — the COO's board seat at the covered issuer (a position of influence) must be disclosed by name. Option A is the closest distractor: 3610 requires disclosures to be clear, meaningful, comprehensive and prominent, and prohibits standard statements where specific, customized disclosure is more appropriate — an anonymous boilerplate fails that standard and the express naming requirement. Options B and C treat disclosure as avoidable through characterization or recusal; the clause mandates disclosure of the relationship itself.
questions.source_quote: "the name of any partner, Director, officer, employee or agent of the Dealer Member who is a partner, director, officer or employee of the issuer, or who serves in an equivalent advisory capacity to the issuer, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5947-5949

questions.stem: Trillium Gate Securities maintains continuous two-sided quotes in the convertible debentures of Saxon Apparel, the subject of an upcoming research report. The research Supervisor asks whether the market-making activity must be disclosed in the report. Under IDPC 3608(2)(vi):

options.A: No, because debentures are debt rather than equity
options.B: Yes — the report must disclose if the dealer is making a market in any equity security or equity related security of the subject issuer, and convertible securities are within 'equity related security'
options.C: No, because market making is disclosed only to CIRO, not to readers
options.D: Yes, but only if the market-making desk is profitable in the security

questions.rationale: Clause 3608(2)(vi) requires disclosure if the dealer is making a market in any equity security or equity related security of the subject issuer, and the 3606 definition of 'equity related security' expressly includes derivatives, convertible securities and income trust units — convertible debentures qualify. Option A is the closest distractor because it relies on the formal debt classification while the definition pulls convertibles in by their performance link to the underlying equity. Option C misdescribes the disclosure as regulatory-facing when 3608 disclosures appear in the research report itself. Option D adds a profitability condition the rule does not contain.
questions.source_quote: "A security whose performance is based on the performance of an underlying equity security or a basket of income producing assets, including derivatives, convertible securities and income trust units." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5901-5903

questions.stem: Argent Bay Securities rates covered stocks 'Accumulate,' 'Hold' or 'Reduce.' A client complains that the firm's research never explains what 'Accumulate' means relative to the other ratings, and the firm has never published how its recommendations are distributed. Under IDPC 3609, identify the dealer's obligations.

options.A: Ratings systems are proprietary and need not be explained to readers
options.B: The firm must abandon word ratings and adopt CIRO's standardized buy/sell scale
options.C: Each report must disclose (or indicate where the reader can find) the dealer's system for rating investment opportunities and how each recommendation fits within it, and the dealer must disclose quarterly the percentage of its recommendations in each category
options.D: Only the quarterly percentage disclosure is required; explaining the rating scale is optional

questions.rationale: Subsection 3609(1)(i) requires a research report to disclose, or indicate where it is otherwise available, the dealer's system for rating investment opportunities and how each recommendation fits within the system; 3609(2) separately requires the dealer to disclose, on a quarterly basis, the percentage of its recommendations that fall into each category of its recommendation system. Option D is the closest distractor — it keeps the quarterly piece but drops the per-report rating-system disclosure, which is equally mandatory. Option B is wrong because CIRO prescribes disclosure of the dealer's own system, not a standardized scale. Option A treats the system as confidential when the rule makes it disclosable.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must, on a quarterly basis, disclose the percentage of its recommendations that fall into each category of its recommendation system." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5958-5959

questions.stem: Compliance at Driftwood Securities proposes inserting one identical two-line 'conflicts may exist from time to time' paragraph into every research report, regardless of the issuer or the dealer's actual relationships, to simplify production. How does IDPC 3610 treat this approach?

options.A: It is acceptable because uniform language reduces error risk
options.B: It fails — disclosures required by 3608 and 3609 must be made in a clear, meaningful, comprehensive and prominent manner, and the dealer must not use standard disclosure statements when it is more appropriate to use specific information and customized disclosures
options.C: It is acceptable if the paragraph appears on the front page
options.D: It fails only for reports covering more than six issuers

questions.rationale: Subsection 3610(1) requires that the research report disclosures required in sections 3608 and 3609 be made in a clear, meaningful, comprehensive and prominent manner, and 3610(2) prohibits using standard disclosure statements when it is more appropriate to use specific information and customized disclosures. A boilerplate paragraph that ignores the dealer's actual relationships defeats both. Option C is the closest distractor — prominence alone cannot rescue a statement that is not meaningful or comprehensive about real conflicts. Option D garbles 3612, which is about where disclosures may be located for multi-issuer reports, not a boilerplate license. Option A elevates convenience over the express prohibition.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must not use standard disclosure statements when it is more appropriate to use specific information and customized disclosures" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5967-5968

questions.stem: Lighthouse Securities distributes, under the third party's own name, equity research prepared by an independent U.S. firm that is a FINRA member. Lighthouse discloses to clients that the reports were not prepared in accordance with Canadian disclosure requirements relating to research reports. Are the IDPC 3608/3609 disclosures required in these reports?

options.A: Yes — distribution under any name always imports the full 3608 and 3609 disclosure obligations with no exceptions
options.B: No, because third-party research is entirely outside Part B of Rule 3600
options.C: No — the 3608/3609 disclosures are not required for independent third party reports issued by FINRA members, where the dealer discloses the reports were not prepared in accordance with Canadian research disclosure requirements
options.D: Yes, but only the rating-system disclosure, not the conflict disclosures
