questions.rationale: Subsection 3611(1) generally applies the 3608/3609 disclosures to independent third party research a dealer distributes under the third party's name, but 3611(2) removes the requirement where the reports are issued by members of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (or persons governed by other regulators approved by the Corporation), or where the dealer is only giving access or supplying at a client's request — and the dealer discloses that the report was not prepared in accordance with Canadian disclosure requirements relating to research reports. Lighthouse fits the FINRA branch plus the required disclosure. Option A states the general rule while ignoring the express exceptions. Option B overshoots — 3611(1) shows third-party research is within the regime by default. Option D splits the disclosures in a way the rule does not.
questions.source_quote: "in the case of independent third party research reports that are issued by members of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority or persons governed by other regulators approved by the Corporation, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5975-5977

questions.stem: Owing to an analyst departure, Halton Ridge Securities will stop covering four issuers it has followed for years via emailed reports to its retail research subscribers. For one of the four, coverage is being suspended solely because the issuer was just placed on Halton Ridge's restricted list. Applying IDPC 3615, what notice is required?

options.A: No notice for any of the issuers, since dropping coverage is a business decision
options.B: Notice to CIRO only, within 10 days of the decision
options.C: Notice of intention to suspend or discontinue coverage for the three ordinary discontinuations, issued to the same audience who received the coverage and in the same manner it was distributed; no notice is required for the issuer suspended solely because it was placed on the restricted list
options.D: Identical public press releases for all four issuers

questions.rationale: Subsection 3615(1) requires the dealer to issue notice of its intention to suspend or discontinue coverage to the same audience who received the coverage and in the same manner the coverage was distributed — here, emailed to the research subscribers. Subsection 3615(2) provides that notice is not required if the sole reason for the suspension is that the issuer has been placed on the dealer's restricted list, which fits the fourth issuer (announcing it could itself signal the restricted-list addition). Option D is the closest distractor — it forces public press releases where the rule requires same-audience/same-manner notice, and ignores the restricted-list exception. Options A and B deny or misdirect the client-facing notice obligation.
questions.source_quote: "Notice of discontinuance of coverage is not required if the sole reason for the suspension is that the issuer has been placed on a Dealer Member’s restricted list." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6010-6011

questions.stem: The analyst writing Copperline Securities' report on Atlas Freight received a bonus eight months ago that was calculated in part from Copperline's overall investment banking revenues. Under IDPC 3620(1), how does this affect the Atlas Freight report?

options.A: The report must disclose that the analyst responsible for the report received compensation within the prior 12 months that was based upon the dealer's investment banking revenues
options.B: The bonus is irrelevant unless it derived specifically from an Atlas Freight mandate
options.C: The analyst must repay the bonus before publishing
options.D: The report must be reassigned to a different analyst

questions.rationale: Subsection 3620(1) requires a research report to disclose if the analyst responsible for the report received compensation within the prior 12 months that was based upon the Dealer Member's investment banking revenues — the eight-month-old bonus is within the look-back and the revenue link triggers disclosure even though it is not issuer-specific. Option B is the closest distractor: it confuses this disclosure with the separate 3620(2) prohibition, under which the dealer must not pay an analyst any bonus, salary or other compensation directly based upon a specific investment banking transaction. Options C and D invent repayment and reassignment remedies; the rule's response to general banking-revenue-based compensation is disclosure.
questions.source_quote: "A research report must disclose if the analyst responsible for the report received compensation within the prior 12 months that was based upon the Dealer Member’s investment banking revenues." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6053-6055

questions.stem: A client asks Westgate Securities how she can learn the firm's rules for who gets its research and when. Under IDPC 3609(1), what must each Westgate research report do with respect to dissemination?

options.A: Attach the complete compliance manual as an appendix
options.B: Provide each client with a personalized distribution schedule
options.C: Nothing — dissemination practices are internal and confidential
options.D: Disclose, or indicate where the information is otherwise available, the dealer's policies and procedures that specifically address the dissemination of its research reports

questions.rationale: Clause 3609(1)(ii) requires a research report to disclose, or indicate where the following information is otherwise available, the dealer's policies and procedures that specifically address the dissemination of its research reports — the syllabus lists 'Policies and procedures for dissemination of research' as the first 8.3 disclosure parameter. Option C is the closest distractor because firms often regard distribution mechanics as internal, but the rule makes the dissemination P&Ps a disclosure item. Option A confuses disclosing (or pointing to) the dissemination policies with publishing the entire manual, and option B invents a personalized schedule the rule does not require.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member’s policies and procedures that specifically address the dissemination of its research reports." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5956-5957

questions.stem: Three research situations arise at Kessler Securities: (1) analyst Mira Voss is drafting on Trent Pharma, where her brother — her associate — is Trent's Vice-President of Finance; (2) analyst Dale Chu is drafting on Orix Tools, to which the designated research Supervisor provides paid advisory services; (3) analyst Lena Brandt is drafting on Calder Mills, whose CFO is her former university classmate with no ongoing connection. Which reports may Kessler issue under IDPC 3614?

options.A: All three, with appropriate conflict disclosure in each report
options.B: Only the Trent Pharma report, because family relationships are exempt
options.C: Only the Calder Mills report — the dealer must not issue a report prepared by an analyst on any issuer for which the analyst, an associate of the analyst or the designated Supervisor serves as an officer, director or employee, or in any advisory capacity
options.D: None of the three reports

questions.rationale: Section 3614 prohibits issuing a research report prepared by an analyst on any issuer for which the analyst, an associate of the analyst, or the designated Supervisor serves as an officer, director or employee of the issuer, or in any advisory capacity to the issuer. Situation 1 is barred (associate is an officer); situation 2 is barred (designated Supervisor serves in an advisory capacity); situation 3 involves a past social connection that creates no officer/director/employee/advisory role, so it is not caught by 3614. Option A is the central trap — 3614 is a prohibition on issuing, not a disclosure obligation that drafting around 3608 could satisfy. Options B and D respectively invent a family exemption and over-extend the prohibition to non-covered relationships.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must not issue a research report prepared by an analyst on any issuer for which the analyst, an associate of the analyst or the designated Supervisor: (i) serves as an officer, director or employee of the issuer, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5996-5998

questions.stem: Provost Securities acted as co-manager of Marin Hydro's initial public offering of equity securities, which priced on May 1. The research department wants to launch coverage with a favourable report on May 7. Separately, the syndicate desk notes the firm was only a junior co-manager. Under IDPC 3622, may the report be issued on May 7?

options.A: Yes, because quiet periods bind only the lead manager, not co-managers
options.B: No — the quiet period for an IPO is 40 days for all syndicate members
options.C: Yes, if the report carries enhanced conflict disclosure
options.D: No — having acted as manager or co-manager, the dealer must not issue a research report on the issuer's equity securities for 10 days after the date of the offering of an initial public offering

questions.rationale: Subsection 3622(1) prohibits a dealer that has acted as manager or co-manager from issuing a research report on equity securities of the subject issuer for 10 days after the date of the offering of an initial public offering — May 7 is within 10 days of May 1, and the rule expressly captures co-managers, so junior syndicate status does not help. Option A misreads the express 'manager or co-manager' language. Option C wrongly converts a timing prohibition into a disclosure cure. Option B invents a 40-day period; the rule's periods are 10 days for an IPO and three days for a secondary offering.
questions.source_quote: "for 10 days after the date of the offering of an initial public offering of equity securities of the subject issuer," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6074-6075

questions.stem: Birchmount Securities is pitching for the financing mandate of Solara Grid. To win the business, the head of equity research proposes telling Solara that if Birchmount is hired, the firm will hold its current 'Hold' rating unchanged for six months instead of moving to the planned downgrade. Analyze this proposal under IDPC 3617.

options.A: It is acceptable because no favourable rating is being promised, only the status quo
options.B: It is acceptable if the research department documents independent reasons for the delay
options.C: It is prohibited only if Solara initiates the request
options.D: It is prohibited — as consideration or inducement for the receipt of business from an issuer, the dealer must not offer to delay the changing of a rating or price target or any other research report element

questions.rationale: Clause 3617(1)(iii) prohibits a dealer, as consideration or inducement for the receipt of business or compensation from an issuer, from offering to delay the changing of a rating or price target on the issuer's securities or the changing of any other research report element, including offering to delay the issue date of the research report. Option A is the central trap — the prohibition covers delaying changes, not merely promising favourable ratings (which clause (ii) addresses separately), so freezing a planned downgrade to win a mandate is squarely caught. Option B cannot launder an offer made as an inducement, and option C is wrong because the clause prohibits the dealer's offer regardless of who first raises it; clause (iv) likewise bars threats to change a rating.
questions.source_quote: "offer to delay the changing of a rating or price target on one or more of the issuer’s securities or the changing of any other research report element, including offering to delay the issue date of the research report, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6020-6022

questions.stem: Analyst Rohan Patel of Glenmore Securities accepts a paid weekend engagement preparing investor materials for a private mining company, without telling anyone at Glenmore, reasoning that the company is private and outside his coverage. When the arrangement surfaces, what rule has been breached and why does it matter for research supervision?

options.A: A breach of IDPC 3623, which requires the dealer to pre-approve an analyst's outside activities; unvetted engagements can create relationships and conflicts the research supervision framework is designed to detect
options.B: No breach — outside activities involving private companies are unregulated
options.C: A breach only if the mining company later goes public
options.D: A breach of the quiet period rules in 3622

questions.rationale: Section 3623(1) requires a Dealer Member to pre-approve an analyst's outside activities — the obligation attaches before the activity begins and is not limited to public issuers or covered names, because undisclosed engagements are precisely how issuer relationships, compensation conflicts and advisory roles (which can trigger the 3614 issuing prohibition and 3608 disclosures) escape the supervision framework; the syllabus pairs analyst conduct with personal trading and outside activities. Option C is the closest distractor — a later IPO might intensify the conflict, but the breach is the absence of pre-approval, complete at the outset. Option B is contradicted by the rule's unqualified text, and option D cites an unrelated provision about post-offering report timing.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must pre-approve an analyst’s outside activities." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6085

questions.stem: At Stratton Hill Securities, an institutional sales trader notices an unusual cluster of buying in Voltan Circuits by an employee account two days before the research department publishes a Voltan upgrade that moves the stock 8%. Compliance finds the firm has no process to look for this pattern. Which IDPC 3619 requirement has the dealer failed to implement?

options.A: The requirement to publish all upgrades after market close
options.B: The requirement that all employee accounts be held at another dealer
options.C: The requirement to have policies and procedures that specifically address detecting and restricting trading in the subject issuer's securities done with knowledge of or in anticipation of the issuance of a research report, a new recommendation, or a change in a recommendation that could reasonably be expected to affect the price
options.D: The requirement to delay all employee trades by 48 hours

questions.rationale: Subsection 3619(1) requires a dealer who issues or distributes research reports to have policies and procedures that specifically address detecting and restricting any trading in equity securities or equity related securities of a subject issuer done with knowledge of or in anticipation of the issuance of a research report, a new recommendation, or a change in a recommendation that could reasonably be expected to have an effect on the price of the subject securities. Having no detection process at all is a direct failure of this requirement — the syllabus frames it as policies and procedures on detecting and restricting trades. Options A, B and D invent mechanical controls (after-hours publishing, external custody, fixed delays) that the rule does not mandate; the obligation is a detection-and-restriction framework, which is why its complete absence, not a missing gadget, is the breach.
questions.source_quote: "policies and procedures that specifically address detecting and restricting any trading in equity securities or equity related securities of a subject issuer that is done with knowledge of or in anticipation of:" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6030-6032

questions.stem: Hollis Lake Securities' research policies state that the firm's P&Ps cover 'the publishing of research reports' but are silent on how analysts behave day-to-day and on how recommendations get made. A CIRO examination flags the policies as incomplete under IDPC 3607(1). What must the policies specifically address?

options.A: Only the publishing of research reports, which the firm already covers
options.B: The conduct of analysts, the publishing of research reports, and the making of recommendations by analysts
options.C: Only the compensation arrangements of analysts
options.D: Only the marketing distribution lists for research products

questions.rationale: Subsection 3607(1) requires a Dealer Member's policies and procedures to specifically address three things: the conduct of analysts, the publishing of research reports, and the making of recommendations by analysts. Covering only publication leaves two mandatory subjects unaddressed — which matters because analyst conduct (including personal trading under 3619 and outside activities under 3623) and recommendation-making (including independence from banking under 3621) are where the supervision risks concentrate. Option A repeats the firm's incomplete position. Options C and D each name a topic that may belong inside the framework but substitute it for the three subjects the rule enumerates.
questions.source_quote: "(i)     the conduct of analysts, (ii)    the publishing of research reports, and (iii)   the making of recommendations by analysts." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5912-5914

questions.stem: Confidential drafts of a pending rating change at Beauport Securities keep reaching favoured institutional clients hours before publication, giving them a trading head start. The research Supervisor is asked to identify the control failure within the Element 8 framework. The failure is best described as:

options.A: Inadequate controls regarding advance dissemination of any part of a research report prior to publication, undermining the trading detection-and-restriction policies required by 3619(1)
options.B: A breach of the 3613 site-visit disclosure rule
options.C: A failure to publish the quarterly rating-distribution percentages
options.D: A failure to obtain CIRO approval of the rating methodology

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for outcome 8.4 requires controls regarding advance dissemination of any part of a research report prior to publication, and the rule anchor is 3619(1), which requires policies and procedures specifically addressing detecting and restricting trading done with knowledge of or in anticipation of the issuance of a research report, a new recommendation or a change in a recommendation that could reasonably be expected to affect the price — selective pre-release is the canonical way such anticipatory trading happens. Option C is the closest rule-based distractor but the 3609(2) quarterly distribution disclosure has nothing to do with leak control. Options B and D point to site-visit disclosure and an invented CIRO methodology approval, neither of which addresses pre-publication leakage.
questions.source_quote:
"Controls regarding advance dissemination of any part of a research
report prior to publication" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 1547-1548

questions.stem: Corporate finance at Selwyn Securities wants to bring analyst Ines Faria 'over the wall' to assist confidentially on a take-private bid for an issuer she covers. According to the syllabus parameters for outcome 8.4, which combination of controls applies to wall-crossing an analyst?

options.A: No controls — wall-crossing is prohibited in all circumstances
options.B: Disclosure of the wall-crossing in the analyst's next published report on the issuer
options.C: Only a verbal reminder of confidentiality from the deal captain
options.D: Policies and procedures on detecting and restricting trades, restrictions on relevant individuals, and approval by designated Executive

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 8.4 lists 'Controls for wall-crossing an Analyst' with three sub-elements: policies and procedures on detecting and restricting trades, restrictions on relevant individuals, and approval by designated Executive. This maps onto the rule fabric — 3619(1) requires the trade detection-and-restriction P&Ps, 3619(2) restricts individuals involved in research, and 3619(3) reserves trade approvals to a designated Executive. Option A overstates: the framework is one of controls, not blanket prohibition. Option C is plainly inadequate against the controls the syllabus enumerates, and option B would itself be a problem — publicly disclosing the analyst's involvement in a confidential bid would compromise the very information the wall protects.
questions.source_quote:
"Controls for wall-crossing an Analyst
- Policies and procedures on detecting and restricting trades
- Restrictions on relevant individuals
- Approval by designated Executive" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 1596-1599

questions.stem: Compliance at Mont-Tremblant Securities just added Cardinal Robotics to the firm's restricted list because the dealer is advising on Cardinal's pending merger. The research department has a Cardinal report in final edit and plans to suspend coverage quietly. Considering the syllabus 8.4 prohibition parameters together with IDPC 3615(2), which course of action is correct?

options.A: Publish the completed report since the work predates the listing, then suspend
options.B: Do not issue the report while Cardinal is on the restricted list; suspend coverage, and no notice of discontinuance is required where the sole reason for the suspension is that the issuer has been placed on the dealer's restricted list
options.C: Publish the report but delete the rating to neutralize it
options.D: Suspend coverage and send all research clients a notice explaining that Cardinal is now on the restricted list

questions.rationale: The syllabus 8.4 parameters place 'Placed on Restricted List' among the prohibitions on issuing research reports, so the in-edit Cardinal report must not be issued while the restriction stands — work-in-progress status (option A) and rating deletion (option C) do not cure an issuing prohibition. On the notice side, 3615(2) provides that notice of discontinuance of coverage is not required if the sole reason for the suspension is that the issuer has been placed on the dealer's restricted list. Option D is the closest distractor: it correctly suspends but then broadcasts the restricted-list placement, signalling the confidential mandate the restriction protects — the very outcome the 3615(2) exception avoids.
questions.source_quote: "Notice of discontinuance of coverage is not required if the sole reason for the suspension is that the issuer has been placed on a Dealer Member’s restricted list." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6010-6011

questions.scenario: Harrowgate Securities, an Investment Dealer headquartered in Calgary, publishes equity research through a six-analyst team. Analyst Camille Roy covers Dunmore Lithium Corp. Three facts are on file as her new Dunmore report heads to the designated research Supervisor, Olivier Banks, for approval: (1) Harrowgate's affiliate held 2.3% of Dunmore's common shares at the most recent measurable month-end under section 3608; (2) five months ago Harrowgate acted as placement agent on Dunmore's private placement, earning a fee; and (3) Camille toured Dunmore's pilot plant in Chile last quarter, with Dunmore reimbursing her airfare. The draft report sets a $19 price target. Separately, Camille's research associate, Devon Park, asks to sell his small personal position in Dunmore shares one week before the planned publication date, and the head of investment banking emails Olivier asking to 'see the Dunmore piece before it goes out, in case the tone needs adjusting.'
questions.stem: Which conflict-of-interest disclosures must Camille's Dunmore report contain in respect of facts (1) and (2)?

options.A: Neither fact — affiliate holdings and placement-agent work are not disclosable matters
options.B: Only fact (1), because placement-agent fees are not investment banking remuneration
options.C: Both — the affiliate's beneficial ownership of one percent or more of a class of Dunmore's equity securities, and the investment banking services (serving as a placement agent is within the 3606 definition) provided for remuneration within the preceding 12 months
options.D: Only fact (2), because ownership disclosure starts at 5%

questions.rationale: Clause 3608(2)(i) requires disclosure where the Dealer Member or its affiliates has beneficial ownership of one percent or more of any class of the issuer's equity securities, measured at the prescribed month-end — 2.3% qualifies. Clause 3608(2)(iv) requires disclosure of investment banking services provided to the issuer for remuneration during the preceding 12 months, and the 3606 definition of investment banking expressly includes serving as a placement agent for an issuer, so the five-month-old mandate is disclosable. Option B's premise fails on the definition; option D misstates the ownership threshold; option A denies both express disclosure items.
questions.source_quote: "(iii) providing venture capital, lines of credit or serving as a placement agent for an issuer." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5907-5908

questions.scenario: Harrowgate Securities, an Investment Dealer headquartered in Calgary, publishes equity research through a six-analyst team. Analyst Camille Roy covers Dunmore Lithium Corp. Three facts are on file as her new Dunmore report heads to the designated research Supervisor, Olivier Banks, for approval: (1) Harrowgate's affiliate held 2.3% of Dunmore's common shares at the most recent measurable month-end under section 3608; (2) five months ago Harrowgate acted as placement agent on Dunmore's private placement, earning a fee; and (3) Camille toured Dunmore's pilot plant in Chile last quarter, with Dunmore reimbursing her airfare. The draft report sets a $19 price target. Separately, Camille's research associate, Devon Park, asks to sell his small personal position in Dunmore shares one week before the planned publication date, and the head of investment banking emails Olivier asking to 'see the Dunmore piece before it goes out, in case the tone needs adjusting.'
questions.stem: Regarding fact (3) — the Chile plant tour — and the $19 price target, what must the Dunmore report disclose?

options.A: Whether, and to what extent, Camille visited the issuer's material operations; that Dunmore reimbursed her travel expenses with respect to the visit; and the valuation method used to set the price target
options.B: Only the price-target valuation method; site visits are not disclosable
options.C: Only that a visit occurred; reimbursement and valuation method are internal matters
options.D: Nothing, because the visit and target both predate publication

questions.rationale: Section 3613 requires the dealer to disclose in its research reports whether, and to what extent, an analyst has visited the issuer's material operations, and if the issuer has paid or reimbursed any of the analyst's travel expenses with respect to the visit — both apply to the reimbursed Chile tour. Section 3616 separately requires that where a price target is set, the report disclose the valuation method used. Option C is the closest distractor, keeping the visit but suppressing the reimbursement and valuation-method limbs, both of which are express. Option B inverts 3613, and option D's timing argument is meaningless — the disclosures exist precisely because these events precede publication.
questions.source_quote: "whether, and to what extent, an analyst has visited the issuer’s material operations, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5992

questions.scenario: Harrowgate Securities, an Investment Dealer headquartered in Calgary, publishes equity research through a six-analyst team. Analyst Camille Roy covers Dunmore Lithium Corp. Three facts are on file as her new Dunmore report heads to the designated research Supervisor, Olivier Banks, for approval: (1) Harrowgate's affiliate held 2.3% of Dunmore's common shares at the most recent measurable month-end under section 3608; (2) five months ago Harrowgate acted as placement agent on Dunmore's private placement, earning a fee; and (3) Camille toured Dunmore's pilot plant in Chile last quarter, with Dunmore reimbursing her airfare. The draft report sets a $19 price target. Separately, Camille's research associate, Devon Park, asks to sell his small personal position in Dunmore shares one week before the planned publication date, and the head of investment banking emails Olivier asking to 'see the Dunmore piece before it goes out, in case the tone needs adjusting.'
questions.stem: Devon Park, who is directly involved in preparing the Dunmore report, wants to sell his Dunmore shares one week before publication. Analyze the request under IDPC 3619.

options.A: The sale falls within the period beginning 30 days prior to and ending five days after issuance, so it is prohibited unless Devon obtains the prior written approval of a designated Executive — and if the report carries a positive recommendation, approval of a contrary sale may not be granted unless special circumstances exist
options.B: Devon may sell freely because the position is small and he is not the named analyst
options.C: Devon may sell if he waits until the morning of publication
options.D: Devon may sell with Camille's verbal consent as lead analyst

questions.rationale: Subsection 3619(2) prohibits an individual directly involved in the preparation or approval of a research report from trading the subject issuer's equity or equity related securities from 30 days before until five days after issuance — one week before publication is inside the window, and position size and job title are irrelevant since the rule turns on direct involvement in preparation. Subsection 3619(3) allows trading only with the prior written approval of a designated Executive, and 3619(4) provides that approval may not be granted for trades contrary to the analyst's current recommendation unless special circumstances exist — a sale against a positive call is contrary. Option C still lands inside the blackout, and options B and D substitute size thresholds and analyst consent for the Executive-approval mechanism.
questions.source_quote: "Approval under subsection 3619(3) may not be granted for trades that are contrary to the analyst’s current recommendation, unless special circumstances exist." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6043-6044

questions.scenario: Harrowgate Securities, an Investment Dealer headquartered in Calgary, publishes equity research through a six-analyst team. Analyst Camille Roy covers Dunmore Lithium Corp. Three facts are on file as her new Dunmore report heads to the designated research Supervisor, Olivier Banks, for approval: (1) Harrowgate's affiliate held 2.3% of Dunmore's common shares at the most recent measurable month-end under section 3608; (2) five months ago Harrowgate acted as placement agent on Dunmore's private placement, earning a fee; and (3) Camille toured Dunmore's pilot plant in Chile last quarter, with Dunmore reimbursing her airfare. The draft report sets a $19 price target. Separately, Camille's research associate, Devon Park, asks to sell his small personal position in Dunmore shares one week before the planned publication date, and the head of investment banking emails Olivier asking to 'see the Dunmore piece before it goes out, in case the tone needs adjusting.'
questions.stem: How should Olivier Banks respond to the head of investment banking's request to review the Dunmore report 'in case the tone needs adjusting' before publication?

options.A: Forward the full draft — internal colleagues may always read pending research
options.B: Forward the draft but ask banking to suggest only stylistic edits
options.C: Refuse — the dealer's policies must prohibit approval of research reports by the investment banking department and limit banking's involvement in the production of research reports solely to the correction of factual errors; 'adjusting the tone' exceeds that limit
options.D: Forward the draft on condition the banker signs a confidentiality undertaking

questions.rationale: Subsection 3621(2) requires policies and procedures that, at minimum, prohibit the approval of research reports by the investment banking department and limit the investment banking department's involvement in the production of research reports solely to the correction of factual errors — tone adjustment is editorial influence, not factual correction, and is precisely the influence 3621(1) requires the dealer to prevent. Clause 3621(2)(iv) further requires systems to control and record the flow of information between analysts and banking staff regarding covered issuers. Option B is the closest distractor: stylistic edits are still beyond factual-error correction. Options A and D treat the restriction as an access or confidentiality issue when it is a substantive independence control.
questions.source_quote: "establishing systems to control and record the flow of information between analysts and investment banking department staff, regarding issuers that are the subject of current or prospective research reports." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6068-6070

questions.scenario: Brightlea Securities operates a retail wealth division in Halifax. During a routine sweep, designated advertising Supervisor Nadia Osei finds four items associated with Registered Representative Marcus Webb: (a) a public social-media profile describing Marcus as 'Senior Vice-President & Award-Winning #1 Advisor — President's Council 2025,' although Brightlea has never appointed him to any corporate office and the President's Council recognizes the firm's top revenue producers; (b) a 'Quarterly Income Letter' that Marcus has been emailing to clients and prospects for a year, containing sector recommendations, sent without anyone's prior approval; (c) a prospect handout showing his model portfolio's annualized returns; and (d) a string of business-related text messages with clients on Marcus's personal phone, none of which appear in Brightlea's archives. Nadia must determine the rule basis for remediating each item and the records the firm must be able to produce.
questions.stem: Item (a): which two clauses of IDPC 3640(2) does Marcus's social-media profile description breach?

options.A: The prohibition on using a corporate officer title without appointment to that corporate office pursuant to applicable corporate law, and the prohibition on using a title, designation, award, or recognition based partly or entirely on sales activity or revenue generation
options.B: The quiet-period rule and the price-target rule
options.C: Only a best-practice guideline; 3640 does not reach social media
options.D: The third-party research exemption and the hyperlink rule

questions.rationale: Two limbs of 3640(2) are engaged: clause (ii) prohibits a client-facing Approved Person from using a corporate officer title unless the dealer has appointed them to that corporate office pursuant to applicable corporate law — Brightlea never appointed Marcus 'Senior Vice-President' — and clause (i) prohibits using a title, designation, award, or recognition based partly or entirely on the Approved Person's sales activity or revenue generation, which captures the revenue-based 'President's Council' and '#1 Advisor' claims. Option C fails because 3640(1) targets any holding out that could reasonably be expected to deceive or mislead, regardless of medium, and the 8.1 syllabus expressly covers social media. Options B and D cite research-report provisions (3622, 3616, 3611, 3612) irrelevant to a profile description.
questions.source_quote: "an Approved Person who interacts with clients must not use any of the following:" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6107-6108

questions.scenario: Brightlea Securities operates a retail wealth division in Halifax. During a routine sweep, designated advertising Supervisor Nadia Osei finds four items associated with Registered Representative Marcus Webb: (a) a public social-media profile describing Marcus as 'Senior Vice-President & Award-Winning #1 Advisor — President's Council 2025,' although Brightlea has never appointed him to any corporate office and the President's Council recognizes the firm's top revenue producers; (b) a 'Quarterly Income Letter' that Marcus has been emailing to clients and prospects for a year, containing sector recommendations, sent without anyone's prior approval; (c) a prospect handout showing his model portfolio's annualized returns; and (d) a string of business-related text messages with clients on Marcus's personal phone, none of which appear in Brightlea's archives. Nadia must determine the rule basis for remediating each item and the records the firm must be able to produce.
questions.stem: Items (b) and (c): applying IDPC 3602(3), what approval treatment did the Quarterly Income Letter and the model-portfolio handout each require before Marcus used them?

options.A: Neither required any approval because Marcus authored them personally
options.B: Both required approval by a designated Supervisor before use or publication — the letter as a market letter and the handout as material containing performance reports or summaries used to solicit clients
options.C: Only the handout, because letters to existing clients are always exempt
options.D: Only the letter, because performance handouts merely need post-use sampling

questions.rationale: The Quarterly Income Letter — recurring market commentary with sector recommendations — is a market letter under 3602(3)(ii), and the prospect handout showing model-portfolio returns is 'material containing performance reports or summaries that is used to solicit clients' under 3602(3)(vi); both are on the list of items the dealer must ensure are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication, so a year of unapproved sending is a sustained breach. Option D is the closest distractor — it correctly catches the letter but routes the handout to post-use sampling, which 3602(4) permits only for materials not listed in 3602(3). Option C invents a client-audience exemption (and the letter also goes to prospects), and option A confuses authorship with exemption.
questions.source_quote: "(ii)    market letters, (iii)   telemarketing scripts, (iv)     promotional seminar texts (excluding educational seminar texts), (v)      original advertisements or original template advertisements, and (vi)     any material containing performance reports or summaries that is used to solicit clients." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5869-5873

questions.scenario: Brightlea Securities operates a retail wealth division in Halifax. During a routine sweep, designated advertising Supervisor Nadia Osei finds four items associated with Registered Representative Marcus Webb: (a) a public social-media profile describing Marcus as 'Senior Vice-President & Award-Winning #1 Advisor — President's Council 2025,' although Brightlea has never appointed him to any corporate office and the President's Council recognizes the firm's top revenue producers; (b) a 'Quarterly Income Letter' that Marcus has been emailing to clients and prospects for a year, containing sector recommendations, sent without anyone's prior approval; (c) a prospect handout showing his model portfolio's annualized returns; and (d) a string of business-related text messages with clients on Marcus's personal phone, none of which appear in Brightlea's archives. Nadia must determine the rule basis for remediating each item and the records the firm must be able to produce.
questions.stem: Nadia learns that Brightlea's research department actually publishes a report on one of the issuers recommended in Marcus's Quarterly Income Letter, and a local radio show now invites Marcus to discuss that issuer's merits on air. If Marcus appears, what does IDPC 3618 require of him?

options.A: Nothing — only analysts are subject to public-comment requirements
options.B: He must obtain the issuer's consent before commenting
options.C: He must read the report's full conflicts appendix on air
options.D: When making any public comment about the merits of the issuer or its securities, he must disclose whether or not Brightlea has issued a relevant research report

questions.rationale: Section 3618 applies when an employee or Approved Person of a Dealer Member gives an interview or otherwise makes any public comment about the merits of an issuer or its securities — the obligation is to disclose whether or not the Dealer Member has issued a relevant research report. Option A is the closest distractor: the section's subject is 'an employee or Approved Person,' not analysts alone, so Marcus is covered. Option C overshoots — the on-air obligation is the issued-report disclosure, not a recital of the written report's conflicts package. Option B invents issuer consent, which has no place in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "When giving an interview or otherwise making any public comment about the merits of an issuer or its securities," — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6026-6027

questions.scenario: Brightlea Securities operates a retail wealth division in Halifax. During a routine sweep, designated advertising Supervisor Nadia Osei finds four items associated with Registered Representative Marcus Webb: (a) a public social-media profile describing Marcus as 'Senior Vice-President & Award-Winning #1 Advisor — President's Council 2025,' although Brightlea has never appointed him to any corporate office and the President's Council recognizes the firm's top revenue producers; (b) a 'Quarterly Income Letter' that Marcus has been emailing to clients and prospects for a year, containing sector recommendations, sent without anyone's prior approval; (c) a prospect handout showing his model portfolio's annualized returns; and (d) a string of business-related text messages with clients on Marcus's personal phone, none of which appear in Brightlea's archives. Nadia must determine the rule basis for remediating each item and the records the firm must be able to produce.
questions.stem: Reviewing item (d) and the year-long pattern, Nadia drafts a findings memo. Brightlea's research Supervisor asks her to also confirm one structural point: if Marcus's unapproved Quarterly Income Letter were treated as research and Marcus held out as an analyst, who at Brightlea would be prohibited from approving such reports, and which framework requirement makes the whole episode a supervision-system failure rather than just one Representative's misconduct?

options.A: The Chief Financial Officer would be the prohibited approver; the failure is the absence of a marketing budget
options.B: Any branch manager would be prohibited; the failure is Marcus's lack of seniority
options.C: The investment banking department would be prohibited from approving the reports, and the dealer-level failure is that 3601(1) requires the dealer's policies and procedures to specifically address communication with the public and requires the dealer to monitor compliance with them — a year of unreviewed, unarchived communications shows the monitoring did not provide reasonable assurance
options.D: External auditors would be prohibited; the failure is the lack of an annual shareholder report

questions.rationale: On the structural point, 3621(2)(i) requires policies prohibiting the approval of research reports by the investment banking department — that is the prohibited approver, with approval belonging to the Supervisors designated under 3607(2). On the system point, 3601(1) requires the dealer's policies and procedures to specifically address communication with the public and requires the dealer to monitor compliance with these policies and procedures to provide reasonable assurance that the dealer, its employees and Approved Persons comply; a year of unapproved letters and off-channel, unarchived texts (which also defeat the 3602(6) retention obligation) is evidence the monitoring failed at the dealer level, not merely that one Representative misbehaved. Options A, B and D name approvers and failures (CFO, branch managers, auditors; budgets, seniority, shareholder reports) that have no anchor in Rule 3600.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must monitor compliance with these policies and procedures to provide reasonable assurance the Dealer Member, its employees and Approved Persons comply with the policies and procedures." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5840-5842

questions.stem: A trader at Lakeview Securities, a CIRO Participant, enters an order on a marketplace on behalf of Birchwood Capital, another Participant that does not have its own access to that marketplace. Under UMIR definitions, the order Lakeview enters is best described as a:

options.A: Bundled order, because it combines the interests of two dealers
options.B: Jitney order, because it is entered by a Participant acting for or on behalf of another Participant
options.C: Bypass order, because it bypasses Birchwood's own systems
options.D: Multiple client order, because more than one firm is involved

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a jitney order as an order entered on a marketplace by a Participant acting for or on behalf of another Participant — exactly this fact pattern. A bundled order (A) combines a client order with a non-client or principal order, not two dealers' access arrangements. A bypass order (C) relates to designated trades executing against disclosed volume. A multiple client order (D) combines orders from more than one client, not orders routed for another Participant.
questions.source_quote: “jitney order” means an order entered on a marketplace by a Participant acting for or on behalf of another Participant.

questions.stem: A retail client of Dorset Investments instructs her advisor to sell 500 shares but only if she can receive at least $14.25 per share. Under UMIR definitions, this instruction creates a:

options.A: Market order, since the client wants the sale executed
options.B: Special Terms Order, since a condition is attached
options.C: Limit order, since the sale is to be executed at a specified minimum price
options.D: Closing Price Order, since the client has set the price

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a limit order to include an order to sell a security to be executed at a specified minimum price — the $14.25 floor. A market order (A) executes upon entry at the best bid with no price condition. A price condition does not make an order a Special Terms Order (B); the Special Terms Order definition expressly excludes conditions as to price. A Closing Price Order (D) trades at the marketplace's closing sale price, not at a client-specified minimum.
questions.source_quote: “limit order” means an order to: (a)     buy a security or derivative to be executed at a specified maximum price; or (b)     sell a security or derivative to be executed at a specified minimum price.

questions.stem: A pension fund client of Trillium Securities wants its purchase of an illiquid listed security to execute at the closing price established for the day, with the order entered during the trading day for the purpose of calculating and executing at that closing price. Under UMIR definitions this is a:

options.A: Closing Price Order, because it references the close
options.B: Volume-Weighted Average Price Order
options.C: Market-on-Close Order
options.D: Last-sale-price order

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a Market-on-Close Order as an order entered on a marketplace on a trading day for the purpose of calculating and executing at the closing price of the security on that marketplace on that trading day — it participates in setting the close. A Closing Price Order (A) is the closest distractor, but it trades at the already-established closing price and is executed subsequent to the establishment of the closing price; it does not participate in calculating it. A VWAP order (B) executes at an average price over the day. "Last sale price" (D) is a defined price concept in UMIR 1.1, not an order type entered to set the close.
questions.source_quote: “Market-on-Close Order” means an order for the purchase or sale of a security entered on a marketplace on a trading day for the purpose of calculating and executing at the closing price of the security on that marketplace on that trading day.

questions.stem: An institutional client asks Northgate Securities to work an order over the day so it executes at an average price of the security traded that day on a known combination of marketplaces. Under UMIR definitions, the order entered for this purpose is a:

options.A: Basis Order
options.B: Call Market Order
options.C: Special Terms Order
options.D: Volume-Weighted Average Price Order

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a Volume-Weighted Average Price Order as an order entered on a marketplace on a trading day for the purpose of executing trades at an average price of the security traded on that trading day. A Basis Order (A) prices off executions in a related derivative or Exempt Exchange-traded Fund, not an average of the security's own trades. A Call Market Order (B) trades at particular times established by the marketplace. A Special Terms Order (C) is defined by odd-lot size, non-price conditions or unusual settlement, and its definition expressly excludes a VWAP order.
questions.source_quote: “Volume-Weighted Average Price Order” means an order for the purchase or sale of a security entered on a marketplace on a trading day for the purpose of executing trades at an average price of the security traded on that trading day on that marketplace

questions.stem: The arbitrage desk at Helix Capital wants to enter a Basis Order to buy a basket of listed securities priced off the day's executions in an index derivative. Before entering the order, what does the UMIR definition of "Basis Order" require the Participant to do?

options.A: Obtain the written consent of every client holding the component securities
options.B: Report to a Market Regulator its intention to enter the order and the details of the related transactions, in the form and manner required by the Market Regulator
options.C: Display the full size of the order in a consolidated market display
options.D: Confirm the order qualifies as a Program Trade with the Exchange

questions.rationale: The UMIR 1.1 definition of Basis Order applies only provided that prior to the entry of the order the Participant or Access Person reports to a Market Regulator its intention to enter the order and the details of the related transactions. Client consent (A) is not an element of the definition. Display (C) is not required — a Basis Order is among the exclusions from the Dark Order definition precisely because of its special pricing. A Program Trade (D) is a separate defined term and not a precondition.
questions.source_quote: provided that prior to the entry of the order the Participant or Access Person reports to a Market Regulator its intention to enter the order and the details of the related transactions, in the form and manner required by the Market Regulator.

questions.stem: Under the UMIR 1.1 definition, an "Opening Order" ceases to be an Opening Order if:

options.A: It is for more than 50 standard trading units
options.B: The order does not trade at the opening of trading of that security on that marketplace on that trading day
options.C: It is entered by an institutional client
options.D: The security also trades on another marketplace

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines an Opening Order as one entered prior to the opening for the purpose of calculating and executing at the opening price, provided an order shall cease to be an Opening Order if the order does not trade at the opening of trading of that security on that marketplace on that trading day. Order size (A), client type (C) and multi-marketplace listing (D) have no bearing on the definition's sunset condition.
questions.source_quote: provided an order shall cease to be an Opening Order if the order does not trade at the opening of trading of that security on that marketplace on that trading day.

questions.stem: A retail client of Pembroke Securities tells his Registered Representative: "You can always trade ahead of me — consider this permanent permission for all my future orders." Under Policy 5.3, how should the firm treat this consent?

options.A: It is invalid — consent must be specific to a particular order and noted on the order ticket; a client cannot give a blanket consent for future orders
options.B: It is valid if confirmed in writing and renewed annually
options.C: It is valid for orders of 50 standard trading units or less only
options.D: It is valid provided the head of trading countersigns it

questions.rationale: Policy 5.3 Part 4 requires that the consent of the client must be specific to a particular order with details noted on the order ticket, and states that a client cannot give a blanket form of consent to permit the Participant to trade alongside or ahead of any future orders. Annual written renewal (B) does not cure a blanket consent. The 50-standard-trading-unit threshold (C) belongs to Rule 8.1 client-principal trading, not client priority consent. Head-of-trading countersignature (D) has no role in the client priority consent regime.
questions.source_quote: A client cannot give a blanket form of consent to permit the Participant to trade alongside or ahead of any future orders the client may give the Participant.

questions.stem: After a trade executes on a marketplace, the buying and selling Participants discover the settlement date was entered incorrectly and agree to correct it before settlement. Under UMIR 7.11, the variation of the trade may be made by the marketplace at the request of a party:

options.A: With the consent of each Participant and Access Person that is a party to the trade, and with notice to the Market Regulator immediately following the variation
options.B: Without any notice, since both parties agree
options.C: Only if the trade is first cancelled and re-executed at the original price
options.D: Only with the prior written approval of the clearing agency's board

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.11 prohibits post-execution cancellation, variation or correction as to price, volume or settlement date except, among other routes, with notice to the Market Regulator immediately following the variation where, prior to settlement, the marketplace acts at the request of a party with the consent of each Participant and Access Person that is a party. Mutual agreement alone without notice (B) does not satisfy the rule. Cancel-and-re-execute (C) is not the prescribed mechanism. Clearing agency board approval (D) is not required; the clearing agency is simply an alternative channel for pre-settlement changes.
questions.source_quote: the marketplace on which the trade was executed at the request of a party to the trade and with the consent of each Participant and Access Person that is a party to the trade, or

questions.stem: Under UMIR 4.1, a Participant with knowledge of a client order that on entry could reasonably be expected to affect the market price of a security is prohibited, prior to entry of that client order, from doing all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: Entering a principal order for the security on a foreign organized regulated market
options.B: Soliciting an order from another person for a related derivative
options.C: Informing another person of the client order other than in the necessary course of business
options.D: Entering an order for an arbitrage account

questions.rationale: UMIR 4.1(2)(f) provides that a Participant does not contravene the frontrunning prohibition if the order is entered for an arbitrage account. Each of the other choices is squarely caught: 4.1(1)(a) covers principal or non-client orders on a marketplace, foreign organized regulated market or other market; 4.1(1)(b) covers soliciting orders for the security or any related derivative; and 4.1(1)(c) covers informing any other person other than in the necessary course of business.
questions.source_quote: (f)     the order is entered for an arbitrage account.

questions.stem: A client of Penrose Securities mentions to her advisor that she is thinking about accumulating a large position in a particular small-cap security over the coming weeks but has not yet placed an order. The firm's proprietary desk then begins a pattern of trading in that security. Under UMIR 2.1(2), the proprietary trading is:

options.A: Permitted because no client order yet exists
options.B: Prohibited — a Participant shall not make a pattern of trading in a particular security with knowledge of an expression of interest by a client in that security
options.C: Permitted if each individual trade is below 50 standard trading units
options.D: Prohibited only if the security is on the firm's restricted list

questions.rationale: UMIR 2.1(2)(b) provides that a Participant shall not make a pattern of trading in a particular security with knowledge of an expression of interest by a client in that particular security — the prohibition does not require an entered or even a placed client order, so (A) fails even though Rule 4.1 frontrunning would. Trade size (C) is irrelevant to the prohibition. Restricted-list status (D) engages Rule 7.7, a separate regime.
questions.source_quote: make a pattern of trading in a particular security with knowledge of an expression of interest by a client in that particular security; or

questions.stem: Under IDPC Rule 5113, if the total loan value in a client margin account results in a net negative loan value, what is required for the account to be in good standing?

options.A: The cash balance in the margin account must be a credit equal to or larger than the net negative loan value
options.B: The client must convert the account to a cash account
options.C: The Dealer Member must liquidate all short positions within one business day
options.D: The account must hold only securities with a margin rate of 50% or less

questions.rationale: IDPC 5113(4)(iii) provides that where the total loan value is a net negative loan value, the cash balance in the margin account must be a credit equal to or larger than the net negative loan value for the account to be in good standing. Conversion to a cash account (B) is not the prescribed remedy. Forced liquidation within one day (C) is not mandated by 5113 — if the client fails to deposit required margin, subsection 5111(2) requires the Dealer Member to provide margin against the shortfall. A cap on margin rates of holdings (D) is not part of the good-standing test.
questions.source_quote: If the total loan value in a client account results in a net negative loan value, the cash balance in the margin account must be a credit equal to or larger than the net negative loan value for the account to be in good standing.

questions.stem: A long-time client of Brockton Securities is going on a six-month sailing trip and asks that her Registered Representative be given written authority to trade her account in her absence; the firm did not solicit the arrangement. Under IDPC Rule 3274, the discretionary account agreement must:

options.A: Be renewable annually for as long as the client remains away
options.B: Have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months and not be renewable
options.C: Have no fixed term provided it is reviewed quarterly
options.D: Have a maximum term of 24 months with one permitted renewal

questions.rationale: IDPC 3274(1) requires that a discretionary account agreement have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months and not be renewable. Annual renewability (A) directly contradicts the non-renewal requirement and is the closest distractor because many client agreements do renew annually. Quarterly review with no fixed term (C) and a 24-month term with one renewal (D) both exceed what the rule permits.
questions.source_quote: (iii)   have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months, (iv)    not be renewable, and

questions.stem: The trading desk at Selwyn Securities receives a branch order to sell a security at $3 when the stock is trading at $20. Under Policy 6.3, the desk's appropriate action before entering the order is to:

options.A: Enter the order immediately as received, since order exposure is mandatory
options.B: Convert the order to a market order to protect the client
options.C: Cancel the order without contacting the branch
options.D: Withhold entry and return the order to the branch for confirmation of its terms, since it is likely that either the price or the stock symbol is wrong

questions.rationale: Policy 6.3 Part 2 gives precisely this example: a Participant who receives an order to sell a security at $3 in a stock trading at $20 may return the order to the branch, as it is likely that either the price or the stock symbol is wrong; Rule 6.3(1)(c) permits withholding to return the order for confirmation of its terms. Immediate entry (A) ignores the available exception and risks executing an erroneous order. Converting to a market order (B) alters the client's instruction without authority. Cancelling without inquiry (C) abandons the order instead of confirming its terms.
questions.source_quote: For example, a Participant who receives an order to sell a security at $3 in a stock trading at $20 may return the order to the branch, as it is likely that either the price or the stock symbol is wrong.

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Coral Securities determines, based on market conditions, that immediately entering a retail client's order for 40 standard trading units would not be in the client's best interests and withholds it under UMIR 6.3(1)(e). If the firm later executes the client order against a principal order, the client must receive:

options.A: A better price than the price the client would have received if the client order had been executed on receipt by the Participant
options.B: The same price the client would have received on receipt
options.C: The volume-weighted average price for the day
options.D: The closing price for the day

questions.rationale: UMIR 6.3(2)(b) requires that if the Participant executes the withheld client order against a principal order or non-client order, the client must receive a better price than the price the client would have received if the order had been executed on receipt. The same price (B) is the guarantee that applies under 6.3(2)(a) when the order is not executed against principal or non-client interests — it is the closest distractor but is insufficient where the firm is on the other side. VWAP (C) and the closing price (D) are unrelated benchmarks.
questions.source_quote: if the Participant executes the client order against a principal order or non- client order, a better price than the price the client would have received if the client order had been executed on receipt by the Participant.

questions.stem: A new retail client of Tamarack Investments places an initial trade before the designated Supervisor has reviewed the account. The Supervisor subsequently declines to approve the account. Under IDPC Rule 3214, the Dealer Member must:

options.A: Reverse the initial trade and refund all commissions
options.B: Restrict the account to only liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities to the client until final approval is provided
options.C: Continue normal trading while the dispute is escalated to the Corporation
options.D: Transfer the account to another Registered Representative for fresh approval

questions.rationale: IDPC 3214(5) provides that if a designated Supervisor does not approve a new account after the initial trade, the Dealer Member must restrict the account to only liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities to the client, with restrictions remaining until the designated Supervisor has provided final approval. Reversing the trade (A) is not the prescribed remedy. Continuing normal trading (C) defeats the restriction. Moving the account to another RR (D) does not address the missing supervisory approval.
questions.source_quote: Member must restrict the account to only liquidating trades, transfers out, paying out funds or delivering securities to the client. These account restrictions must remain in place until the designated Supervisor has provided final approval of the account.

questions.stem: A client of Fairmile Securities instructs his Registered Representative to buy 1,000 shares of a specific issuer "sometime today at whatever price looks good." The account is an ordinary advisory account, not a discretionary or managed account. Under IDPC Rule 3221, the Registered Representative:

options.A: Must refuse the order entirely because any element of discretion is prohibited
options.B: May exercise full discretion because the client initiated the instruction
options.C: May exercise time and price discretion in fulfilling the Dealer Member's best execution obligation relating to a client order for a specific amount of a specific security
options.D: Must obtain a power of attorney before accepting the order

questions.rationale: IDPC 3221(1) prohibits discretionary trading, including time and price discretion, outside a discretionary or managed account — but 3221(2) provides that this does not apply to time and price discretion exercised in fulfilling the Dealer Member's best execution obligation relating to a client order for a specific amount or a specific security. Here the client fixed the amount and security. Refusing the order (A) ignores the 3221(2) carve-out. Full discretion (B) would extend beyond time and price to security and quantity, which only a discretionary or managed account permits. A power of attorney (D) is not required for time and price discretion within the carve-out.
questions.source_quote: Subsection 3221(1) does not apply to time and price discretion exercised in fulfilling the Dealer Member’s best execution obligation relating to a client order for a specific amount or a specific security.

questions.stem: Under UMIR 7.1(3), each Participant must appoint:

options.A: A Chief Risk Officer responsible for marketplace technology
options.B: An independent monitor reporting to the Market Regulator
options.C: A head of trading who shall be responsible to supervise the trading activities of the Participant in a marketplace
options.D: A trading ombudsperson to resolve client execution complaints

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.1(3) requires that each Participant appoint a head of trading who shall be responsible to supervise the trading activities of the Participant in a marketplace. A Chief Risk Officer (A) and an independent monitor (B) are not UMIR 7.1 appointments. An ombudsperson (D) addresses complaints, not the supervision of trading activities that 7.1(3) mandates.
questions.source_quote: Each Participant shall appoint a head of trading who shall be responsible to supervise the trading activities of the Participant in a marketplace.

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 2, which statement correctly describes the required separation between supervision and compliance at a Participant?

options.A: The compliance function must be conducted by persons other than those who supervise the trading activity
options.B: The head of trading must also serve as the chief compliance officer
options.C: Compliance staff must report exclusively to the head of trading
options.D: Supervision and compliance may be performed by the same person at firms with fewer than 10 traders

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 2, element 4, requires the Participant to designate individuals responsible for supervision and compliance and states that the compliance function must be conducted by persons other than those who supervise the trading activity. Combining the head of trading and CCO roles (B) or having compliance report exclusively to the head of trading (C) would defeat that separation. There is no small-firm exemption (D) in the Policy from this requirement.
questions.source_quote: Designate individuals responsible for supervision and compliance. The compliance function must be conducted by persons other than those who supervise the trading activity.

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 2, how must a Participant document its trading supervision system?

options.A: In quarterly memoranda from the compliance department to the Market Regulator
options.B: By preparing a written policies and procedures manual that must be accessible to all relevant employees and kept current
options.C: Only through the written agreement with any third-party risk-control provider
options.D: In the audited annual financial statements

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 2, element 2, requires the Participant to document the supervision system by preparing a written policies and procedures manual which must be accessible to all relevant employees and kept current (with an historical copy advised). Quarterly memoranda to the regulator (A) are not the documentation mechanism. Third-party agreements (C) cover the outsourcing of specific risk controls under Rule 7.1(7)-(8), not the supervision system itself. Financial statements (D) have nothing to do with the supervision manual.
questions.source_quote: Document the supervision system by preparing a written policies and procedures manual. The manual must be accessible to all relevant employees. The manual must be kept current and Participants are advised to maintain an historical copy.

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 2, how often must a Participant review its trading supervision system to ensure it continues to be reasonably designed to prevent and detect violations of Requirements?

options.A: At least monthly
options.B: At least quarterly
options.C: At least annually, with more frequent reviews possibly required if past reviews have detected problems with supervision and compliance
options.D: Only when the Market Regulator requests a review

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 2, element 7, requires review of the supervision system at least annually and notes that more frequent reviews may be required if past reviews have detected problems with supervision and compliance. Monthly (A) and quarterly (B) are review frequencies that apply to specific compliance tests (e.g., trade-through testing monthly; manipulative and deceptive sampling quarterly), not the system review. Waiting for a regulator request (D) ignores the affirmative annual obligation.
questions.source_quote: Review the supervision system at least annually to ensure it continues to be reasonably designed to prevent and detect violations of Requirements. More frequent reviews may be required if past reviews have detected problems with supervision and compliance.

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 2, for how long must a Participant maintain the results of all compliance reviews of its trading supervision system?

options.A: At least two years
options.B: At least five years
options.C: At least seven years
options.D: At least ten years

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 2, element 9, requires the Participant to maintain results of all reviews for at least five years. Seven years (C) is the closest distractor because it is the UMIR retention period for gatekeeper records under Rule 10.16(5) and for client withholding instructions under Policy 5.3 and Policy 6.3 — but the compliance review results requirement in Policy 7.1 is five years. Two years (A) is only the readily-accessible window for certain client consents, and ten years (D) appears nowhere in these provisions.
questions.source_quote: Maintain results of all reviews for at least five years.

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 2, what must a Participant report to its board of directors regarding trading supervision, and how often?

options.A: Every gatekeeper report filed with the Market Regulator, monthly
options.B: Each trader's order error rate, quarterly
options.C: Detailed trade blotters, daily
options.D: A summary of the compliance reviews conducted and the results of the supervision system review, at least annually — with the board advised immediately if significant issues are identified

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 2, element 10, requires reporting to the board of directors (or partners) a summary of the compliance reviews conducted and the results of the supervision system review at least annually, and requires the board to be advised immediately if the Market Regulator or the Participant identifies significant issues concerning the supervision system or compliance procedures. Individual gatekeeper reports (A), trader error rates (B) and daily blotters (C) are not the prescribed board reporting content or cadence.
questions.source_quote: Report to the board of directors of the Participant or, if applicable, the partners, a summary of the compliance reviews conducted and the results of the supervision system review. These reports must be made at least annually.

questions.stem: A desk Supervisor at Otterburn Securities skips several monthly reviews prescribed by the firm's trading supervision procedures; no trading violations occur during the period. Under Policy 7.1 Part 1, the Supervisor:

options.A: Has not breached any obligation because no underlying trading violation occurred
options.B: Has breached Rule 10.16 by failing to file a gatekeeper report
options.C: Will have failed to comply with their supervisory obligations under Rule 7.1(4), because a supervisor who has not followed the supervision procedures adopted by the Participant fails their supervisory obligations
options.D: Is excused if the compliance department also reviews the same trading

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 1 states that if a supervisor has not followed the supervision procedures adopted by the Participant, the supervisor will have failed to comply with their supervisory obligations under Rule 7.1(4) — the failure is independent of whether a trading violation occurred, so (A) is wrong. Rule 10.16 (B) is triggered by awareness of potentially violative account activity, which is not this fact pattern. Compliance department monitoring (D) is a separate detection function; it does not substitute for the supervisor following the firm's procedures.
questions.source_quote: If a supervisor has not followed the supervision procedures adopted by the Participant, the supervisor will have failed to comply with their supervisory obligations under Rule 7.1(4).

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 1, the Participant's obligation to supervise trading applies to an order entered on a marketplace:

options.A: Only when entered by a trader employed by the Participant
options.B: Only when the order exceeds 50 standard trading units
options.C: Whether entered by a trader employed by the Participant, by an employee through an order routing system, directly by a client and routed through the Participant's trading system, or by any other means
options.D: Only when the order is for a retail client account

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 1 states the obligation to supervise applies whether the order is entered by a trader employed by the Participant, by an employee through an order routing system, directly by a client and routed to a marketplace through the trading system of the Participant, or by any other means — and that in performing trading supervision the Participant acts as a gatekeeper. Limiting supervision to staff-entered orders (A), large orders (B) or retail orders (D) all contradict the Policy's comprehensive scope.
questions.source_quote: directly by a client and routed to a marketplace through the trading system of the Participant, or •    by any other means.

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 3, a Participant's supervision system must at a minimum include the regular review of compliance with a list of marketplace trading provisions. Which of the following correctly pairs a provision with the rule the Policy cites for it?

options.A: Client Priority — Rule 8.1
options.B: Frontrunning — Rule 4.1
options.C: Order Exposure requirements — Rule 10.14
options.D: Audit Trail requirements — Rule 6.3

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 3 lists Frontrunning (Rule 4.1). The other pairings are scrambled: Client Priority is Rule 5.3, while Rule 8.1 is Client/Principal Trading (A); Order Exposure requirements are Rule 6.3, while Rule 10.14 is time synchronization (C); and Audit Trail requirements are Rule 10.11, not Rule 6.3 (D).
questions.source_quote: Frontrunning (Rule 4.1) •    Client/Principal Trading (Rule 8.1) •    Client Priority (Rule 5.3)

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 12, how often must a Participant review trading in securities on its Grey or Watch list and its Restricted list?

options.A: On a daily basis
options.B: On a weekly basis
options.C: On a quarterly basis
options.D: On an annual basis

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 12 requires the Participant to maintain a supervision system to review securities about which it may have non-public information (Grey or Watch list) and securities subject to trading restrictions (Restricted list), and states that each Participant must review the trading described above on a daily basis. Quarterly (C) applies to manipulative-and-deceptive sampling, audit trail, trade confirmation and order-handling reviews — the heightened sensitivity of inside-information lists demands daily review. Weekly (B) and annual (D) frequencies are not prescribed for these lists.
questions.source_quote: Each Participant must review the trading described above on a daily basis.

questions.stem: Under Policy 7.1 Part 10, the minimum policies and procedures regarding audit trail requirements must ensure the accurate recording of certain information for each order and trade. Which item is on that minimum list?

options.A: The client's investment objectives and risk profile
options.B: The Registered Representative's commission split
options.C: The issuer's most recent financial statements
options.D: Date and time of entry, amendment, cancellation, execution and expiration

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 10 requires accurate recording of, among other items, the date and time of entry, amendment, cancellation, execution and expiration, the quantity, buy/sell/short-sale marker, market or limit order marker, price (if limit order), security name or symbol, identity of order recipient or trader, client name or account number and special instructions, client consent, and applicable Rule 6.2 designations. Investment objectives (A) belong to KYC records, commission splits (B) to compensation records, and issuer financials (C) to research files — none is an audit trail element.
questions.source_quote: date and time of entry, amendment, cancellation, execution and expiration

questions.stem: Under UMIR 10.14, each Participant must synchronize the clocks it uses for recording the time and date of any event that must be recorded pursuant to UMIR to:

options.A: The clock of the Toronto Stock Exchange only
options.B: Coordinated Universal Time as published by the National Research Council
options.C: The clock used by the Market Regulator for this purpose
options.D: The clock of the clearing agency that settles its trades

questions.rationale: UMIR 10.14 requires each marketplace and each Participant to synchronize the clocks used for recording the time and date of any event that must be recorded pursuant to UMIR to the clock used by the Market Regulator for this purpose. The TSX clock (A), a national time standard (B) and the clearing agency's clock (D) are plausible-sounding references, but the rule fixes the benchmark as the Market Regulator's clock.
questions.source_quote: Each marketplace and each Participant shall synchronize the clocks used for recording the time and date of any event that must be recorded pursuant to UMIR to the clock used by the Market Regulator for this purpose.
